decision.title: Government Approves Labour Law Amendments Introducing Paternity Leave, Wage Transparency, and Gender Pay Gap Reporting
decision.summary: At its 114th session on February 19, the Montenegrin Government approved amendments to the Labour Law introducing 10 days of paternity leave for fathers, mandatory wage transparency in job postings (employers can no longer ask about previous salary), gender pay gap reporting for companies with 100+ employees, and purchasing power as a criterion for minimum wage. Differences exceeding 5% without objective justification require corrective measures. Reported by Portal Analitika, CdM, Adria TV, and MINA.
decision.full_text: The Montenegrin Government approved amendments to the Labour Law introducing several worker protections. Fathers now receive 10 working days off following a child's birth. The law mandates at least two months of parental leave that cannot be transferred between parents, encouraging fathers to use this right. Parents and caregivers can request flexible hours, reduced workdays, or remote work. Work outside employer premises is voluntary and employees cannot face consequences for declining it. Job candidates must receive salary information before accepting positions. Employers cannot ask candidates about previous compensation. Workers gain access to average wages for equivalent roles and protection against retaliation for discussing pay. Companies exceeding 100 employees must report wage differences between genders. If differences exceed five percent without objective justification, corrective measures become mandatory. The law introduces purchasing power as an additional criterion for determining minimum wage, reviewed every two years. These reforms align with EU legal standards under Chapter 19 and international conventions Montenegro has ratified.
decision.title_mne: Vlada odobrila izmjene Zakona o radu kojima se uvode očinsko odsustvo, transparentnost zarada i izvještavanje o rodnom jazu u platama
decision.summary_mne: Na svojoj 114. sjednici, održanoj 19. februara, Vlada Crne Gore usvojila je izmjene Zakona o radu kojima se uvodi 10 dana očinskog odsustva za očeve, obavezna transparentnost zarada u oglasima za posao (poslodavci više ne smiju pitati o prethodnoj plati), izvještavanje o rodnom jazu u platama za kompanije sa 100 i više zaposlenih, te kupovna moć kao kriterijum za određivanje minimalne zarade. Razlike koje prelaze 5% bez objektivnog opravdanja zahtijevaju korektivne mjere. Izvještavaju Portal Analitika, CdM, Adria TV i MINA.

assessments.summary: The Labour Law amendments represent a fiscally moderate reform package with significant strategic value for Montenegro's EU accession under Chapter 19. The direct budgetary impact is limited -- paternity leave costs fall primarily on employers and social insurance, while gender pay gap reporting affects roughly 457 companies with 100+ employees. The most consequential fiscal element is linking minimum wage to purchasing power, which could create upward pressure on public-sector wage bills and social transfers. However, the reforms strengthen Montenegro's position in EU accession negotiations, which unlocks access to pre-accession funds and investor confidence worth far more than the compliance costs. The package is a net positive for public finances when viewed through the lens of EU convergence, but requires careful implementation to avoid unintended fiscal drag from the minimum wage mechanism.
assessments.executive_summary: The Ministry of Finance assesses the Labour Law amendments as positive, scoring 7 out of 10. While the reforms carry manageable direct fiscal costs, the key concern is the purchasing power-linked minimum wage mechanism which could drive public expenditure growth beyond projections in a budget already at a record 3.79 billion EUR. The Ministry recommends coupling the minimum wage formula with a fiscal sustainability cap and phasing in compliance timelines to protect smaller enterprises.
assessments.reasoning:
DIRECT FISCAL IMPACT ASSESSMENT

The 10-day paternity leave provision creates a modest fiscal obligation. In Montenegro's system, short-term leave compensation is typically shared between employers and social insurance funds. With approximately 7,000-7,500 births per year and assuming roughly 60-70% uptake among eligible fathers (based on regional patterns), the annual cost to the social insurance system would be in the range of 1-2 million EUR -- a negligible figure relative to the 3.79 billion EUR 2026 budget. The two-month non-transferable parental leave component is more significant but mirrors existing maternity leave funding mechanisms. The fiscal risk here is manageable, though Montenegro's social insurance fund is already under demographic pressure with an aging population.

WAGE TRANSPARENCY AND GENDER PAY GAP REPORTING

The compliance burden falls on approximately 457 companies with 100+ employees. These are overwhelmingly the largest taxpayers and employers, including state-owned enterprises. The administrative cost of gender pay gap reporting is a one-time systems setup plus recurring annual reporting, estimated at 2,000-5,000 EUR per company annually based on EU impact assessments for Directive 2023/970. This totals roughly 1-2 million EUR across the private sector -- a minimal macroeconomic impact. However, if the 5% corrective threshold triggers actual wage adjustments, the aggregate wage bill increase could be more substantial. The ILO has documented a 21.6% gender pay gap in Montenegro, meaning many firms will need to make corrections. These wage increases would generate additional income tax and social contribution revenue, partially offsetting costs. From a pure revenue standpoint, closing the gender pay gap actually expands the tax base.

THE MINIMUM WAGE MECHANISM -- THE KEY FISCAL VARIABLE

This is the most consequential fiscal element. Montenegro's minimum wage has been stagnant at 670 EUR since mid-2024, while inflation ran at approximately 4.3% in 2025. Linking the minimum wage to purchasing power creates an automatic upward adjustment mechanism. This has three fiscal implications: (1) Direct public sector impact -- approximately 20-25% of Montenegro's workforce is in the public sector, and minimum wage increases cascade through the entire wage grid, creating multiplier effects on the public wage bill. (2) Social transfer indexation -- several social benefits are linked to the minimum wage, so increases automatically raise transfer spending. (3) Revenue upside -- higher wages generate more income tax and social contribution revenue. The two-year review cycle provides some predictability for budget planning, but the lack of a defined formula or fiscal sustainability cap is concerning. Montenegro's budget is already at record levels, and the IMF has flagged wage bill pressures as a key fiscal risk. Without guardrails, this mechanism could add 50-100 million EUR annually to public expenditure in a worst-case scenario.

EU ACCESSION VALUE

This is where the fiscal calculus becomes clearly positive. The amendments directly address closing benchmarks for Chapter 19, which is one of the remaining hurdles for Montenegro's EU accession. Progress on accession has tangible fiscal benefits: continued access to IPA III pre-accession funds (Montenegro's allocation is approximately 100+ million EUR for 2021-2027), improved sovereign credit ratings reducing borrowing costs on the 60% debt-to-GDP stock, and enhanced foreign direct investment flows. Montenegro issued an 850 million EUR Eurobond at 4.875% in March 2025; even a 25 basis point improvement in borrowing costs from EU accession progress saves over 2 million EUR annually on that single issuance. The strategic fiscal return of these reforms far exceeds their compliance costs.

IMPACT ON BUSINESS ENVIRONMENT AND INVESTMENT

Montenegro competes with Serbia, Albania, and North Macedonia for foreign investment. The wage transparency provisions could be perceived as additional regulatory burden, but they also signal alignment with EU standards that international investors expect. The flexible work provisions (remote work, reduced hours for parents) align with modern workforce expectations and could improve Montenegro's attractiveness to tech companies and digital nomads, supporting the growing services sector. The prohibition on asking about previous salaries may actually benefit employers by reducing wage inflation spirals in tight labor markets.

FISCAL RISKS AND MISSING ELEMENTS

The amendments lack a fiscal impact assessment, which is standard practice for EU member states when transposing directives. There is no clear funding mechanism identified for the expanded parental leave provisions. The purchasing power criterion for minimum wage lacks a defined methodology -- will it use HICP, national CPI, or a consumption basket? This ambiguity creates fiscal planning uncertainty. Furthermore, labour inspection capacity in Montenegro remains weak according to the European Commission's 2024 progress report, meaning enforcement costs for these new provisions are not budgeted.
key_concerns: The purchasing power-linked minimum wage lacks a defined calculation methodology and fiscal sustainability cap, creating unpredictable budgetary exposure through public sector wage bill cascading and indexed social transfers
No fiscal impact assessment accompanies the amendments, contrary to EU best practice for legislative proposals with budgetary implications -- the Ministry has not been presented with cost projections

Labour inspection capacity remains inadequate per the European Commission's assessment, and no additional budget allocation has been proposed for enforcement of the new wage transparency and reporting obligations

The 5% gender pay gap corrective threshold, combined with Montenegro's documented 21.6% gap (ILO data), could trigger significant aggregate wage adjustments with second-round effects on employer social contribution obligations and competitiveness

Expanded parental leave entitlements (paternity leave plus non-transferable two-month leave) lack a clearly identified funding source within the social insurance system, which already faces demographic pressure
recommendations: Mandate a comprehensive fiscal impact assessment before parliamentary adoption, quantifying costs across the social insurance fund, public sector wage bill, and enforcement budget over a 5-year horizon

Define the minimum wage purchasing power methodology by statutory instrument -- specify the price index (HICP or national CPI), the reference basket, and include a fiscal sustainability clause capping annual increases at nominal GDP growth plus a defined margin

Allocate dedicated budget for strengthening labour inspection capacity (estimated 1-2 million EUR annually for additional inspectors and IT systems) to ensure the new reporting obligations are enforceable

Phase in the gender pay gap reporting requirement: companies with 250+ employees from 2027, 100+ employees from 2028, aligning with the EU Pay Transparency Directive's own phased approach and giving firms time to build compliance infrastructure

Establish a ring-fenced funding mechanism within the social insurance system for expanded parental leave, potentially through a modest employer contribution adjustment of 0.1-0.2 percentage points, rather than drawing from general budget revenues
Include a sunset review clause requiring the Government to assess the fiscal and economic impact of these provisions within 3 years of implementation and report to Parliament

counter_proposal.title: Fiscally Anchored Labour Reform Package
counter_proposal.summary: The Ministry of Finance would retain all core provisions of the amendments but add fiscal safeguards: a defined minimum wage formula with a GDP-growth cap, phased compliance timelines aligned with the EU Directive's own schedule, a dedicated social insurance funding stream for parental leave, and a mandatory fiscal impact assessment requirement for all labour legislation with budgetary implications. This approach achieves the same EU accession objectives while protecting fiscal sustainability.
key_changes: Replace the vague purchasing power criterion with a transparent formula: minimum wage = f(median wage, CPI, GDP per capita growth), capped at nominal GDP growth rate plus 1 percentage point per review cycle
Phase gender pay gap reporting: 250+ employees from January 2027, 100+ employees from January 2028, with free government-provided reporting templates and guidance
Introduce a 0.15 percentage point parental leave contribution surcharge on employers, ring-fenced in the social insurance fund, to fully finance expanded paternity and parental leave entitlements
Require a fiscal impact assessment signed off by the Ministry of Finance for all labour law amendments before Cabinet approval
Establish a Labour Reform Monitoring Unit (2-3 staff) within the Ministry of Finance to track fiscal impacts of labour legislation in real time
expected_benefits: Predictable minimum wage trajectory enabling accurate multi-year budget planning, preventing the wage-bill overruns flagged by the IMF
Reduced compliance shock for businesses through phased implementation, improving business climate rankings
Fiscally neutral parental leave expansion through dedicated contribution stream rather than general budget pressure
Institutional capacity to assess fiscal implications of social legislation before adoption, preventing unfunded mandates
Full alignment with EU acquis under Chapter 19 while maintaining fiscal discipline consistent with Maastricht-adjacent targets
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High. All proposed modifications are technical additions to existing provisions rather than fundamental changes to the reform's direction. The phased timeline aligns with EU precedent. The contribution surcharge of 0.15 percentage points is minimal and comparable to similar mechanisms in Slovenia and Croatia. The fiscal impact assessment requirement can be implemented by Government decree without additional legislation. Total additional implementation cost estimated at 300,000-500,000 EUR annually for the monitoring unit and reporting infrastructure.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Paket reformi tržišta rada s fiskalnim sidrištem
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Ministarstvo finansija zadržalo bi sve ključne odredbe amandmana, ali bi dodalo fiskalne zaštitne mehanizme: definisanu formulu minimalne zarade s gornjom granicom vezanom za rast BDP-a, fazne rokove usklađivanja usaglašene s rasporedom EU direktive, namjenski tok finansiranja socijalnog osiguranja za roditeljsko odsustvo i obavezan zahtjev za procjenu fiskalnog uticaja za sve radne zakone s budžetskim implikacijama. Ovim pristupom ostvaruju se isti ciljevi pristupanja EU uz zaštitu fiskalne održivosti.
key_changes_mne: Zamijeniti nejasni kriterijum kupovne moći transparentnom formulom: minimalna zarada = f(medijalna zarada, CPI, rast BDP-a po glavi stanovnika), ograničena na stopu nominalnog rasta BDP-a plus 1 procentni poen po ciklusu pregleda

Fazno uvođenje izvještavanja o rodnom jazu u platama: preduzeća s 250+ zaposlenih od januara 2027., preduzeća s 100+ zaposlenih od januara 2028., uz besplatne šablone i smjernice za izvještavanje koje obezbjeđuje država

Uvesti dodatni doprinos za roditeljsko odsustvo od 0,15 procentnih poena na teret poslodavaca, izdvojen u fondu socijalnog osiguranja, radi potpunog finansiranja proširenih prava na očinsko i roditeljsko odsustvo
Obavezati Ministarstvo finansija da potpiše procjenu fiskalnog uticaja za sve izmjene radnog zakonodavstva prije usvajanja na Vladi
Uspostaviti Jedinicu za praćenje reformi tržišta rada (2–3 službenika) u okviru Ministarstva finansija radi praćenja fiskalnih uticaja radnog zakonodavstva u realnom vremenu
expected_benefits_mne: Predvidiva putanja minimalne zarade koja omogućava tačno višegodišnje budžetsko planiranje i sprječava prekoračenja mase zarada na koja je upozorio MMF
Smanjenje šoka usklađivanja za preduzeća kroz faznu primjenu, što poboljšava rangiranje poslovnog ambijenta
Fiskalno neutralno proširenje roditeljskog odsustva putem namjenskog toka doprinosa umjesto pritiska na opšti budžet
Institucionalni kapacitet za procjenu fiskalnih implikacija socijalnog zakonodavstva prije usvajanja, čime se sprječavaju nefinansirana zakonska zaduženja
Potpuna usklađenost s EU acquis u okviru Poglavlja 19 uz održavanje fiskalne discipline u skladu s ciljevima bliskim mastrihtskim kriterijumima
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka. Sve predložene izmjene predstavljaju tehničke dopune postojećim odredbama, a ne suštinske promjene smjera reforme. Fazni vremenski okvir usaglašen je s EU praksom. Dodatni doprinos od 0,15 procentnih poena je minimalan i uporediv sa sličnim mehanizmima u Sloveniji i Hrvatskoj. Zahtjev za procjenu fiskalnog uticaja može se uvesti uredbom Vlade bez dodatnog zakonodavstva. Ukupni dodatni troškovi implementacije procjenjuju se na 300.000–500.000 EUR godišnje za jedinicu za praćenje i infrastrukturu izvještavanja.

assessments.summary_mne: Izmjene Zakona o radu predstavljaju fiskalno umjeren reformski paket sa značajnom strateškom vrijednošću za pristupanje Crne Gore EU u okviru Poglavlja 19. Direktan uticaj na budžet je ograničen — troškovi porodiljskog odsustva za očeve padaju pretežno na teret poslodavaca i socijalnog osiguranja, dok izvještavanje o rodnom jazu u zaradama obuhvata otprilike 457 kompanija sa 100 i više zaposlenih. Najznačajniji fiskalni element je vezivanje minimalne zarade za kupovnu moć, što bi moglo stvoriti pritisak na rast rashoda za zarade u javnom sektoru i socijalna davanja. Ipak, reforme jačaju poziciju Crne Gore u pregovorima o pristupanju EU, čime se otvara pristup fondovima pretpristupne pomoći i povjerenju investitora, što vrijedi daleko više od troškova usaglašavanja. Paket predstavlja neto pozitivan ishod za javne finansije posmatrano kroz prizmu evropskih integracija, ali zahtijeva pažljivu implementaciju kako bi se izbjegao nenamjerni fiskalni teret od mehanizma minimalne zarade.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Ministarstvo finansija ocjenjuje izmjene Zakona o radu pozitivno, sa ocjenom 7 od 10. Iako reforme nose savladive direktne fiskalne troškove, ključna zabrinutost odnosi se na mehanizam minimalne zarade vezane za kupovnu moć, koji bi mogao pokrenuti rast javne potrošnje iznad projektovanih vrijednosti u budžetu koji već dostiže rekordnih 3,79 milijardi EUR. Ministarstvo preporučuje da se formula minimalne zarade upari s klauzulom fiskalne održivosti i da se rokovi za usaglašavanje uvode postepeno kako bi se zaštitila manja preduzeća.
key_concerns_mne: Minimalna zarada vezana za kupovnu moć nema definisanu metodologiju obračuna niti granicu fiskalne održivosti, što stvara nepredvidivo budžetsko opterećenje putem lančanog efekta na fond zarada u javnom sektoru i indeksirane socijalne transfere
Izmjenama nije priložena procjena fiskalnog uticaja, što je suprotno najboljoj praksi EU za zakonodavne prijedloge sa budžetskim implikacijama — Ministarstvu nisu prezentovane projekcije troškova
Kapacitet inspekcije rada ostaje nedovoljan prema ocjeni Evropske komisije, a nije predložena nikakva dodatna budžetska sredstva za sprovođenje novih obaveza transparentnosti zarada i izvještavanja

Prag za korektivne mjere u slučaju rodnog jaza od 5%, u kombinaciji sa dokumentovanim jazom od 21,6% u Crnoj Gori (podaci MOR-a), mogao bi pokrenuti značajne agregatne usklađivanja zarada sa efektima drugog kruga na obaveze poslodavaca po osnovu socijalnih doprinosa i na konkurentnost

Proširena prava na roditeljsko odsustvo (odsustvo za očeve i neprenosivi dvomjesečni odmor) nemaju jasno utvrđen izvor finansiranja unutar sistema socijalnog osiguranja, koji već trpi demografski pritisak
recommendations_mne: Obavezati izradu sveobuhvatne procjene fiskalnog uticaja prije parlamentarnog usvajanja, s kvantifikacijom troškova za fond socijalnog osiguranja, fond zarada u javnom sektoru i budžet za inspekciju u petogodišnjem horizontu

Definisati metodologiju minimalne zarade vezane za kupovnu moć podzakonskim aktom — odrediti cjenovni indeks (HICP ili nacionalni CPI), referentnu korpu i uključiti klauzulu fiskalne održivosti koja godišnje povećanje ograničava na nominalni rast BDP-a uz definisanu maržu

Obezbijediti namjenska budžetska sredstva za jačanje kapaciteta inspekcije rada (procijenjeno 1–2 miliona EUR godišnje za dodatne inspektore i IT sisteme) kako bi nove obaveze izvještavanja bile sprovodive

Uvesti postepenu primjenu obaveze izvještavanja o rodnom jazu u zaradama: za kompanije sa 250 i više zaposlenih od 2027. godine, a za kompanije sa 100 i više zaposlenih od 2028. godine, usklađujući se sa faznim pristupom EU Direktive o transparentnosti zarada i dajući preduzećima vremena da izgrade infrastrukturu za usaglašavanje

Uspostaviti poseban mehanizam finansiranja unutar sistema socijalnog osiguranja za prošireno roditeljsko odsustvo, potencijalno kroz skromno povećanje doprinosa poslodavaca od 0,1 do 0,2 procentna poena, umjesto oslanjanja na opšte budžetske prihode
Uključiti klauzulu o preispitivanju po isteku roka, kojom se Vlada obavezuje da u roku od 3 godine od primjene ocijeni fiskalni i ekonomski uticaj ovih odredbi i podnese izvještaj Parlamentu

assessments.summary: The Labour Law amendments represent a well-directed reform package that advances EU accession under Chapter 19 while addressing genuine structural problems in Montenegro's labour market, including a 21.6% gender pay gap, the absence of statutory paternity leave, and erosion of real wages through inflation. The Ministry of Economy views wage transparency, gender pay gap reporting, and the purchasing power criterion for minimum wage as economically sound measures that should improve labour market efficiency, reduce informality incentives, and strengthen Montenegro's attractiveness to quality foreign investors. However, the reform's reach is limited by the dominance of micro and small enterprises (99.3% of all businesses) that fall below the 100-employee reporting threshold, and by an informal economy estimated at 30% of GDP where these protections will have little practical effect without stronger enforcement capacity.
assessments.executive_summary: The Ministry of Economy gives a positive assessment to these Labour Law amendments, which are a necessary step for closing Chapter 19 benchmarks and address real gaps including Montenegro's 21.6% gender pay gap and the lack of statutory paternity leave. The primary concern is that gender pay gap reporting applies only to companies with 100+ employees, covering less than 1% of Montenegro's 59,000 active businesses, while the informal economy (30% of GDP) remains largely untouched. The Ministry recommends phased extension of reporting obligations to companies with 50+ employees and a parallel investment in labour inspectorate capacity to ensure these rights translate from paper into practice.
assessments.reasoning:
From the perspective of the Ministry of Economy, these amendments merit a positive but not uncritical assessment across several dimensions.

EU ACCESSION AND INVESTMENT CLIMATE: The amendments serve as a closing benchmark under Chapter 19 (Social Policy and Employment), which is strategically important for Montenegro's EU accession trajectory. Aligning with the EU Pay Transparency Directive (2023/970) and Work-Life Balance Directive signals regulatory predictability to foreign investors. Quality FDI, particularly from EU-based companies already subject to these standards, will find Montenegro's legal environment more familiar and compliant. This is a meaningful competitive advantage over regional peers that have not yet adopted similar measures.

GENDER PAY GAP AND WAGE TRANSPARENCY: Montenegro's gender pay gap stands at approximately 21.6% according to ILO data, significantly above the EU average of around 13%. The introduction of mandatory wage transparency in job postings, prohibition on salary history inquiries, and gender pay gap reporting are evidence-based interventions that have shown effectiveness in EU member states. The 5% threshold triggering corrective action mirrors the EU Directive standard. From an economic efficiency standpoint, wage transparency reduces information asymmetry in the labour market, enabling better matching of workers to jobs and reducing monopsony power that depresses wages, particularly for women. This should contribute to more efficient human capital allocation.

SCOPE LIMITATIONS AND SME IMPACT: The 100-employee threshold for gender pay gap reporting is a significant limitation. Montenegro's economy is overwhelmingly composed of small enterprises: 99.33% of the approximately 59,000 active business entities are small companies. Only around 457 companies have more than 100 employees. This means the reporting obligation will affect less than 1% of businesses. While this mirrors the EU Directive threshold and avoids imposing disproportionate administrative costs on SMEs, it also means the vast majority of the workforce will not benefit from systematic pay gap monitoring. The wage transparency provisions in job postings and the prohibition on salary history questions do apply universally, which partially offsets this gap.

INFORMAL ECONOMY CHALLENGE: Montenegro's informal economy is estimated at nearly 30% of GDP, with widespread practices of officially paying minimum wage while supplementing with unreported cash payments. This structural reality means that formal legal protections, however well-designed, will not reach a large segment of workers. The amendments do nothing to directly address informality. The purchasing power criterion for minimum wage could have a dual effect: raising the official minimum wage may reduce the gap between declared and actual wages, potentially reducing incentives for envelope payments, but it could also increase employer costs and push more activity underground if not accompanied by enforcement reforms.

PATERNITY LEAVE AND LABOUR MARKET PARTICIPATION: The introduction of 10 days of paternity leave and 2 months of non-transferable parental leave is economically significant. Research from Nordic and Western European countries demonstrates that earmarked paternity leave reduces the motherhood penalty in wages and career progression, increases female labour force participation, and contributes to long-term GDP growth. Montenegro's female labour force participation remains notably low, and these provisions could help. However, 10 days is relatively modest; the EU Work-Life Balance Directive mandates a minimum of 10 working days, so Montenegro is adopting only the floor standard. The economic impact will depend heavily on whether employers, particularly in the private sector, actually facilitate this leave or create informal pressure against taking it.

MINIMUM WAGE AND PURCHASING POWER: The introduction of purchasing power as a criterion for minimum wage determination addresses a real problem. Montenegro's minimum wage of 670 EUR net per month has remained unchanged despite inflation running at approximately 4.3%, meaning workers have experienced real wage erosion. Setting the minimum wage for two-year periods with a purchasing power adjustment is a step toward a more rational wage-setting mechanism. However, a two-year review cycle may be insufficiently responsive in periods of volatile inflation. The economic risk is that if the minimum wage rises too quickly relative to productivity gains, it could increase unemployment among low-skilled workers or push activity further into the informal sector, but given that the current minimum wage appears to be below the market-clearing wage for many occupations, moderate adjustments linked to purchasing power are unlikely to have significant negative employment effects.

ENFORCEMENT CAPACITY: The critical weakness across all these measures is enforcement. Montenegro's labour inspectorate has historically been under-resourced, and fines for non-compliance (EUR 600 to 6,000 for entrepreneurs) may be insufficient to deter violations. The administrative burden on companies already spending approximately 300 hours per year on tax compliance alone is a legitimate concern. Without investment in enforcement capacity and digital reporting infrastructure, these reforms risk remaining aspirational rather than operational.

COMPETITIVENESS CONSIDERATIONS: Some business interests may argue that additional labour regulations reduce competitiveness. The Ministry of Economy's assessment is that these specific measures, being broadly aligned with standards Montenegro's trading partners already apply, do not meaningfully reduce competitiveness. In fact, improved working conditions and wage transparency may help address Montenegro's severe brain drain problem by making domestic employment more attractive relative to emigration to EU member states. The demographic challenge of working-age population emigration is a far greater threat to economic competitiveness than the marginal administrative costs of these reforms.
key_concerns: Gender pay gap reporting covers less than 1% of Montenegro's approximately 59,000 businesses due to the 100-employee threshold, leaving the vast majority of the workforce without systematic pay gap monitoring.
The informal economy, estimated at 30% of GDP, is entirely unaddressed by these formal legal mechanisms; workers receiving envelope wages will see no practical benefit.
Montenegro's labour inspectorate lacks the capacity and resources to effectively enforce these new provisions, risking a gap between legal rights and workplace reality.
The two-year minimum wage review cycle may be too slow to respond to inflation volatility, allowing further erosion of real purchasing power between reviews.
Ten days of paternity leave meets only the EU minimum floor and may be insufficient to meaningfully shift cultural norms around caregiving in a society with traditional gender roles.
Compliance costs for the approximately 457 companies subject to gender pay gap reporting have not been publicly estimated, and no transition support or guidance has been announced.
There is no mention of social partner consultation in the design of these amendments; the Social Council's role in shaping the final provisions is unclear.
recommendations: Phase in gender pay gap reporting obligations for companies with 50+ employees within three years of initial implementation, to significantly expand coverage while allowing time for capacity building.

Allocate dedicated budget for strengthening the labour inspectorate, including hiring additional inspectors, providing training on the new provisions, and developing digital reporting tools to reduce administrative burden on companies.

Establish a publicly accessible online platform for gender pay gap reports, modeled on the UK's Gender Pay Gap Service, to create transparency-driven incentives for improvement beyond the regulatory minimum.
Introduce an automatic annual indexation mechanism for the minimum wage linked to a basket of CPI and productivity growth indicators, rather than relying on bipartisan negotiation every two years.

Design a targeted support program through the Employment Bureau to help SMEs voluntarily adopt wage transparency practices, including template salary scales and self-assessment tools, even where reporting is not mandatory.

Pair these formal labour market reforms with intensified measures against informal employment, including increased penalties for undeclared work and simplified registration procedures for micro-enterprises.

Ensure meaningful engagement of the Social Council (Socijalni savjet) in the parliamentary phase of these amendments, with employer associations and trade unions given adequate time to provide input on implementation timelines and technical standards.

Consider extending paternity leave to 15 working days within the next review cycle, as evidence from comparable economies suggests that longer earmarked leave correlates with higher uptake rates and greater shifts in household labour division.

counter_proposal.title: Comprehensive Labour Market Modernisation with Enforcement Infrastructure
counter_proposal.summary: The Ministry of Economy would retain all core provisions of the current amendments but embed them within a broader labour market modernisation framework that addresses enforcement capacity, informal economy reduction, and SME support simultaneously. Rather than introducing rights without infrastructure, this approach would pair each new obligation with the institutional capacity to enforce it and the support mechanisms for businesses to comply. The key philosophical difference is treating labour law reform not as a standalone compliance exercise for EU benchmarks, but as an integrated economic development tool.
key_changes: Lower the gender pay gap reporting threshold to 50 employees within three years, with immediate voluntary reporting incentives (tax credits or simplified compliance certification) for companies between 50-99 employees.

Establish a Labour Law Implementation Fund of at least 2 million EUR annually, financed from EU IPA funds and budget reallocation, dedicated to inspectorate hiring, training, and digital reporting infrastructure.

Replace the two-year minimum wage review cycle with an automatic annual indexation formula based on 50% CPI change plus 50% productivity growth, with a Social Council override mechanism for exceptional circumstances.

Create a free SME Compliance Toolkit developed jointly by the Ministry of Economy and the Employment Bureau, including wage-scale templates, pay gap self-assessment calculators, and model employment contracts compliant with new provisions.

Introduce a graduated enforcement approach: first-year focus on advisory visits and compliance assistance for the 457 companies subject to reporting, with full penalty enforcement beginning in year two.

Couple paternity leave introduction with an employer cost-sharing mechanism through the Health Insurance Fund to ensure that the cost of 10 days leave does not fall disproportionately on small employers who may be reluctant to hire younger men.
expected_benefits: Broader effective coverage of wage transparency norms across approximately 1,500-2,000 companies (50+ employees) rather than only 457, reaching a significantly larger share of the formal workforce.
Stronger enforcement credibility from day one, reducing the risk that the amendments become symbolic EU benchmarks without practical labour market impact.
Reduced administrative burden on SMEs through free compliance tools, turning a potential source of business frustration into an opportunity for professionalisation of HR practices.
More responsive minimum wage adjustment that protects purchasing power in real time while maintaining a link to productivity, avoiding both wage erosion and economically destabilising jumps.
Better employer buy-in for paternity leave through cost-sharing, increasing actual uptake rates and maximising the intended economic benefits of higher female labour force participation.
Stronger signal to EU evaluators that Montenegro is not merely adopting legal text but building institutional capacity, potentially accelerating Chapter 19 closure.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High feasibility for most components. The SME Compliance Toolkit and graduated enforcement approach can be implemented within existing institutional frameworks at minimal cost. The Labour Law Implementation Fund would require approximately 2 million EUR annually, which is achievable through EU IPA III allocation (Montenegro receives approximately 80-90 million EUR annually in IPA funds) combined with modest budget reallocation. Lowering the reporting threshold to 50 employees is a medium-term legislative change that requires a subsequent amendment cycle. The automatic minimum wage indexation formula requires Social Council agreement but is technically straightforward. The main political feasibility challenge is the employer cost-sharing mechanism for paternity leave, which would require negotiation with social partners and Health Insurance Fund restructuring.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Sveobuhvatna modernizacija tržišta rada s infrastrukturom za sprovođenje
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Ministarstvo ekonomije zadržalo bi sve ključne odredbe trenutnih izmjena, ali bi ih ugradilo u širi okvir modernizacije tržišta rada koji istovremeno adresira kapacitet za sprovođenje, smanjenje sive ekonomije i podršku malim i srednjim preduzećima. Umjesto uvođenja prava bez prateće infrastrukture, ovaj pristup bi svaku novu obavezu uparilo s institucionalnim kapacitetom za njeno sprovođenje i mehanizmima podrške preduzećima radi usklađivanja. Ključna filozofska razlika jeste u tome što se reforma radnog zakonodavstva ne tretira kao izolovana vježba usklađivanja s EU standardima, već kao integrisani alat ekonomskog razvoja.
key_changes_mne: Sniziti prag obaveznog izvještavanja o rodnom jazu u zaradama na 50 zaposlenih u roku od tri godine, uz neposredne podsticaje za dobrovoljno izvještavanje (poreski krediti ili pojednostavljena sertifikacija usklađenosti) za preduzeća s 50–99 zaposlenih.

Uspostaviti Fond za implementaciju radnog zakonodavstva u iznosu od najmanje 2 miliona EUR godišnje, finansiran iz EU IPA fondova i preraspodjele budžeta, namijenjen zapošljavanju i obuci inspektora te izgradnji digitalne infrastrukture za izvještavanje.

Zamijeniti dvogodišnji ciklus preispitivanja minimalne zarade automatskom godišnjom formulom indeksacije zasnovanom na 50% promjene CPI-ja i 50% rasta produktivnosti, uz mehanizam prevazilaženja od strane Socijalnog savjeta u izuzetnim okolnostima.

Izraditi besplatan komplet alata za usklađivanje za MSP, koji zajednički razvijaju Ministarstvo ekonomije i Zavod za zapošljavanje, uključujući predloške platnih skala, kalkulatore za samoprocjenu rodnog jaza i modele ugovora o radu usklađenih s novim odredbama.

Uvesti postepeni pristup sprovođenju: u prvoj godini fokus na savjetodavnim posjetama i pomoći u usklađivanju za 457 preduzeća obuhvaćenih obavezom izvještavanja, a punu primjenu sankcija od druge godine.

Uvođenje porodiljskog odsustva za očeve pratiti mehanizmom podjele troškova između poslodavaca i Fonda zdravstvenog osiguranja, kako trošak 10 dana odsustva ne bi nesrazmjerno pao na male poslodavce koji bi mogli biti nevoljni da zapošljavaju mlađe muškarce.
expected_benefits_mne: Šira efektivna primjena normi o transparentnosti zarada u oko 1.500–2.000 preduzeća (s 50 ili više zaposlenih), umjesto samo 457, čime bi se obuhvatilo znatno veći udio formalne radne snage.
Veći kredibilitet sprovođenja od prvog dana, čime se smanjuje rizik da izmjene ostanu simbolični EU standardi bez stvarnog uticaja na tržište rada.
Smanjen administrativni teret za MSP zahvaljujući besplatnim alatima za usklađivanje, pretvarajući potencijalni izvor poslovnog nezadovoljstva u priliku za profesionalizaciju HR prakse.
Fleksibilnije usklađivanje minimalne zarade koje u realnom vremenu čuva kupovnu moć, uz zadržavanje veze s produktivnošću – izbjegavajući i eroziju zarada i ekonomski destabilizujuće skokove.

Veća prihvaćenost odsustva za očeve od strane poslodavaca kroz podjelu troškova, što povećava stvarnu stopu korišćenja i maksimizira namjeravane ekonomske benefite od veće participacije žena na tržištu rada.
Snažniji signal EU evaluatorima da Crna Gora ne usvaja samo pravni tekst, već gradi institucionalne kapacitete, što potencijalno može ubrzati zatvaranje Poglavlja 19.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka izvodljivost za većinu komponenti. Komplet alata za usklađivanje za MSP i postepeni pristup sprovođenju mogu se implementirati unutar postojećih institucionalnih okvira uz minimalne troškove. Fond za implementaciju radnog zakonodavstva zahtijevao bi oko 2 miliona EUR godišnje, što je ostvarivo kroz alokaciju u okviru EU IPA III (Crna Gora godišnje prima oko 80–90 miliona EUR iz IPA fondova) u kombinaciji s umjerenom preraspodjelom budžeta. Snižavanje praga izvještavanja na 50 zaposlenih predstavlja zakonodavnu promjenu na srednji rok koja zahtijeva naredni ciklus izmjena. Automatska formula indeksacije minimalne zarade zahtijeva saglasnost Socijalnog savjeta, ali je tehnički jednostavna. Glavni izazov u pogledu političke izvodljivosti jeste mehanizam podjele troškova između poslodavaca i Fonda zdravstvenog osiguranja za odsustvo očeva, što bi zahtijevalo pregovore sa socijalnim partnerima i restrukturiranje Fonda zdravstvenog osiguranja.

assessments.summary_mne: Izmjene Zakona o radu predstavljaju dobro usmjeren reformski paket koji unapređuje pristupanje EU u okviru Poglavlja 19, istovremeno rješavajući stvarne strukturne probleme na crnogorskom tržištu rada, uključujući razliku u platama između polova od 21,6%, odsustvo zakonskog porodiljskog odsustva za očeve i eroziju realnih zarada kroz inflaciju. Ministarstvo ekonomije smatra da su transparentnost zarada, izvještavanje o razlikama u platama između polova i kriterijum kupovne moći za minimalnu zaradu ekonomski opravdane mjere koje bi trebalo da poboljšaju efikasnost tržišta rada, smanje podsticaje za rad u sivoj ekonomiji i ojačaju atraktivnost Crne Gore za kvalitetne strane investitore. Međutim, domašaj ove reforme je ograničen dominacijom mikro i malih preduzeća (99,3% svih privrednih subjekata) koja su ispod praga od 100 zaposlenih za obavezno izvještavanje, kao i neformalnom ekonomijom koja se procjenjuje na 30% BDP-a, gdje ove zaštite neće imati gotovo nikakvog praktičnog efekta bez jačanja kapaciteta za sprovođenje.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Ministarstvo ekonomije pozitivno ocjenjuje ove izmjene Zakona o radu, koje predstavljaju neophodan korak ka ispunjenju mjerila za zatvaranje Poglavlja 19 i kojima se adresiraju stvarni nedostaci, uključujući razliku u platama između polova od 21,6% i odsustvo zakonskog porodiljskog odsustva za očeve. Osnovna zabrinutost je što se obaveza izvještavanja o razlici u platama između polova odnosi samo na kompanije s 100 i više zaposlenih, pokrivajući manje od 1% od oko 59.000 aktivnih preduzeća u Crnoj Gori, dok neformalna ekonomija (30% BDP-a) ostaje uglavnom van dosega ovih mjera. Ministarstvo preporučuje postepeno proširenje obaveze izvještavanja na kompanije s 50 i više zaposlenih, uz paralelno ulaganje u kapacitete inspekcije rada, kako bi se osiguralo da ova prava pređu iz teorije u svakodnevnu praksu.
key_concerns_mne: Izvještavanje o razlici u platama između polova obuhvata manje od 1% od oko 59.000 preduzeća u Crnoj Gori, zbog praga od 100 zaposlenih, što ostavlja veliku većinu radne snage bez sistematskog praćenja razlika u platama.
Neformalna ekonomija, procijenjena na 30% BDP-a, u potpunosti je izvan dosega ovih formalnih pravnih mehanizama; radnici koji primaju zarade u koverti neće osjetiti nikakvu praktičnu korist.
Inspekcija rada u Crnoj Gori nema dovoljno kapaciteta ni resursa da efikasno sprovede ove nove odredbe, što stvara rizik od jaza između zakonskih prava i stvarnosti na radnom mjestu.
Dvogodišnji ciklus preispitivanja minimalne zarade može biti previše spor da bi odgovorio na volatilnost inflacije, što između dva preispitivanja može dovesti do dalje erozije realne kupovne moći.

Deset dana porodiljskog odsustva za očeve ispunjava samo minimalni prag EU i može biti nedovoljno da na smislen način promijeni kulturne norme u pogledu brige o djeci u društvu s tradicionalnim rodnim ulogama.

Troškovi usklađivanja za oko 457 kompanija koje podliježu obavezi izvještavanja o razlici u platama između polova nijesu javno procijenjeni, a nisu najavljene ni mjere podrške ni smjernice za prelazni period.
Ne pominje se konsultacija socijalnih partnera u procesu izrade ovih izmjena; uloga Socijalnog savjeta u oblikovanju konačnih odredbi ostaje nejasna.
recommendations_mne: Postepeno uvesti obavezu izvještavanja o razlici u platama između polova za kompanije s 50 i više zaposlenih u roku od tri godine od početka primjene, kako bi se značajno proširio obuhvat uz istovremeno ostavljanje vremena za izgradnju kapaciteta.

Obezbijediti namjenska sredstva za jačanje inspekcije rada, uključujući zapošljavanje dodatnih inspektora, obuku o novim odredbama i razvoj digitalnih alata za izvještavanje radi smanjenja administrativnog opterećenja za kompanije.

Uspostaviti javno dostupnu online platformu za izvještaje o razlici u platama između polova, po uzoru na britanski Gender Pay Gap Service, kako bi se stvorili podsticaji za napredak zasnovani na transparentnosti, koji prevazilaze regulatorni minimum.

Uvesti automatski godišnji mehanizam indeksacije minimalne zarade vezan za korpu pokazatelja koji uključuju indeks potrošačkih cijena i rast produktivnosti, umjesto oslanjanja na bipartitne pregovore svake dvije godine.

Osmisliti ciljani program podrške kroz Zavod za zapošljavanje kako bi se malim i srednjim preduzećima pomoglo da dobrovoljno usvoje prakse transparentnosti zarada, uključujući predloške platnih razreda i alate za samoprocjenu, čak i tamo gdje izvještavanje nije obavezno.

Uz ove formalne reforme tržišta rada sprovesti pojačane mjere protiv neformalnog zapošljavanja, uključujući više kazne za neprijavljeni rad i pojednostavljivanje postupka registracije za mikro-preduzeća.

Osigurati smisleno učešće Socijalnog savjeta u parlamentarnoj fazi razmatranja ovih izmjena, uz dodjelu adekvatnog vremena udruženjima poslodavaca i sindikatima da daju doprinos u pogledu rokova sprovođenja i tehničkih standarda.

Razmotriti produženje porodiljskog odsustva za očeve na 15 radnih dana u narednom ciklusu preispitivanja, budući da iskustva uporedivih ekonomija ukazuju na to da duže namjensko odsustvo korelira s većom stopom korišćenja i značajnijim promjenama u podjeli kućnih obaveza.

assessments.summary: The Labour Law amendments are a positive step for the education sector, particularly through wage transparency provisions that will expose and help correct the estimated 9-11% gender pay gap among teachers, and through parental leave reforms that will benefit the predominantly female teaching workforce. However, the decision does not directly address the chronic underpayment of education professionals relative to other public sectors, nor does it contain sector-specific provisions to mitigate brain drain among qualified teachers and academics. The 100-employee threshold for gender pay gap reporting excludes most schools individually, potentially limiting the law's impact in education unless reporting is aggregated at the institutional or ministry level.
assessments.executive_summary: The Ministry of Education views these Labour Law amendments positively, as wage transparency and parental leave reforms will particularly benefit the female-dominated teaching workforce and help close the documented 9-11% gender pay gap among educators. However, the 100-employee reporting threshold risks excluding most individual schools, and the reforms miss an opportunity to address education-sector-specific challenges such as chronically low teacher salaries and brain drain among qualified professionals.
assessments.reasoning:
The Labour Law amendments approved at the 114th Cabinet session carry meaningful implications for Montenegro's education sector, which employs a heavily feminized workforce where women constitute the vast majority of primary and secondary school teachers, consistent with OECD averages where 84% of primary and 64% of secondary teachers are women.

WAGE TRANSPARENCY AND GENDER PAY GAP: Data from multiple salary surveys indicates that male teachers in Montenegro earn approximately 9-11% more than female teachers performing equivalent work. The new requirement for employers to disclose salary ranges before hiring, the prohibition on asking candidates about previous salaries, and employees' right to access average wage data for equivalent roles will directly benefit teachers seeking equitable compensation. However, the mandatory gender pay gap reporting applies only to employers with more than 100 employees. Most individual schools in Montenegro fall well below this threshold. Unless the Ministry of Education or municipal education directorates are treated as the employer of record for reporting purposes, the education sector may paradoxically be exempted from the very transparency mechanisms that could address its documented pay disparities. This is a significant implementation gap that needs clarification.

PARENTAL LEAVE REFORMS: The introduction of 10 days of paternity leave and 60 non-transferable days for each parent is progressive. However, the Women's Rights Center (Centar za zenska prava) has raised valid concerns that the new structure effectively reduces the primary caregiver's leave from 12 to 10 months rather than adding leave. For teachers, who often time pregnancies around the academic calendar, this reduction could create staffing complications and place additional pressure on returning mothers. The education sector should have been consulted on transition arrangements, including substitute teacher availability and academic continuity planning.

FLEXIBLE WORK ARRANGEMENTS: The provisions allowing parents and caregivers to request flexible hours, reduced workdays, or remote work are welcome in principle but present implementation challenges in education. Teaching is inherently place-based work requiring physical presence in classrooms. Without sector-specific guidance, these provisions may create expectations among teaching staff that cannot be practically fulfilled, or conversely, teachers may feel excluded from benefits available to workers in other sectors. Administrative and support staff in schools could benefit, but clear guidelines are needed.

EU ACCESSION AND CHAPTER 19: These amendments serve as a closing benchmark under Chapter 19 on Social Policy and Employment. From an education perspective, Montenegro must also meet requirements under Chapter 26 on Education and Culture. The alignment with EU Directive 2023/970 on pay transparency is commendable. However, the education sector faces its own EU alignment challenges: adult learning participation stands at only 4.6% compared to the EU average of 10.8%, and teacher professional development systems need strengthening according to UNICEF assessments. The Labour Law reforms, while necessary, should not be treated as sufficient for comprehensive EU alignment in the education-adjacent social policy space.

BRAIN DRAIN IMPLICATIONS: Approximately 50% of young Montenegrins express interest in emigrating, and 28.2% of highly educated respondents actively consider living or working abroad. Teacher salaries in Montenegro, averaging around 1,960 EUR monthly, remain uncompetitive with EU member states. The purchasing power criterion for minimum wage is a welcome addition, but the minimum wage mechanism alone will not address the structural underpayment of education professionals. Wage transparency could actually accelerate brain drain if teachers gain clearer evidence of how much less they earn compared to other sectors or compared to teachers in neighboring EU countries. Without complementary measures to improve education sector compensation, transparency alone could be counterproductive for retention.

VOCATIONAL EDUCATION AND TRAINING: With 68.8% of students enrolled in VET programmes, Montenegro has a large vocational education sector. VET instructors often come from industry backgrounds where gender pay gaps may be more pronounced. The wage transparency provisions could help attract and retain qualified VET instructors by ensuring fair compensation, but only if the reporting mechanisms capture VET institutions, many of which are small in staff numbers.

The reforms are genuinely positive in their direction and reflect EU best practices. The concern from the education perspective is not with the policy intent but with the implementation architecture, which appears designed primarily with larger private sector employers in mind. The public education sector, with its unique structure of many small institutions under centralized management, requires tailored implementation guidance to realize the full benefits of these amendments.
key_concerns: The 100-employee threshold for mandatory gender pay gap reporting may exclude most individual schools in Montenegro, potentially leaving the heavily feminized education sector outside the scope of the very transparency mechanisms designed to address pay inequality.

The reduction of primary caregiver leave from 12 to 10 months, as flagged by the Women's Rights Center, could disproportionately affect female teachers who constitute the majority of the teaching workforce, creating staffing disruptions and pressure on returning educators.

Flexible work provisions (remote work, reduced hours) are difficult to implement in teaching, which requires physical classroom presence, potentially creating a two-tier system where education workers are effectively excluded from benefits available in other sectors.

Wage transparency, without complementary salary improvements for education professionals, could accelerate brain drain by making teachers more acutely aware of compensation gaps relative to other sectors and to teaching positions in EU member states.

The law lacks sector-specific implementation guidance for public education, where employment structures (many small schools under centralized management) differ fundamentally from the private sector model the law appears designed around.
recommendations: Clarify that gender pay gap reporting obligations in the education sector apply at the level of the employer of record (Ministry of Education or municipal directorates), not individual schools, to ensure the education workforce is captured by the 100-employee threshold.

Adopt the Women's Rights Center proposal to maintain 365 days of total parental leave while adding the 60 non-transferable days for the second parent, rather than redistributing existing leave, to protect both parental rights and school staffing stability.

Develop education-sector-specific implementation guidelines for flexible work provisions, identifying which roles (administrative, support, certain planning functions) can accommodate remote or flexible arrangements and which require physical presence.

Couple wage transparency reforms with a dedicated teacher salary competitiveness review benchmarked against EU and regional standards, to ensure that transparency leads to pay improvement rather than increased emigration awareness.

Ensure VET institutions and their instructors are explicitly included in wage transparency and pay gap reporting frameworks, given the sector's size (68.8% of enrolled students) and its particular vulnerability to gender-based pay disparities.

Establish a transition fund or substitute teacher pool to manage the staffing implications of expanded paternity and parental leave in schools, particularly in rural areas where teacher shortages are already acute.

counter_proposal.title: Education-Adapted Labour Standards Package
counter_proposal.summary: The Ministry of Education would retain the core principles of wage transparency and expanded parental leave while adapting implementation to the realities of the education sector. This includes aggregated employer-level reporting for schools, additive rather than redistributive parental leave, a teacher salary competitiveness benchmark, and a substitute teacher deployment system to manage leave-related staffing gaps.
key_changes: Apply the 100-employee reporting threshold at the municipal education directorate or Ministry level, ensuring all teachers are covered by gender pay gap reporting.
Extend total parental leave to 425 days (365 plus 60 non-transferable for the second parent) rather than redistributing the existing 365-day allocation.
Introduce a biennial teacher salary competitiveness index comparing Montenegrin teacher compensation with EU averages, Western Balkans peers, and domestic cross-sector equivalents.
Create a national substitute teacher registry and rapid deployment system to prevent learning disruptions when teachers take parental or paternity leave.
Define education-specific flexible work guidelines distinguishing classroom roles from administrative and planning functions.
expected_benefits: Full coverage of the education workforce under gender pay gap reporting, exposing and enabling correction of the documented 9-11% pay gap among teachers.
Protection of existing parental rights while still encouraging paternal involvement, reducing resistance to reform from the teaching workforce.
Evidence-based salary adjustments that could help retain qualified teachers and mitigate brain drain, currently affecting 28% of highly educated Montenegrins.
Uninterrupted educational continuity for students when teachers exercise their parental leave rights.
Realistic flexible work options that acknowledge the nature of teaching while providing benefits where feasible.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High feasibility. The aggregated reporting mechanism requires only administrative clarification, not new legislation. The additive parental leave model increases costs by approximately 17% over the current proposal but remains within the fiscal envelope of comparable EU candidate states. The substitute teacher registry builds on existing mechanisms and could be operational within one academic year. The salary competitiveness index requires only data collection and analysis capacity already present within MONSTAT and the Ministry.
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Ministarstvo prosvjete zadržalo bi osnovna načela transparentnosti zarada i proširenog roditeljskog odsustva, prilagođavajući primjenu stvarnostima obrazovnog sektora. To uključuje agregirano izvještavanje na nivou poslodavca za škole, aditivno umjesto redistributivnog roditeljskog odsustva, referentnu vrijednost konkurentnosti nastavničkih plata i sistem raspoređivanja zamjenskih nastavnika radi upravljanja kadrovskim nedostacima uzrokovanim odsustvima.
key_changes_mne: Primijeniti prag obaveze izvještavanja od 100 zaposlenih na nivou opštinske prosvjetne uprave ili Ministarstva, osiguravajući da svi nastavnici budu obuhvaćeni izvještavanjem o rodnom jazu u platama.
Produžiti ukupno roditeljsko odsustvo na 425 dana (365 plus 60 neprenosivih dana za drugog roditelja), umjesto preraspodjele postojeće kvote od 365 dana.
Uvesti dvogodišnji indeks konkurentnosti nastavničkih plata koji poredi naknade crnogorskih nastavnika sa prosjekom EU, zemljama Zapadnog Balkana i domaćim međusektorskim ekvivalentima.
Uspostaviti nacionalni registar zamjenskih nastavnika i sistem brzog raspoređivanja kako bi se spriječili poremećaji u nastavi kada nastavnici koriste roditeljsko ili očinsko odsustvo.
Definisati smjernice za fleksibilno radno angažovanje specifične za obrazovanje, praveći razliku između nastavne uloge i administrativnih i planskih funkcija.
expected_benefits_mne: Potpuna obuhvaćenost obrazovnih radnika izvještavanjem o rodnom jazu u platama, čime se otkriva i omogućava korekcija dokumentovanog jaza od 9–11% u platama nastavnika.
Zaštita postojećih roditeljskih prava uz istovremeno podsticanje uključenosti očeva, čime se smanjuje otpor prema reformi od strane nastavničkog kadra.
Prilagođavanja plata zasnovana na dokazima koja bi mogla doprinijeti zadržavanju kvalifikovanih nastavnika i ublažavanju odliva mozgova, koji trenutno pogađa 28% visoko obrazovanih Crnogoraca.
Neprekinut obrazovni kontinuitet za učenike kada nastavnici ostvare pravo na roditeljsko odsustvo.
Realne opcije fleksibilnog rada koje uvažavaju prirodu nastavničkog poziva, a istovremeno pružaju pogodnosti gdje je to izvodljivo.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka izvodljivost. Mehanizam agregiranog izvještavanja zahtijeva samo administrativno pojašnjenje, a ne novo zakonodavstvo. Aditivni model roditeljskog odsustva povećava troškove za otprilike 17% u odnosu na trenutni prijedlog, ali ostaje u okviru fiskalnog prostora uporedivih država kandidatkinja za članstvo u EU. Registar zamjenskih nastavnika nadograđuje postojeće mehanizme i mogao bi biti operativan u roku od jedne akademske godine. Indeks konkurentnosti plata zahtijeva samo kapacitete za prikupljanje i analizu podataka koji već postoje u okviru MONSTATA i Ministarstva.

assessments.summary_mne: Izmjene Zakona o radu predstavljaju pozitivan korak za sektor obrazovanja, posebno kroz odredbe o transparentnosti zarada koje će rasvijetliti i pomoći u ispravljanju procijenjenog rodnog jaza u platama od 9–11% među nastavnicima, kao i kroz reforme porodiljskog odsustva koje će biti od koristi pretežno ženskom nastavnom kadru. Međutim, odluka ne rješava direktno hronično potplaćivanje stručnjaka u obrazovanju u odnosu na druge javne sektore, niti sadrži sektorski specifične odredbe za ublažavanje odliva mozgova kvalifikovanih nastavnika i akademskog osoblja. Prag od 100 zaposlenih za obavezno izvještavanje o rodnom jazu u platama isključuje većinu škola pojedinačno, što potencijalno ograničava uticaj zakona u obrazovanju ukoliko se izvještavanje ne objedini na nivou institucije ili ministarstva.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Ministarstvo prosvjete pozitivno ocjenjuje ove izmjene Zakona o radu, budući da će reforme transparentnosti zarada i roditeljskog odsustva biti od posebne koristi pretežno ženskom nastavnom kadru i pomoći u smanjivanju dokumentovanog rodnog jaza u platama od 9–11% među nastavnicima. Međutim, prag izvještavanja od 100 zaposlenih prijeti isključivanjem većine pojedinačnih škola, a reformama je propuštena prilika da se odgovori na izazove specifične za sektor obrazovanja, poput hronično niskih nastavničkih plata i odliva mozgova kvalifikovanih stručnjaka.
key_concerns_mne: Prag od 100 zaposlenih za obavezno izvještavanje o rodnom jazu u platama može isključiti većinu pojedinačnih škola u Crnoj Gori, potencijalno ostavljajući pretežno feminizovani sektor obrazovanja van domašaja upravo onih mehanizama transparentnosti koji su osmišljeni radi rješavanja nejednakosti u platama.

Smanjenje primarnog roditeljskog odsustva sa 12 na 10 mjeseci, na što je ukazao Centar za ženska prava, moglo bi nesrazmjerno pogoditi nastavnice koje čine većinu nastavnog kadra, izazivajući kadrovske poremećaje i pritisak na prosvjetne radnike koji se vraćaju na posao.

Odredbe o fleksibilnom radu (rad na daljinu, skraćeno radno vrijeme) teško je primjeniti u nastavi, koja zahtijeva fizičko prisustvo u učionici, što potencijalno stvara sistem sa dva nivoa u kojem su prosvjetni radnici de facto isključeni iz pogodnosti dostupnih u drugim sektorima.

Transparentnost zarada, bez pratećeg poboljšanja plata stručnjaka u obrazovanju, mogla bi ubrzati odliv mozgova jer bi nastavnici postali svjesniji razlike u naknadama u odnosu na druge sektore i na nastavna mjesta u državama članicama EU.

Zakonu nedostaju sektorski specifične smjernice za primjenu u javnom obrazovanju, gdje se strukture zapošljavanja (mnoge male škole pod centralizovanim upravljanjem) suštinski razlikuju od modela privatnog sektora oko kojeg je zakon, čini se, koncipiran.
recommendations_mne: Razjasniti da se obaveze izvještavanja o rodnom jazu u platama u sektoru obrazovanja primjenjuju na nivou poslodavca upisanog u registar (Ministarstvo prosvjete ili opštinski sekretarijati), a ne na nivou pojedinačnih škola, kako bi se osiguralo da nastavni kadar bude obuhvaćen pragom od 100 zaposlenih.

Prihvatiti prijedlog Centra za ženska prava o zadržavanju ukupno 365 dana roditeljskog odsustva uz dodavanje 60 dana koja su isključivo rezervisana za drugog roditelja, umjesto preraspodjelom postojećeg odsustva, radi zaštite roditeljskih prava i stabilnosti školskog kadra.

Izraditi smjernice za primjenu fleksibilnih oblika rada specifične za sektor obrazovanja, uz identifikaciju radnih mjesta (administrativnih, tehničkih, određenih planskih funkcija) koja mogu biti organizovana na daljinu ili fleksibilno, te onih koja zahtijevaju fizičko prisustvo.

Reforme transparentnosti zarada dopuniti namjenskim pregledom konkurentnosti nastavničkih plata prema referentnim vrijednostima EU i regiona, kako bi transparentnost dovela do povećanja zarada, a ne do pojačane svjesnosti o mogućnostima iseljavanja.

Obezbijediti da ustanove strukovnog obrazovanja i njihovi nastavnici budu izričito uključeni u okvire transparentnosti zarada i izvještavanja o rodnom jazu u platama, uzimajući u obzir veličinu ovog sektora (68,8% upisanih učenika) i njegovu posebnu ranjivost na rodno uslovljene razlike u platama.

Uspostaviti tranzicioni fond ili rezervni kadar nastavnika radi upravljanja kadrovskim posljedicama proširenog očinskog i roditeljskog odsustva u školama, posebno u ruralnim sredinama gdje je nestašica nastavnika već izražena.

assessments.summary: The Labour Law amendments are broadly positive for public health and the health workforce, introducing paternity leave aligned with EU Directive 2019/1158, wage transparency that could help address health worker retention, and minimum wage provisions tied to purchasing power. However, from a health perspective, the effective reduction of mothers' parental leave from 12 to 10 months raises concerns about maternal and infant health outcomes, breastfeeding duration, and postnatal mental health, particularly given that only around 400 fathers per year currently take parental leave in Montenegro. The gender pay gap reporting obligation is welcome and relevant to the healthcare sector, where women constitute the majority of the workforce. Overall, the reforms advance EU alignment under Chapter 19 but require careful implementation safeguards to avoid unintended negative health consequences for mothers and infants.
assessments.executive_summary: The Ministry of Health views these Labour Law amendments as a positive step for worker wellbeing and EU alignment, but flags a significant health risk: reducing mothers' effective parental leave from 12 to 10 months could harm breastfeeding rates, postnatal mental health, and infant development, especially since fewer than 430 fathers annually use parental leave. The Ministry recommends maintaining the full 12-month leave for mothers while adding the new paternity entitlements, and leveraging the wage transparency provisions to specifically address health workforce retention.
assessments.reasoning:
From a public health perspective, these Labour Law amendments present both significant opportunities and notable risks that require careful examination.

ALIGNMENT WITH EU HEALTH AND SOCIAL ACQUIS: The amendments are explicitly designed to align Montenegro with EU Directive 2019/1158 on work-life balance for parents and carers, which is part of Chapter 19 (Social Policy and Employment) of the EU accession acquis. The 10-day paternity leave matches the EU directive minimum exactly. The two months of non-transferable parental leave also mirrors the directive's requirement. This alignment is important for Montenegro's EU accession trajectory and demonstrates legislative progress. However, alignment with Chapter 19 should not come at the cost of health outcomes that fall under Chapter 28 (Consumer and Health Protection).

MATERNAL AND INFANT HEALTH CONCERNS: The most significant health concern is the de facto reduction of maternal leave from 12 months to 10 months. Under the current system, mothers can take up to 365 days of parental leave. Under the new system, two months are designated as non-transferable for the father, meaning the mother's effective maximum drops to approximately 10 months. This is concerning because WHO recommends exclusive breastfeeding for six months and continued breastfeeding alongside complementary foods up to two years. Longer maternity leave is consistently associated with higher breastfeeding rates and duration. Montenegro's breastfeeding rates already require improvement. Data from the Center for Women's Rights (Centar za zenska prava) shows that only 353 fathers used parental leave in 2023, 426 in 2024, and 423 in 2025. This means that in practice, the two months reserved for fathers will likely go unused by the vast majority of families, resulting in a net loss of two months of parental care for infants. The reform risks penalizing mothers and children in households where fathers do not or cannot take leave, without providing any compensating health benefit.

POSTNATAL MENTAL HEALTH: Shorter parental leave for primary caregivers is associated with higher rates of postnatal depression and anxiety. Montenegro's mental health infrastructure is already underdeveloped, with limited community-based mental health services and heavy reliance on institutional care. Reducing the period during which new mothers are protected from workplace pressures could increase demand for mental health services that the system is not equipped to provide. This intersects with Montenegro's ongoing deinstitutionalisation efforts, which aim to shift mental health care to community settings but remain incomplete.

HEALTH WORKFORCE IMPLICATIONS: The wage transparency provisions are potentially very beneficial for the health sector. Montenegro's healthcare system suffers from significant brain drain, with doctors and nurses emigrating to EU countries offering better compensation. Women constitute approximately 70-75% of the health workforce in Montenegro, concentrated in nursing and allied health professions. Gender pay gap reporting for entities with over 100 employees would cover the Clinical Centre of Montenegro and other major health institutions. Making wage disparities visible could help address inequities within the health system and potentially improve retention. The prohibition on asking about previous salary could also benefit health workers moving between public and private sectors. The purchasing power criterion for minimum wage is relevant to lower-paid health workers such as medical technicians, cleaners, and administrative staff in healthcare facilities. If minimum wage adjustments better reflect actual living costs, this could reduce the push factor driving support staff emigration and improve staffing levels in healthcare institutions.

FLEXIBLE WORKING ARRANGEMENTS: The provisions for flexible hours, reduced workdays, and remote work for parents and caregivers have mixed implications for healthcare. While these are positive for work-life balance, healthcare delivery often requires physical presence and fixed schedules. The law's provision that remote work is voluntary and cannot result in adverse consequences is important, but implementation in hospital settings will be challenging. Clear guidelines will be needed to prevent staffing shortages while respecting workers' rights.

SOCIAL PROTECTION CONTEXT: Montenegro's social protection system, administered through Centres for Social Work, already faces capacity constraints. The new parental leave framework will require administrative adjustments to process paternity leave claims and enforce the non-transferable leave provisions. The Ministry of Health is concerned that without adequate investment in social protection infrastructure, the reforms may create bureaucratic barriers that delay or discourage leave uptake, particularly among lower-income families who depend most on uninterrupted income.

DISABILITY AND CAREGIVERS: The flexible working arrangements for carers are particularly relevant for families caring for persons with disabilities. Montenegro's deinstitutionalisation strategy relies heavily on family-based care, and these provisions could support families in maintaining employment while providing care. However, five days of carers' leave per year, while matching the EU minimum, is insufficient for families managing complex care needs associated with severe disability.

Sources consulted: EU Directive 2019/1158 on work-life balance; ILO report on gender pay gap in Montenegro (21.6% average gap); Center for Women's Rights data on father leave uptake (353 in 2023, 426 in 2024, 423 in 2025); WHO European Observatory Health Systems in Action Montenegro 2024; OECD Health at a Glance 2025; Portal Analitika, CdM, Adria TV reporting on the amendments; Montenegro Gender Equality Index data.
key_concerns: Effective reduction of mothers' parental leave from 12 to 10 months poses risks to breastfeeding rates, infant nutrition, and early childhood development, contradicting WHO recommendations on exclusive breastfeeding for six months

Extremely low father leave uptake (only 353-426 fathers per year) means the non-transferable two-month provision will in practice result in lost leave for most families rather than shared caregiving, directly harming infant and maternal health outcomes

Shorter protected leave for primary caregivers may increase postnatal depression and anxiety in a context where Montenegro's community mental health services are underdeveloped and deinstitutionalisation incomplete

Healthcare sector implementation of flexible working arrangements will be challenging given the physical-presence requirements of clinical care, potentially creating staffing conflicts in hospitals and primary care
Five days annual carers' leave is insufficient for families managing complex disability care needs, undermining the deinstitutionalisation strategy that depends on family-based care capacity

No specific provisions address the health workforce brain drain; wage transparency alone will not close the compensation gap between Montenegro and EU member states that drives emigration of doctors and nurses
recommendations: Maintain the full 365-day parental leave entitlement for the primary caregiver (typically the mother) while adding the 10-day paternity leave and two months of additional father-specific leave on top, rather than carving from existing entitlements, to avoid harming maternal and infant health outcomes

Introduce targeted incentives (bonus payments, pension credit enhancements) for fathers who take parental leave, to increase uptake from the current 400 per year before making the non-transferable provision binding with leave-reducing consequences for mothers

Mandate that gender pay gap reports from healthcare institutions (Clinical Centre, hospitals, health centres) be submitted to and published by the Ministry of Health, enabling sector-specific analysis and retention strategies

Include healthcare worker wage competitiveness benchmarking as part of the purchasing power minimum wage review, comparing Montenegro health sector compensation to regional and EU averages to inform retention policy
Develop specific guidelines for implementing flexible working arrangements in healthcare settings, balancing workers' rights with patient safety and continuous care requirements

Expand carers' leave beyond five days for families caring for persons with severe disabilities, aligned with the National Deinstitutionalisation Strategy, to be reviewed in coordination with the Ministry of Health

Commission an impact assessment of the parental leave changes on maternal mental health, breastfeeding rates, and infant health indicators within the first two years of implementation, with predetermined thresholds for policy correction

counter_proposal.title: Additive Parental Leave Model with Health Workforce Retention Package
counter_proposal.summary: Rather than redistributing existing parental leave between parents (effectively cutting mothers' leave from 12 to 10 months), the Ministry of Health proposes an additive model: maintain the full 365-day entitlement for the primary caregiver and add the father's 10-day paternity leave and two-month parental leave as new, additional entitlements. This preserves the health benefits of extended maternal leave while still encouraging paternal involvement. Simultaneously, the Ministry proposes leveraging the wage transparency framework to create a health-sector-specific retention strategy, requiring public health institutions to report not only gender pay gaps but also compensation benchmarks against EU and regional averages, feeding directly into workforce retention planning.
key_changes: Add father's paternity leave (10 days) and parental leave (2 months) as new entitlements on top of existing 365-day maternal leave, rather than carving from it
Phase in the non-transferable clause over 3 years, tied to measurable increases in father leave uptake, with a minimum threshold of 2000 fathers per year before enforcement
Require health institutions with 100+ employees to submit wage gap reports directly to the Ministry of Health in addition to the labour inspectorate
Include health workforce compensation benchmarking in the biennial minimum wage purchasing power review
Extend carers' leave to 15 days per year for families caring for persons with disabilities rated at 80% or higher incapacity
Mandate a health impact assessment of parental leave changes within 18 months, measuring breastfeeding rates, postnatal depression incidence, and infant health indicators
expected_benefits: Preserves WHO-recommended breastfeeding duration and early bonding period by maintaining full maternal leave
Avoids increased postnatal mental health burden on an already strained mental health system
Creates a health-sector-specific evidence base for workforce retention through targeted wage gap reporting
Phased approach to non-transferable leave allows cultural adjustment and avoids punishing families where fathers cannot take leave
Extended carers' leave supports deinstitutionalisation by strengthening family-based care capacity
Health impact assessment creates an evidence-based feedback loop for future policy adjustments
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: Moderately feasible. The additive parental leave model would cost approximately 1.5-2 million euros annually (based on current leave compensation rates and projected father uptake), which is manageable within the social insurance budget. The phased approach to non-transferable leave reduces political and social resistance. Health-sector wage reporting requires only administrative adjustments to existing reporting frameworks. The main obstacle is fiscal: adding leave entitlements rather than redistributing them increases total costs, but this can be partially offset by the fact that father uptake will remain low initially. The health impact assessment is low-cost and can be implemented through existing MONSTAT and Institute of Public Health data collection mechanisms.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Aditivni model roditeljskog odsustva s paketom zadržavanja zdravstvenih radnika
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Umjesto preraspodjele postojećeg roditeljskog odsustva između roditelja (što bi efektivno smanjilo majčino odsustvo s 12 na 10 mjeseci), Ministarstvo zdravlja predlaže aditivni model: zadržati puno pravo na 365 dana odsustva za primarnog njegovatelja te dodati očevo odsustvo po osnovu očinstva od 10 dana i dvomjesečno roditeljsko odsustvo kao nova, dodatna prava. Na taj način čuvaju se zdravstvene prednosti produženog majčinog odsustva, uz istovremeno podsticanje uključenosti očeva. Ministarstvo istovremeno predlaže korišćenje okvira za transparentnost zarada radi stvaranja strategije zadržavanja kadrova specifične za zdravstveni sektor, kojom bi se od javnih zdravstvenih ustanova zahtijevalo da izvještavaju ne samo o rodnim razlikama u zaradama, već i o kompenzacionim referentnim vrijednostima u poređenju s prosjekom EU i regiona, što bi se direktno ugrađivalo u planiranje zadržavanja radne snage.
