key_changes_mne: Dodati očevo odsustvo po osnovu očinstva (10 dana) i roditeljsko odsustvo (2 mjeseca) kao nova prava povrh postojećeg majčinog odsustva od 365 dana, a ne oduzimanjem od njega
Postepeno uvođenje klauzule o neprenosivosti tokom 3 godine, vezano za mjerljivo povećanje korišćenja očevog odsustva, s minimalnim pragom od 2000 očeva godišnje prije primjene
Obavezati zdravstvene ustanove s 100 i više zaposlenih da izvještaje o razlici u zaradama dostavljaju direktno Ministarstvu zdravlja, pored inspekcije rada
Uključiti referentno vrednovanje kompenzacija zdravstvenih radnika u dvogodišnji pregled kupovne moći minimalne zarade
Produžiti odsustvo za njegu na 15 dana godišnje za porodice koje se staraju o licima s invaliditetom ocijenjenim na 80% ili više nesposobnosti
Propisati obavezu procjene zdravstvenog uticaja promjena roditeljskog odsustva u roku od 18 mjeseci, uz mjerenje stope dojenja, učestalosti postnatalne depresije i zdravstvenih pokazatelja dojenčadi
expected_benefits_mne: Čuva trajanje dojenja i period ranog vezivanja koje preporučuje SZO, zadržavanjem punog majčinog odsustva
Izbjegava povećano opterećenje ionako preopterećenog sistema mentalnog zdravlja postporođajnim poremećajima
Stvara bazu dokaza specifičnu za zdravstveni sektor radi zadržavanja radne snage putem ciljanog izvještavanja o razlikama u zaradama
Postepeni pristup neprenosivom odsustvu omogućava kulturološko prilagođavanje i izbjegava kažnjavanje porodica u kojima očevi ne mogu koristiti odsustvo
Produženo odsustvo za njegu podržava deinstitucionalizaciju jačanjem kapaciteta porodičnog zbrinjavanja
Procjena zdravstvenog uticaja stvara povratnu spregu zasnovanu na dokazima za buduća usklađivanja politika
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Umjereno izvodljivo. Aditivni model roditeljskog odsustva koštao bi približno 1,5–2 miliona eura godišnje (na osnovu trenutnih stopa naknade za odsustvo i projektovanog korišćenja od strane očeva), što je prihvatljivo u okviru budžeta socijalnog osiguranja. Postepeni pristup neprenosivom odsustvu smanjuje politički i društveni otpor. Izvještavanje o zaradama u zdravstvenom sektoru zahtijeva samo administrativna prilagođavanja postojećih okvira za izvještavanje. Glavna prepreka je fiskalne prirode: dodavanje prava na odsustvo umjesto njihove preraspodjele povećava ukupne troškove, ali to se djelimično može nadoknaditi činjenicom da će korišćenje od strane očeva u početku ostati nisko. Procjena zdravstvenog uticaja je niskobudžetna i može se sprovesti putem postojećih mehanizama prikupljanja podataka MONSTAT-a i Instituta za javno zdravlje.

assessments.summary_mne: Izmjene Zakona o radu su u širem smislu pozitivne za javno zdravlje i zdravstvene radnike, uvodeći roditeljsko odsustvo za očeve usklađeno s Direktivom EU 2019/1158, transparentnost zarada koja bi mogla pomoći u zadržavanju zdravstvenih radnika, te odredbe o minimalnoj plati vezane za kupovnu moć. Međutim, iz zdravstvene perspektive, efektivno smanjenje roditeljskog odsustva majki sa 12 na 10 mjeseci izaziva zabrinutost u pogledu ishoda po zdravlje majke i djeteta, dužine dojenja i postnatalnog mentalnog zdravlja, posebno imajući u vidu da samo oko 400 očeva godišnje koristi roditeljsko odsustvo u Crnoj Gori. Obaveza izvještavanja o razlici u zaradama između polova je dobrodošla i relevantna za sektor zdravstvene zaštite, u kome žene čine većinu radne snage. Sveukupno, reforme unapređuju usklađenost s EU u okviru Poglavlja 19, ali zahtijevaju pažljive zaštitne mjere pri implementaciji kako bi se izbjegle nenamjerne negativne zdravstvene posljedice po majke i dojenčad.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Ministarstvo zdravlja smatra ove izmjene Zakona o radu pozitivnim korakom za dobrobit radnika i usklađivanje s EU, ali ukazuje na značajan zdravstveni rizik: smanjenje efektivnog roditeljskog odsustva majki sa 12 na 10 mjeseci moglo bi negativno uticati na stope dojenja, postnatalno mentalno zdravlje i razvoj dojenčadi, posebno imajući u vidu da manje od 430 očeva godišnje koristi roditeljsko odsustvo. Ministarstvo preporučuje zadržavanje punog 12-mjesečnog odsustva za majke uz dodavanje novih prava za očeve, te korišćenje odredbi o transparentnosti zarada u cilju specifičnog rješavanja pitanja zadržavanja zdravstvenih radnika.
key_concerns_mne: Efektivno smanjenje roditeljskog odsustva majki sa 12 na 10 mjeseci predstavlja rizik po stope dojenja, ishranu dojenčadi i rani razvoj djeteta, što je u suprotnosti s preporukama SZO o isključivom dojenju tokom šest mjeseci

Izuzetno niska stopa korišćenja odsustva od strane očeva (svega 353–426 očeva godišnje) znači da će neprenosiva odredba od dva mjeseca u praksi rezultirati izgubljenim odsustvom za većinu porodica, umjesto zajedničkog roditeljstva, čime se direktno narušavaju zdravstveni ishodi dojenčadi i majki

Kraće zaštićeno odsustvo za primarnog staratelja može povećati postnatalnu depresiju i anksioznost u kontekstu u kome su službe za zaštitu mentalnog zdravlja u zajednici u Crnoj Gori nerazvijene, a deinstitucionalizacija nedovršena

Primjena fleksibilnih radnih aranžmana u sektoru zdravstvene zaštite biće izazovna s obzirom na zahtjeve fizičke prisutnosti u kliničkoj njezi, što bi moglo izazvati kadrovske konflikte u bolnicama i primarnoj zdravstvenoj zaštiti

Pet dana godišnjeg odsustva za njegu nedovoljno je za porodice koje upravljaju složenim potrebama za njegom osoba s invaliditetom, čime se podriva strategija deinstitucionalizacije koja počiva na kapacitetu porodične njege

Ne postoje posebne odredbe koje se bave odlivom mozgova u sektoru zdravstvene radne snage; sama transparentnost zarada neće zatvoriti jaz u naknadi između Crne Gore i država članica EU koji pokreće emigraciju ljekara i medicinskih sestara
recommendations_mne: Zadržati puno pravo na roditeljsko odsustvo od 365 dana za primarnog staratelja (najčešće majku), uz dodavanje 10-dnevnog odsustva za oca i dva dodatna mjeseca odsustva specifičnog za oca povrh postojećih prava, umjesto umanjivanja postojećih prava, kako bi se izbjegle štetne posljedice po zdravlje majke i djeteta

Uvesti ciljane podsticaje (bonuse, unapređenje penzijskih kredita) za očeve koji koriste roditeljsko odsustvo, kako bi se povećala stopa korišćenja sa trenutnih 400 godišnje, prije nego što neprenosiva odredba postane obavezujuća s posljedicama smanjenja odsustva za majke

Obavezati zdravstvene ustanove (Klinički centar, bolnice, domove zdravlja) da izvještaje o razlici u zaradama između polova dostavljaju Ministarstvu zdravlja i objavljuju ih, čime se omogućava sektorska analiza i razvoj strategija zadržavanja kadra

Uključiti benchmarking konkurentnosti zarada zdravstvenih radnika u pregled minimalne plate zasnovane na kupovnoj moći, upoređujući naknade u zdravstvenom sektoru Crne Gore s regionalnim i EU prosjekima radi informisanja politike zadržavanja kadra
Izraditi posebne smjernice za primjenu fleksibilnih radnih aranžmana u zdravstvenim ustanovama, usklađujući prava radnika sa zahtjevima sigurnosti pacijenata i kontinuirane njege

Proširiti odsustvo za njegu iznad pet dana za porodice koje se brinu o licima s teškim invaliditetom, usklađeno s Nacionalnom strategijom deinstitucionalizacije, uz preispitivanje u koordinaciji s Ministarstvom zdravlja

Naručiti procjenu uticaja izmjena roditeljskog odsustva na mentalno zdravlje majki, stope dojenja i zdravstvene pokazatelje dojenčadi u prvim dvjema godinama od primjene, s unaprijed utvrđenim pragovima za korekciju politike

assessments.summary: The Labour Law amendments on paternity leave, wage transparency, and gender pay gap reporting are a social policy reform under EU Chapter 19 (Social Policy and Employment) with no direct impact on environmental protection, climate change, waste management, coastal zone management, or any other domain under the Ministry of Environment's competence. While the decision is a legitimate step toward EU accession, it does not advance Montenegro's critically lagging Chapter 27 (Environment and Climate Change) obligations, which remain the most complex and resource-intensive chapter in the accession process.
assessments.executive_summary: This labour law reform is environmentally neutral -- it neither helps nor hinders environmental objectives. The Ministry of Environment's primary concern is the opportunity cost: government legislative capacity spent on Chapter 19 benchmarks while Chapter 27 implementation remains critically behind schedule, with the National Waste Management Plan still unadopted and the National Energy and Climate Plan delayed. We recommend the government allocate equivalent legislative urgency to pending environmental legislation.
assessments.reasoning:
The Labour Law amendments address paternity leave, wage transparency, gender pay gap reporting, and minimum wage criteria. These fall squarely within the domain of EU Chapter 19 (Social Policy and Employment) and have no direct bearing on environmental protection, climate policy, biodiversity, waste management, water quality, air pollution, spatial planning, or any other area under the Ministry of Environment's mandate.

From a purely environmental standpoint, this decision is neither positive nor negative. The flexible work arrangements provisions (remote work, reduced hours) could theoretically reduce commuting-related emissions in a marginal way, but this effect would be negligible in the Montenegrin context where public transport infrastructure is underdeveloped and car dependency is high regardless.

The more substantive concern from the Ministry of Environment's perspective is one of legislative prioritization and government bandwidth. According to the Green Home shadow report and the European Commission's own assessments, Montenegro has achieved only limited progress on Chapter 27 closing benchmarks. Critical environmental legislation remains unadopted: the National Waste Management Plan has not been finalized, the National Energy and Climate Plan (NECP) submitted in draft in December 2024 still requires formal adoption and implementation, and numerous by-laws in air quality, nature protection, and climate change areas are pending. The UNECE Environmental Performance Review flagged waste management and climate adaptation as pressing challenges.

Montenegro's government has stated its ambition to close all negotiating chapters by end of 2026, with EU membership targeted for 2028. Chapter 27 is widely recognized as the most technically complex and costly chapter in the EU accession process. While advancing Chapter 19 benchmarks is legitimate and necessary, the Ministry of Environment notes that the same legislative urgency and political will demonstrated for labour reforms has not been consistently applied to environmental legislation. The 114th Cabinet session agenda itself included border control infrastructure and airport concession items but no environmental legislation.

This is not a criticism of the labour reforms themselves, which appear well-designed and aligned with EU directives on work-life balance and pay transparency. Rather, it is an observation that the government's legislative pipeline should reflect the scale of the challenge posed by Chapter 27, where implementation gaps are widening rather than narrowing. Every session that passes without advancing key environmental legislation increases the risk of Chapter 27 becoming the bottleneck that delays Montenegro's overall EU accession timeline.
key_concerns: No direct environmental relevance -- the decision does not address any aspect of environmental protection, climate policy, or natural resource management
Legislative opportunity cost -- government capacity is devoted to Chapter 19 while Chapter 27 implementation remains critically behind schedule with limited progress reported
The National Waste Management Plan remains unadopted despite being a key Chapter 27 closing benchmark, and was not addressed at the 114th session
The National Energy and Climate Plan (NECP), submitted in draft in December 2024, still awaits formal government adoption and implementation
Pending environmental by-laws in air quality, nature protection, and climate change continue to accumulate delays with extended deadlines
Chapter 27 risks becoming the bottleneck for Montenegro's 2026 chapter-closure target and 2028 EU membership ambition
recommendations: The government should schedule dedicated Cabinet sessions or session blocks specifically for Chapter 27 environmental legislation to match the pace shown on Chapter 19 reforms
Urgently adopt the National Waste Management Plan and accelerate adoption of remaining environmental by-laws identified in the Chapter 27 Action Plan
Formally adopt and begin implementation of the National Energy and Climate Plan (NECP) which has been in draft since December 2024

Future labour and social policy reforms should integrate environmental sustainability considerations where applicable -- for example, green job training provisions or incentives for environmentally sustainable remote work infrastructure
Establish a cross-ministerial legislative tracking dashboard that makes visible the relative progress across all open negotiating chapters, ensuring Chapter 27 receives proportional attention

counter_proposal.title: Parallel Environmental Legislative Fast-Track
counter_proposal.summary: Rather than opposing these labour reforms, which are sound policy, the Ministry of Environment would propose a parallel fast-track legislative initiative for critical Chapter 27 legislation. For every Cabinet session that advances a Chapter 19 benchmark, a corresponding session should advance Chapter 27 benchmarks. Specifically, the next two Cabinet sessions should include the formal adoption of the National Waste Management Plan and the National Energy and Climate Plan, along with at least three priority environmental by-laws identified in the Chapter 27 Action Plan.
key_changes: Adopt the National Waste Management Plan at the 115th or 116th Cabinet session
Formally adopt the National Energy and Climate Plan (NECP) with implementation timeline
Introduce a chapter-balancing principle requiring environmental legislation to be scheduled alongside social legislation in the Cabinet work programme
Prioritize at least three overdue environmental by-laws (air quality, nature protection, climate) for adoption within Q1 2026
expected_benefits: Balanced progress across EU negotiating chapters, reducing risk of Chapter 27 becoming a bottleneck
Demonstrates to the European Commission that Montenegro treats environmental obligations with equal seriousness as social policy
Accelerates adoption of critical waste and climate frameworks before 2026 chapter-closure deadline
Strengthens Montenegro's overall EU accession credibility and timeline
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High. This proposal does not require additional funding or institutional reform -- only political will to schedule environmental legislation with the same urgency as labour reforms. The draft plans already exist; they need to be finalized and placed on the Cabinet agenda.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Paralelno ubrzano usvajanje zakona iz oblasti životne sredine
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Umjesto da se suprotstavlja ovim reformama radnog zakonodavstva, koje predstavljaju kvalitetnu politiku, Ministarstvo životne sredine predlaže paralelnu inicijativu za ubrzano donošenje ključnih zakona iz Poglavlja 27. Za svaku sjednicu Vlade na kojoj se unapređuje neka referentna vrijednost iz Poglavlja 19, odgovarajuća sjednica trebalo bi da unapređuje referentne vrijednosti iz Poglavlja 27. Konkretno, naredne dvije sjednice Vlade trebalo bi da uključe formalno usvajanje Nacionalnog plana upravljanja otpadom i Nacionalnog energetskog i klimatskog plana, zajedno s najmanje tri prioritetna podzakonska akta iz oblasti životne sredine utvrđena Akcionim planom za Poglavlje 27.
key_changes_mne: Usvajanje Nacionalnog plana upravljanja otpadom na 115. ili 116. sjednici Vlade
Formalno usvajanje Nacionalnog energetskog i klimatskog plana (NECP) s vremenskim okvirom za implementaciju
Uvođenje principa balansiranja poglavlja kojim se zahtijeva da zakonodavstvo iz oblasti životne sredine bude planirano paralelno sa socijalnim zakonodavstvom u programu rada Vlade
Davanje prioriteta najmanje tri zakašnjela podzakonska akta iz oblasti životne sredine (kvalitet vazduha, zaštita prirode, klima) radi usvajanja u prvom kvartalu 2026. godine
expected_benefits_mne: Uravnotežen napredak u svim poglavljima pregovora s EU, čime se smanjuje rizik da Poglavlje 27 postane usko grlo
Demonstrira Evropskoj komisiji da Crna Gora prema obavezama u oblasti životne sredine postupa s jednakom ozbiljnošću kao prema socijalnoj politici
Ubrzava usvajanje ključnih okvira u oblasti upravljanja otpadom i klimatskih promjena prije krajnjeg roka za zatvaranje poglavlja 2026. godine
Jača ukupni kredibilitet i dinamiku pristupanja Crne Gore Evropskoj uniji
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka. Ovaj prijedlog ne zahtijeva dodatna sredstva niti institucionalnu reformu — jedino što je potrebno jeste politička volja da se zakonodavstvo iz oblasti životne sredine planira s istom hitnošću kao i reforme radnog zakonodavstva. Nacrti planova već postoje; potrebno ih je finalizovati i uvrstiti na dnevni red Vlade.

assessments.summary_mne: Izmjene Zakona o radu koje se tiču porodiljskog odsustva za očeve, transparentnosti zarada i izvještavanja o rodnom jazu u platama predstavljaju reformu socijalne politike u okviru Poglavlja 19 EU (Socijalna politika i zapošljavanje) i nemaju direktan uticaj na zaštitu životne sredine, klimatske promjene, upravljanje otpadom, upravljanje obalnim područjem niti na bilo koju drugu oblast u nadležnosti Ministarstva ekologije. Mada je ova odluka legitiman korak ka pristupanju EU, njome se ne napreduje u ispunjavanju obaveza iz Poglavlja 27 (Životna sredina i klimatske promjene) u kojima Crna Gora kritično zaostaje, a koje ostaje najsloženije i najzahtjevnije poglavlje u procesu pristupanja po pitanju resursa.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Ova reforma radnog zakonodavstva je neutralna sa aspekta životne sredine -- ne doprinosi niti šteti ekološkim ciljevima. Primarni problem Ministarstva ekologije jeste oportunitetni trošak: zakonodavni kapaciteti Vlade troše se na ispunjavanje referentnih uslova iz Poglavlja 19, dok implementacija Poglavlja 27 kritično kasni, Nacionalni plan upravljanja otpadom još uvijek nije usvojen, a Nacionalni energetski i klimatski plan je prolongiran. Preporučujemo da Vlada s jednakim zakonodavnim prioritetom pristupi rješavanju odloženog ekološkog zakonodavstva.
key_concerns_mne: Nema direktne relevantnosti za životnu sredinu -- odluka ne adresira nijedan aspekt zaštite životne sredine, klimatske politike ni upravljanja prirodnim resursima
Oportunitetni trošak na zakonodavnom planu -- kapaciteti Vlade usmjereni su na Poglavlje 19, dok implementacija Poglavlja 27 kritično kasni uz ograničen napredak koji se bilježi
Nacionalni plan upravljanja otpadom i dalje nije usvojen, uprkos tome što predstavlja ključni referentni uslov za zatvaranje Poglavlja 27, a nije razmatran ni na 114. sjednici
Nacionalni energetski i klimatski plan (NECP), dostavljen u nacrtu u decembru 2024. godine, još uvijek čeka zvaničan vladino usvajanje i sprovođenje
Podzakonski akti iz oblasti kvaliteta vazduha, zaštite prirode i klimatskih promjena koji su na čekanju nastavljaju da kasne, uz produžavanje rokova
Poglavlje 27 prijeti da postane usko grlo za Crnu Goru u ostvarivanju cilja zatvaranja poglavlja do 2026. i ambicije punopravnog članstva u EU do 2028. godine
recommendations_mne: Vlada bi trebalo da zakaže posebne sjednice Kabineta ili blokove sjednica posvećene isključivo ekološkom zakonodavstvu iz Poglavlja 27, kako bi se dostigao tempo koji je pokazan u reformama Poglavlja 19
Hitno usvojiti Nacionalni plan upravljanja otpadom i ubrzati usvajanje preostalih podzakonskih akata iz oblasti životne sredine predviđenih Akcionim planom za Poglavlje 27
Zvanično usvojiti i pristupiti sprovođenju Nacionalnog energetskog i klimatskog plana (NECP), koji je u nacrtu od decembra 2024. godine

Buduće reforme radnog i socijalnog zakonodavstva trebalo bi, gdje je primjenljivo, da integirišu aspekte ekološke održivosti -- na primjer, odredbe o osposobljavanju za zelena zanimanja ili podsticaje za ekološki održivu infrastrukturu za rad na daljinu

Uspostaviti međuresornu platformu za praćenje zakonodavnih aktivnosti koja će transparentno prikazivati relativni napredak u svim otvorenim pregovaračkim poglavljima, osiguravajući da Poglavlje 27 dobije srazmjernu pažnju

assessments.summary: The Labour Law amendments represent a meaningful step toward EU Chapter 19 alignment and improved worker protections that will benefit the tourism workforce in the medium term. Wage transparency and gender pay gap reporting address real inequities in a sector where women are concentrated in low-paid, seasonal positions. However, the amendments do not address the most pressing tourism labour crisis -- the lack of a permanent seasonal worker framework -- and risk imposing compliance costs on small and medium tourism enterprises without accompanying support. The decision is welcome but insufficient on its own to resolve the structural labour challenges that threaten tourism competitiveness.
assessments.executive_summary: The Labour Law amendments are a positive but incomplete reform for tourism. While wage transparency and parental leave provisions will help attract and retain workers in a sector suffering acute labour shortages, the reforms miss the sector's most urgent need: a legal framework for permanent seasonal workers. The Ministry of Tourism recommends coupling these amendments with targeted seasonal employment legislation and compliance support for small tourism businesses.
assessments.reasoning:
The Montenegrin tourism sector accounts for 25-30% of GDP and approximately 22% of total employment, making any labour law reform highly consequential for the industry. The sector faces an acute and worsening labour shortage -- Budva alone needs over 8,000 seasonal workers annually -- while many Montenegrin workers emigrate to Croatia, Slovenia, or Italy for better conditions. Against this backdrop, the Labour Law amendments must be evaluated on whether they help or hinder tourism's ability to attract, retain, and fairly compensate its workforce.

WAGE TRANSPARENCY: This is the most directly beneficial provision for tourism. The requirement that employers disclose salary information in job postings and the prohibition on asking about previous compensation address a real problem in the hospitality sector, where opaque wage practices have contributed to worker distrust and high turnover. Wage transparency can help tourism employers compete more effectively for scarce labour by signalling fair pay upfront. For the northern regions (Kolašin, Žabljak, Plav), where tourism wages are even lower than on the coast, transparency could expose regional disparities and create pressure for more equitable compensation that supports year-round tourism development.

GENDER PAY GAP REPORTING: Women constitute a disproportionate share of tourism and hospitality workers, concentrated in seasonal, part-time, and low-skilled positions. ILO data shows Montenegro's gender pay gap stands at 14-16%, with a significant structural component driven by motherhood penalties and occupational segregation. The 100-employee threshold for mandatory reporting is reasonable but will capture only the largest hotel chains and tour operators -- the vast majority of Montenegrin tourism businesses are small enterprises with fewer than 50 employees. This means the provision's impact on the tourism sector will be limited unless the threshold is lowered over time.

PATERNITY AND PARENTAL LEAVE: The 10-day paternity leave and non-transferable parental leave of at least two months are progressive measures aligned with the EU Work-Life Balance Directive. For tourism, the timing implications are significant: if fathers take leave during the May-October peak season, small operations will face staffing gaps that are already difficult to fill. However, these provisions also make tourism employment more attractive to parents who previously might have avoided the sector due to inflexible conditions, potentially expanding the available labour pool. The flexible working arrangements provision (remote work, reduced hours) could support efforts to extend the tourism season and develop year-round tourism products in the north.

MINIMUM WAGE AND PURCHASING POWER: Linking the minimum wage to purchasing power and reviewing it every two years introduces predictability for employers but could raise labour costs in a sector already struggling with thin margins outside peak season. For northern Montenegro, where the cost of living is lower but tourism development is a policy priority, a national minimum wage indexed to purchasing power could make it harder for nascent tourism businesses to compete. That said, higher minimum wages could reduce the brain drain that depletes northern communities of tourism-capable workers.

WHAT IS MISSING -- THE PERMANENT SEASONAL WORKER FRAMEWORK: The most significant gap from a tourism perspective is that these amendments do nothing to address the longstanding absence of a legal framework for permanent seasonal workers. As reported by multiple sources, this reform has been stuck in legislative limbo despite broad consensus on its necessity. The Montenegrin Chamber of Commerce has pushed for a digital registration system for seasonal workers, reduction of the grey economy, and a legal status that provides year-round social protections for workers who are employed seasonally. Without this framework, the Labour Law amendments apply imperfectly to the reality of tourism employment, where many workers cycle between formal seasonal contracts and informal arrangements.

EU ALIGNMENT: While these amendments primarily address Chapter 19 (Social Policy and Employment) rather than Chapter 27 (Environment and Climate Change), there is an indirect connection. A better-regulated, better-compensated tourism workforce is essential for implementing environmental and sustainability standards in tourism operations -- from waste management in hotels to carrying capacity enforcement at national parks and coastal zones. Workers who are fairly treated and professionally trained are more likely to implement sustainable tourism practices. The amendments contribute modestly to the broader EU accession framework that includes environmental standards.

REGIONAL COMPETITIVENESS: Croatia already offers more comprehensive seasonal worker protections and has been attracting Montenegrin tourism workers as a result. These amendments narrow the gap somewhat but do not close it. Albania, Montenegro's competitor for budget-conscious tourists, has lower labour costs, meaning that increased compliance burdens without productivity gains could erode Montenegro's price competitiveness in certain segments.
key_concerns: The amendments do not address the most urgent tourism labour issue: the absence of a legal framework for permanent seasonal workers, which has been stalled for years despite broad political consensus
The 100-employee threshold for gender pay gap reporting will miss the vast majority of tourism businesses, which are small and medium enterprises, limiting the provision's impact on the sector
Paternity leave and parental leave obligations during the May-October peak season could exacerbate existing staffing shortages at small coastal and national park tourism operations
Purchasing power-indexed minimum wage increases could squeeze margins for emerging tourism businesses in northern Montenegro, where the sector is still developing
No accompanying compliance support or transition period has been announced for small tourism businesses that lack HR capacity to implement wage transparency and reporting requirements
The reforms do not address the grey economy in seasonal tourism employment, where informal arrangements remain widespread and labour protections are effectively unenforceable
recommendations: Urgently finalize and adopt the Law on Permanent Seasonal Workers as a companion measure, including digital registration, year-round social insurance coverage, and grey economy enforcement mechanisms

Introduce a phased compliance timeline for tourism SMEs (under 50 employees), with free guidance materials and simplified reporting templates developed jointly by the Ministry of Tourism and Labour Inspectorate
Lower the gender pay gap reporting threshold to 50 employees within three years, with sector-specific benchmarks for hospitality and tourism
Create a seasonal staffing contingency mechanism -- such as shared labour pools or inter-enterprise lending arrangements -- to mitigate peak-season disruptions from parental leave uptake

Pilot wage transparency requirements in the northern tourism municipalities first, using the data to design targeted incentives for employers who offer above-minimum wages in underdeveloped tourism regions

Link labour law compliance to tourism quality certification and licensing, so that hotels and tour operators meeting the new standards receive preferential treatment in destination marketing and public procurement

counter_proposal.title: Integrated Tourism Labour Reform Package
counter_proposal.summary: Rather than adopting general Labour Law amendments in isolation, the Ministry of Tourism would bundle these worker protection reforms with the long-delayed Law on Permanent Seasonal Workers and sector-specific implementation support. This integrated approach would address both the rights dimension (wage transparency, gender equity, parental leave) and the structural dimension (seasonal employment instability, grey economy, labour shortages) simultaneously, ensuring that new obligations come with the tools and frameworks needed for tourism businesses to comply effectively.
key_changes: Simultaneously adopt the Law on Permanent Seasonal Workers alongside the Labour Law amendments, creating a unified reform package
Establish a Tourism Labour Standards Fund (financed through tourism tax revenue) to subsidize compliance costs for SMEs in the first two years
Create sector-specific wage transparency templates for hospitality that account for tips, seasonal bonuses, and accommodation-inclusive compensation packages
Implement a digital seasonal worker registration platform that integrates wage transparency data, enabling real-time monitoring of pay equity in tourism
Design differentiated compliance timelines: immediate for large hotel chains, 12 months for medium enterprises, 24 months for micro-enterprises in northern regions
expected_benefits: Holistic resolution of both worker rights and structural seasonal employment challenges, reducing the risk that new obligations are undermined by informal arrangements
Reduced compliance burden on tourism SMEs through subsidies and sector-specific guidance, improving adoption rates
Better labour market data from the digital registration platform, enabling evidence-based tourism workforce planning
Stronger positioning against regional competitors (Croatia, Albania) by offering a complete, attractive employment framework for tourism workers
Accelerated EU Chapter 19 progress by demonstrating implementation capacity, not just legislative transposition
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: Moderately feasible. The Labour Law amendments are already approved, so the additional measures would need to be fast-tracked as a supplementary package. The Law on Permanent Seasonal Workers has been in draft for years, meaning much of the preparatory work is done -- political will is the binding constraint, not technical complexity. The Tourism Labour Standards Fund could be established through reallocation of existing tourism tax revenues (estimated at 15-20 million euros annually) without new taxation. The digital registration platform could build on existing MONSTAT and Tax Administration infrastructure. The main risk is inter-ministerial coordination between Tourism, Labour, and Finance, which has historically been slow.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Integrisani paket reformi radnog prava u turizmu
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Umjesto usvajanja opštih izmjena Zakona o radu izolovano, Ministarstvo turizma bi ove reforme zaštite radnika objedinilo sa dugo odlaganjem Zakona o stalnim sezonskim radnicima i sektorski specifičnom podrškom implementaciji. Ovaj integrisani pristup bi istovremeno rješavao i dimenziju prava (transparentnost zarada, rodna ravnopravnost, roditeljsko odsustvo) i strukturnu dimenziju (nestabilnost sezonskog zapošljavanja, siva ekonomija, nedostatak radne snage), osiguravajući da nove obaveze dolaze s alatima i okvirima potrebnim da turistička preduzeća mogu efektivno da ih ispune.
key_changes_mne: Istovremeno usvojiti Zakon o stalnim sezonskim radnicima zajedno s izmjenama Zakona o radu, stvarajući jedinstven reformski paket
Osnovati Fond za standarde rada u turizmu (finansiran prihodima od turističke takse) radi sufinansiranja troškova usklađivanja za mala i srednja preduzeća u prve dvije godine
Izraditi sektorski specifične šablone transparentnosti zarada za ugostiteljstvo koji uzimaju u obzir napojnice, sezonske bonuse i pakete naknada koji uključuju smještaj
Uvesti digitalnu platformu za registraciju sezonskih radnika koja integriše podatke o transparentnosti zarada, omogućavajući praćenje ravnopravnosti plata u turizmu u realnom vremenu
Osmisliti diferencirane rokove za usklađivanje: odmah za velike hotelske lance, 12 mjeseci za srednja preduzeća, 24 mjeseca za mikro-preduzeća u sjevernim regionima
expected_benefits_mne: Sveobuhvatno rješavanje kako prava radnika tako i strukturnih izazova sezonskog zapošljavanja, smanjujući rizik da nove obaveze budu potkopane neformalnim aranžmanima
Smanjen teret usklađivanja za turistička mala i srednja preduzeća kroz subvencije i sektorski specifične smjernice, što poboljšava stopu prihvatanja
Bolji podaci o tržištu rada zahvaljujući digitalnoj platformi za registraciju, što omogućava planiranje turističke radne snage zasnovano na dokazima
Jače pozicioniranje u odnosu na regionalne konkurente (Hrvatska, Albanija) nuđenjem potpunog i atraktivnog okvira zapošljavanja za turističke radnike
Ubrzani napredak u okviru Poglavlja 19 EU demonstriranjem kapaciteta implementacije, a ne samo zakonodavne transpozicije
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Umjereno izvodljivo. Izmjene Zakona o radu su već usvojene, pa bi dodatne mjere trebalo brzo sprovesti kao dopunski paket. Zakon o stalnim sezonskim radnicima je u nacrtu već godinama, što znači da je veći dio pripremnog posla obavljen — politička volja je ključno ograničenje, a ne tehnička složenost. Fond za standarde rada u turizmu mogao bi se osnovati preraspodjelom postojećih prihoda od turističke takse (procijenjenih na 15–20 miliona eura godišnje) bez uvođenja novih poreza. Digitalna platforma za registraciju mogla bi se nadovezati na postojeću infrastrukturu MONSTAT-a i Poreske uprave. Glavni rizik je međuministarska koordinacija između Ministarstva turizma, Ministarstva rada i Ministarstva finansija, koja je istorijski bila spora.

assessments.summary_mne: Izmjene Zakona o radu predstavljaju značajan korak ka usklađivanju s Poglavljem 19 EU i unapređenju zaštite radnika, što će u srednjoročnom periodu koristiti radnoj snazi u turizmu. Transparentnost zarada i izvještavanje o razlici u platama između polova adresiraju stvarne nejednakosti u sektoru u kome su žene koncentrisane na slabo plaćenim, sezonskim pozicijama. Međutim, izmjene ne rješavaju najhitniju krizu radne snage u turizmu -- odsustvo stalnog okvira za sezonske radnike -- i rizikuju nametanje troškova usklađenosti malim i srednjim preduzećima u turizmu bez prateće podrške. Odluka je dobrodošla, ali sama po sebi nije dovoljna da riješi strukturne izazove na tržištu rada koji prijete konkurentnosti turizma.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Izmjene Zakona o radu predstavljaju pozitivnu, ali nepotpunu reformu za turizam. Iako će odredbe o transparentnosti zarada i roditeljskom odsustvu pomoći u privlačenju i zadržavanju radnika u sektoru koji pati od akutnog nedostatka radne snage, reforme propuštaju najhitniju potrebu sektora: pravni okvir za stalne sezonske radnike. Ministarstvo turizma preporučuje da se ove izmjene dopune ciljanim zakonodavstvom o sezonskom zapošljavanju i podrškom za usklađivanje malih turističkih preduzeća.
key_concerns_mne: Izmjene ne rješavaju najhitnije pitanje radne snage u turizmu: odsustvo pravnog okvira za stalne sezonske radnike, što je godinama blokirano uprkos širokom političkom konsenzusu
Prag od 100 zaposlenih za izvještavanje o razlici u platama između polova neće obuhvatiti veliku većinu turističkih preduzeća, koja su mala i srednja, čime se ograničava uticaj ove odredbe na sektor

Obaveze porodiljskog i roditeljskog odsustva tokom glavne sezone od maja do oktobra mogle bi pogoršati postojeće kadrovske probleme u malim obalskim turističkim objektima i turističkim preduzećima u nacionalnim parkovima
Povećanje minimalne zarade indeksirano kupovnom moći moglo bi stisnuti marže novoosnovanih turističkih preduzeća na sjeveru Crne Gore, gdje se sektor još uvijek razvija

Nije najavljeno nikakvo prateće rješenje za usklađivanje niti prelazni period za mala turistička preduzeća kojima nedostaju kadrovske kapacitete za sprovođenje zahtjeva transparentnosti zarada i izvještavanja
Reforme ne adresiraju sivu ekonomiju u sezonskom turističkom zapošljavanju, gdje neformalni aranžmani ostaju rasprostranjeni i gdje su zaštita na radu i radno pravo praktično nesprovodivi
recommendations_mne: Hitno finalizovati i usvojiti Zakon o stalnim sezonskim radnicima kao prateću mjeru, uključujući digitalnu registraciju, cjelogodišnje pokriće socijalnim osiguranjem i mehanizme za suzbijanje sive ekonomije

Uvesti fazni vremenski okvir za usklađivanje turističkih MSP-a (ispod 50 zaposlenih), uz besplatne smjernice i pojednostavljene obrasce za izvještavanje, izrađene u saradnji Ministarstva turizma i Inspektorata rada
Sniziti prag za izvještavanje o razlici u platama između polova na 50 zaposlenih u roku od tri godine, uz sektorski specifične referentne vrijednosti za ugostiteljstvo i turizam

Uspostaviti mehanizam za vanredne kadrovske situacije tokom sezone -- kao što su zajednički fondovi radne snage ili aranžmani međupreduzetničkog posuđivanja radnika -- kako bi se ublažile smetnje tokom glavne sezone usljed korišćenja roditeljskog odsustva

Pilot-projekat za zahtjeve transparentnosti zarada sprovesti prvo u sjevernim turističkim opštinama, koristeći podatke za osmišljavanje ciljanih podsticaja za poslodavce koji nude plate iznad minimuma u nerazvijenim turističkim regijama

Uskladiti poštovanje radnog zakonodavstva sa turističkom sertifikacijom kvaliteta i licenciranjem, kako bi hoteli i turistički operateri koji ispunjavaju nove standarde imali prednost u promociji destinacije i javnim nabavkama

assessments.summary: The Labour Law amendments are a welcome step toward EU alignment under Chapter 19 and will benefit the Ministry of Interior's workforce, including police officers, border police, and civil protection staff. However, the Ministry has significant operational concerns about the impact of new leave entitlements on an already chronically understaffed police force, and about the administrative burden of gender pay gap reporting within a large, complex security apparatus. The reforms are sound in principle but require tailored implementation planning for security sector institutions to avoid degrading operational readiness.
assessments.executive_summary: The Ministry of Interior views these Labour Law amendments positively as necessary EU alignment under Chapter 19, but flags serious operational risks for an already understaffed police force where the authorized strength of 5,376 officers remains far from filled. The top concern is that new paternity and parental leave entitlements, without accompanying workforce planning or temporary replacement mechanisms, could further strain critical units including border police and organized crime investigators. We recommend the Government adopt a security-sector implementation protocol that pairs these new rights with accelerated police recruitment and shift-coverage contingency plans.
assessments.reasoning:
From the Ministry of Interior's perspective, these Labour Law amendments must be evaluated through the lens of their impact on Montenegro's security apparatus -- a major public sector employer with unique operational demands.

POSITIVE DIMENSIONS: The amendments advance Montenegro's EU accession under Chapter 19 (Social Policy and Employment), which is a closing benchmark requirement. The Ministry of Interior supports all reforms that bring Montenegro closer to EU membership, as this also strengthens the framework for Chapter 24 (Justice, Freedom, and Security) cooperation. Wage transparency and anti-retaliation protections for discussing pay are progressive measures that could improve morale across public sector institutions, including within the police. The introduction of paternity leave and flexible working arrangements reflects modern employment standards and can improve workforce retention -- a critical concern given that the Police Directorate struggles to attract and retain qualified officers.

OPERATIONAL READINESS CONCERNS: The Police Directorate's authorized establishment is 5,376 officers, but actual staffing is considerably lower, with some departments chronically understaffed (as documented in the Ministry's own organizational bylaws and confirmed by European Commission reports). The European Commission's 2025 Montenegro Report specifically flagged the need to fill vacancies in the Special Police Unit and other critical formations. Introducing 10 days of paternity leave plus a non-transferable two-month parental leave period, while fully justified from a social policy standpoint, will create temporary absences that must be absorbed by units already operating below capacity. This is particularly acute for: (a) border police units managing Montenegro's external borders as part of Schengen preparation, where 24/7 coverage is mandatory; (b) organized crime investigation teams where case continuity is essential; (c) civil protection units that must maintain disaster response readiness; and (d) the International Operational Room / 24/7 Single Point of Contact (SPOC) established in April 2025 per EU requirements.

ADMINISTRATIVE BURDEN: The gender pay gap reporting requirement for organizations with 100+ employees will apply to the Ministry of Interior and the Police Directorate. While the Ministry supports transparency, implementing this within the complex pay structure of the security sector -- where allowances for hazardous duty, night shifts, border postings, and specialized units create legitimate pay differentials -- will require careful methodology. The 5% threshold triggering mandatory corrective measures could generate false positives if the reporting framework does not adequately account for security-sector-specific compensation structures. The Ministry's human resources and administrative capacity is already stretched thin managing ongoing police reform and recruitment drives.

FLEXIBLE WORK ARRANGEMENTS: The provisions allowing parents and caregivers to request flexible hours, reduced workdays, or remote work are straightforward to implement in administrative roles but fundamentally incompatible with operational policing. The law needs clear guidance on how security sector employers handle such requests without undermining operational capacity. While the law states employees cannot face consequences for declining remote work, the inverse -- officers requesting remote work in roles that require physical presence -- could create friction if not properly regulated.

RECRUITMENT AND RETENTION IMPLICATIONS: On the positive side, improved working conditions could make the Ministry of Interior a more attractive employer, potentially helping address chronic understaffing. Younger officers considering starting families may be more likely to stay in service if paternity leave and flexible arrangements are available. This could partially offset the operational strain. The wage transparency provisions, requiring salary disclosure in job postings, should also help the Ministry compete more effectively for talent by making its compensation packages visible upfront.

EU ALIGNMENT ASSESSMENT: These reforms are clearly driven by Chapter 19 closing benchmark requirements and align with EU Directive 2019/1158 on work-life balance and Directive 2023/970 on pay transparency. The Ministry recognizes this as essential for the accession process. However, the Government should have consulted more thoroughly with security sector stakeholders about implementation timelines and transitional measures.
key_concerns: The Police Directorate is already chronically understaffed well below its authorized strength of 5,376 officers, and new leave entitlements will create additional temporary absences without any accompanying workforce replacement mechanism
Border police units preparing for Schengen require uninterrupted 24/7 coverage, and paternity/parental leave absences in these units could compromise border management obligations

The 5% gender pay gap threshold triggering mandatory corrective measures may produce misleading results in the security sector, where legitimate pay differentials exist for hazardous duty, night shifts, and specialized postings
Administrative capacity within MoI human resources is already strained by ongoing police reform, and adding gender pay gap reporting obligations increases the burden without additional resources
Flexible working arrangements (remote work, reduced hours) are incompatible with most operational policing roles, and the law lacks clear guidance for security sector implementation
No transitional period or phased implementation is specified for security sector institutions to adapt staffing and scheduling systems
recommendations: Adopt a security-sector implementation protocol with a phased timeline, giving the Ministry of Interior 12 months to adapt internal regulations, shift schedules, and HR systems before full enforcement
Accelerate police recruitment to reduce the gap between actual and authorized staffing levels, ensuring that leave-related absences do not degrade operational capacity in critical units

Develop sector-specific guidelines for gender pay gap reporting that account for legitimate security sector pay differentials (hazardous duty, night shifts, border postings) to avoid false positives under the 5% threshold

Create a temporary duty assignment mechanism allowing officers from less operationally critical units to backfill positions in border police, organized crime units, and civil protection during parental leave absences

Clarify in implementing regulations how flexible work requests from security sector employees in operational roles will be handled, with explicit criteria for justified refusal based on operational necessity

Allocate additional HR administrative positions within the Ministry of Interior to handle the new reporting and compliance obligations without diverting resources from police reform and Schengen preparation

counter_proposal.title: Security-Sector Adaptive Implementation Framework
counter_proposal.summary: The Ministry of Interior proposes that the Labour Law amendments be accompanied by a dedicated security-sector implementation regulation that phases in leave entitlements alongside accelerated recruitment, establishes a temporary duty backfill system for operational units, and creates tailored pay gap reporting methodology for institutions with complex duty-based compensation structures. This would preserve the social policy gains while safeguarding operational readiness.
key_changes: 12-month transitional period for security sector institutions before full enforcement of new leave provisions
Mandatory operational readiness assessment before approving leave in understaffed critical units (border police, organized crime, civil protection)
Cross-unit temporary duty assignment pool to maintain minimum staffing levels during leave absences
Tailored gender pay gap reporting methodology for the security sector that separates base pay analysis from duty-specific allowances
Dedicated HR capacity increase of at least 10 positions within MoI to manage compliance without diverting from police reform priorities
Quarterly reporting mechanism to Government on operational impact of new leave provisions during the first two years
expected_benefits: Maintains full operational capacity of border police during Schengen preparation phase
Prevents disruption to ongoing organized crime investigations and prosecutions required under Chapter 24 benchmarks
Ensures accurate gender pay gap reporting that reflects genuine inequities rather than duty-based differentials
Creates a replicable model for other security sector employers (intelligence agency, military) facing similar challenges
Balances workers' rights with public safety obligations, demonstrating that EU social standards and security readiness are not mutually exclusive
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High. A security-sector implementation protocol is a standard approach across EU member states. Many EU countries (e.g., Germany, France, Croatia) maintain sector-specific regulations for police and military that adapt general labour law provisions to operational realities while fully complying with EU directives. The European Commission recognizes that operational services require tailored implementation and this would not conflict with Chapter 19 benchmarks.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Okvir za Adaptivnu Implementaciju u Sektoru Bezbjednosti
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Ministarstvo unutrašnjih poslova predlaže da izmjene Zakona o radu budu praćene posebnom uredbom o implementaciji za sektor bezbjednosti, koja bi postepeno uvodila prava na odsustvo uporedo s ubrzanim zapošljavanjem, uspostavila privremeni sistem popune dužnosti za operativne jedinice i kreirala prilagođenu metodologiju izvještavanja o razlici u platama za institucije sa složenim strukturama naknada zasnovanim na dužnostima. Na taj način bi se očuvali socijalno-politički rezultati uz istovremeno čuvanje operativne spremnosti.
key_changes_mne: Prelazni period od 12 mjeseci za institucije sektora bezbjednosti prije pune primjene novih odredbi o odsustvu
Obavezna procjena operativne spremnosti prije odobravanja odsustva u kritičnim jedinicama s nedovoljnim brojem osoblja (granična policija, organizovani kriminal, civilna zaštita)
Zajednički fond za privremeno raspoređivanje osoblja između jedinica radi održavanja minimalnog broja zaposlenih tokom odsustva
Prilagođena metodologija izvještavanja o rodnom jazu u platama za sektor bezbjednosti koja odvaja analizu osnovne plate od naknada vezanih za specifične dužnosti
Namjensko povećanje kadrovskih kapaciteta za najmanje 10 mjesta unutar MUP-a radi upravljanja usklađenošću bez preusmjeravanja s prioriteta policijske reforme
Kvartalni mehanizam izvještavanja Vlade o operativnom uticaju novih odredbi o odsustvu tokom prve dvije godine
expected_benefits_mne: Održava punu operativnu kapacitet granične policije tokom faze pripreme za Šengen
Sprečava poremećaje u tekućim istragama i krivičnim gonjenima organizovanog kriminala koji se zahtijevaju prema mjerilima Poglavlja 24
Osigurava tačno izvještavanje o rodnom jazu u platama koje odražava stvarne nejednakosti, a ne razlike zasnovane na prirodi dužnosti
Stvara model koji se može primijeniti i kod drugih poslodavaca u sektoru bezbjednosti (obavještajne agencije, vojska) koji se suočavaju sa sličnim izazovima
Usklađuje radnička prava s obavezama javne bezbjednosti, dokazujući da se EU socijalni standardi i operativna spremnost ne isključuju međusobno
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka. Protokol o implementaciji za sektor bezbjednosti standardan je pristup u državama članicama EU. Mnoge zemlje EU (npr. Njemačka, Francuska, Hrvatska) zadržavaju sektorski specifične propise za policiju i vojsku koji prilagođavaju opšte odredbe radnog prava operativnim realnostima, uz potpunu usklađenost s direktivama EU. Evropska komisija prepoznaje da operativne službe zahtijevaju prilagođenu implementaciju, te ovo ne bi bilo u suprotnosti s mjerilima Poglavlja 19.

assessments.summary_mne: Izmjene Zakona o radu predstavljaju dobrodošao korak ka usklađivanju s EU u okviru Poglavlja 19 i donijeće korist zaposlenima u Ministarstvu unutrašnjih poslova, uključujući policijske službenike, graničnu policiju i osoblje civilne zaštite. Međutim, Ministarstvo ima ozbiljne operativne zabrinutosti u pogledu uticaja novih prava na odsustvo na ionako hronično nepopunjenu policijsku silu, kao i u pogledu administrativnog opterećenja koje donosi izvještavanje o rodnom jazu u platama unutar velikog i složenog bezbjednosnog aparata. Reforme su načelno ispravne, ali zahtijevaju prilagođeno planiranje implementacije za institucije bezbjednosnog sektora kako bi se izbjeglo narušavanje operativne spremnosti.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Ministarstvo unutrašnjih poslova gleda pozitivno na ove izmjene Zakona o radu kao na neophodnu usklađenost s EU u okviru Poglavlja 19, ali ukazuje na ozbiljne operativne rizike za ionako nepopunjenu policijsku silu u kojoj je ovlašćena snaga od 5.376 službenika daleko od popunjenosti. Ključna zabrinutost odnosi se na to da nova prava na očinsko i roditeljsko odsustvo, bez pratećeg planiranja radne snage ili mehanizama privremene zamjene, mogu dodatno opteretiti kritične jedinice, uključujući graničnu policiju i istražitelje organizovanog kriminala. Preporučujemo Vladi da usvoji protokol implementacije za bezbjednosni sektor koji ova nova prava poveže s ubrzanim zapošljavanjem u policiji i planovima za smjensko pokriće.
key_concerns_mne: Policijska uprava je već hronično nepopunjena, daleko ispod ovlašćene snage od 5.376 službenika, a nova prava na odsustvo stvoriće dodatna privremena odsustva bez ikakvih pratenameć mehanizama za popunu radnih mjesta

Jedinice granične policije koje se pripremaju za Šengen zahtijevaju neprekidno pokriće 24/7, a odsustva zbog očinskog/roditeljskog odsustva u tim jedinicama mogla bi ugroziti obaveze upravljanja granicom

Prag rodnog jaza u platama od 5% koji aktivira obavezne korektivne mjere može davati pogrešne rezultate u bezbjednosnom sektoru, gdje postoje opravdane razlike u platama za rad na opasnim dužnostima, noćne smjene i specijalizovana raspoređivanja

Administrativni kapaciteti unutar sektora za ljudske resurse MUP-a već su opterećeni tekućom policijskom reformom, a dodavanje obaveza izvještavanja o rodnom jazu u platama povećava teret bez dodatnih resursa
Fleksibilni radni aranžmani (rad na daljinu, skraćeno radno vrijeme) nespojivi su s većinom operativnih policijskih uloga, a zakon ne sadrži jasne smjernice za implementaciju u bezbjednosnom sektoru
Nije predviđen prelazni period niti fazna implementacija za institucije bezbjednosnog sektora radi prilagođavanja sistema popune i raspoređivanja kadra
recommendations_mne: Usvojiti protokol implementacije za bezbjednosni sektor s faznim vremenskim planom, dajući Ministarstvu unutrašnjih poslova 12 mjeseci da prilagodi interne propise, smjenski raspored i sisteme upravljanja ljudskim resursima prije potpune primjene

Ubrzati zapošljavanje u policiji radi smanjenja jaza između stvarnog i ovlašćenog broja službenika, osiguravajući da odsustva po osnovu korišćenja prava ne umanjuju operativni kapacitet u kritičnim jedinicama

Razviti sektorski specifične smjernice za izvještavanje o rodnom jazu u platama koje uzimaju u obzir opravdane razlike u platama u bezbjednosnom sektoru (rad na opasnim dužnostima, noćne smjene, granična raspoređivanja) kako bi se izbjegli lažno pozitivni rezultati ispod praga od 5%

Uspostaviti mehanizam privremenog raspoređivanja koji omogućava službenicima iz operativno manje kritičnih jedinica da popune pozicije u graničnoj policiji, jedinicama za organizovani kriminal i civilnoj zaštiti tokom roditeljskih odsustva

Pojašnjiti provedbenim propisima kako će se postupati sa zahtjevima za fleksibilan rad zaposlenih u bezbjednosnom sektoru na operativnim radnim mjestima, uz eksplicitne kriterijume za opravdano odbijanje na osnovu operativne neophodnosti

Obezbijediti dodatna administrativna radna mjesta za ljudske resurse unutar Ministarstva unutrašnjih poslova radi ispunjavanja novih obaveza izvještavanja i usklađenosti bez preusmjeravanja resursa od policijske reforme i priprema za Šengen

assessments.summary: The Labour Law amendments represent a constitutionally sound and legally progressive step that advances Montenegro's alignment with the EU acquis, particularly EU Directive 2023/970 on Pay Transparency and Directive 2019/1158 on Work-Life Balance. The introduction of paternity leave, wage transparency, gender pay gap reporting, and purchasing power-linked minimum wages strengthens fundamental rights protections under the Montenegrin Constitution and addresses long-standing structural discrimination documented by the ILO (21.6% gender pay gap). However, the Ministry of Justice identifies significant enforcement risks: Montenegro has only 36 labour inspectors (1.28 per 10,000 workers), the informal economy remains at approximately 20.6% of GDP, and penalties for labour law violations are rarely applied. Without robust enforcement mechanisms, judicial remedies, and institutional capacity-building, these progressive legal provisions risk remaining aspirational rather than operational.
assessments.executive_summary: The Labour Law amendments are a positive, constitutionally grounded reform that meaningfully advances EU accession under Chapter 19 and strengthens workers' fundamental rights. The central concern is that Montenegro's chronically under-resourced labour inspectorate and weak enforcement culture could render these provisions unenforceable in practice. The Ministry of Justice recommends establishing dedicated judicial and enforcement mechanisms, including accessible complaint procedures with burden-of-proof reversal, before these amendments take effect.
assessments.reasoning:
CONSTITUTIONAL COMPLIANCE: The amendments are well-grounded in the Montenegrin constitutional framework. The Constitution guarantees equality (Article 8), prohibits discrimination (Article 7), and protects workers' rights including the right to work and just working conditions (Articles 62-67). The gender equality provisions align with the constitutional mandate that the state shall guarantee equality of women and men and develop policies of equal opportunities. The paternity leave provisions reinforce parental rights and the protection of the family. There is no constitutional tension with any of the proposed measures.

HUMAN RIGHTS ADVANCEMENT: From a human rights perspective, these amendments address documented structural discrimination in the Montenegrin labour market. According to ILO data, women in Montenegro earn on average 21.6% less than men -- a gap that cannot be explained by differences in observable characteristics but has a significant structural component driven by discrimination. Montenegro's Gender Equality Index scores only 61.9 in the "Money" domain compared to the EU average of 82.6. The wage transparency and gender pay gap reporting provisions directly target these inequities. The prohibition on asking about previous salary is particularly significant, as salary history perpetuates discriminatory pay patterns from previous employment. The 5% threshold with mandatory corrective measures provides a concrete, enforceable standard.

EU LEGAL HARMONIZATION: The amendments demonstrate strong alignment with the EU acquis, directly addressing closing benchmarks for Chapter 19 (Social Policy and Employment). Specifically, the wage transparency provisions transpose key elements of EU Directive 2023/970 (Pay Transparency Directive), which EU Member States must implement by June 2026 -- meaning Montenegro is adopting these standards contemporaneously with EU members. The paternity leave and flexible working arrangements align with Directive 2019/1158 on Work-Life Balance for Parents and Carers. The 100-employee threshold for gender pay gap reporting mirrors the EU Directive's approach. This is strategically important as the European Commission has recently noted Montenegro's progress on Chapter 19 at the 16th Subcommittee meeting on Innovation, Human Resources, Information Society and Social Policy.

RULE OF LAW CONCERNS -- ENFORCEMENT GAP: The most significant concern from a justice perspective is the enforcement gap. Montenegro has only 36 labour inspectors and 10 occupational health and safety inspectors, amounting to 1.28 labour inspectors per 10,000 workers. The US State Department and international observers have noted that the government does not have sufficient resources to enforce existing labour laws, that penalties are rarely applied, and that inspectors rarely exercise their authority for unannounced inspections or sanctions. Adding new obligations (pay transparency compliance, gender pay gap reporting, corrective measures) to an already overstretched enforcement system risks creating law on paper that cannot be upheld in practice. This undermines the rule of law principle that laws must be effectively enforced to be meaningful.

JUDICIAL ACCESS AND REMEDIES: The amendments as described do not appear to include specific provisions for judicial remedies for workers whose rights are violated. The EU Pay Transparency Directive 2023/970 requires Member States to provide judicial procedures, shift the burden of proof to employers in pay discrimination cases, and ensure access to compensation including full back pay. If these elements are not included in the amendments, this represents a significant gap in transposition. Workers must have accessible, affordable, and timely judicial channels to challenge violations. The Women's Rights Center (Centar za zenska prava) has rightly noted that reforms must be both fair and applicable, and that institutional infrastructure must be strengthened to support implementation.

ANTI-CORRUPTION DIMENSION: Wage transparency has an indirect but important anti-corruption benefit. Opaque pay structures in both public and private sectors can mask nepotistic hiring and politically motivated salary differentials. Requiring salary disclosure in job postings and enabling workers to access average wage data for equivalent roles increases accountability and reduces the scope for patronage-based employment practices, which remain a concern in Montenegro's labour market.

INFORMAL ECONOMY CHALLENGE: With the informal economy estimated at 20.6% of GDP (Ministry of Finance figures), a substantial proportion of Montenegrin workers will fall entirely outside the scope of these protections. Workers in the informal sector -- disproportionately women, migrants, and vulnerable groups -- will not benefit from wage transparency, paternity leave, or pay gap reporting requirements. The amendments do not appear to address this structural exclusion.

PURCHASING POWER AND MINIMUM WAGE: Linking minimum wage to purchasing power and establishing a two-year review cycle is a sound approach that addresses the cost-of-living pressures facing Montenegrin workers. However, the legal criteria for determining purchasing power should be transparent and clearly defined to avoid political manipulation of what should be an evidence-based calculation.
key_concerns: Enforcement capacity is critically insufficient: Montenegro has only 36 labour inspectors (1.28 per 10,000 workers), and existing labour law penalties are rarely applied, raising serious doubts about whether new obligations on wage transparency and gender pay gap reporting can be effectively monitored and enforced.

The amendments may lack adequate judicial remedy provisions: the EU Pay Transparency Directive requires burden-of-proof reversal, access to compensation including full back pay, and judicial procedures for pay discrimination claims -- if these are not included, transposition is incomplete and workers lack effective legal recourse.

Approximately 20.6% of GDP operates in the informal economy, meaning a significant proportion of workers (disproportionately women and vulnerable groups) will fall entirely outside the scope of these protections, creating a two-tier system of labour rights.

The Women's Rights Center has flagged that reform implementation must be matched by institutional infrastructure and capacity -- without adequate funding for enforcement bodies, training for judges and inspectors, and public awareness campaigns, the law risks being aspirational rather than effective.

Retaliation protections for workers who discuss pay or file complaints need robust enforcement mechanisms, as fear of employer retaliation is a well-documented barrier to exercising transparency rights, particularly in Montenegro's small and interconnected labour market.
recommendations: Establish a dedicated labour rights enforcement unit within the inspectorate, with at least 20 additional inspectors specifically trained in wage transparency verification and gender pay gap auditing, funded through the EU accession support instruments (IPA III).

Amend the law to explicitly include burden-of-proof reversal in pay discrimination cases, in line with Article 18 of EU Directive 2023/970, so that once a worker establishes a prima facie case of pay discrimination, the employer must prove that no violation occurred.

Create an accessible, low-cost complaint mechanism for workers, potentially housed within the Ombudsman's office or a dedicated equality body, with clear timelines for investigation and resolution -- ensuring that judicial remedies are not the only recourse.

Include a mandatory 18-month implementation period with phased enforcement milestones, beginning with public sector employers and state-owned enterprises before extending to private sector entities, accompanied by a public awareness campaign.
Develop specific provisions addressing informal economy workers, including incentives for formalization and extending basic wage transparency protections to non-standard employment arrangements.

Require that the purchasing power criterion for minimum wage determination be calculated using a transparent, publicly available methodology developed in consultation with MONSTAT and verified by independent economic experts.

Mandate judicial training programs on pay transparency law and gender discrimination adjudication, ensuring that courts are prepared to handle the new category of claims these amendments will generate.

counter_proposal.title: Enforcement-First Labour Rights Reform
counter_proposal.summary: The Ministry of Justice would pursue the same substantive reforms but sequence them differently: first building enforcement infrastructure and judicial capacity, then phasing in new obligations with clear implementation timelines. This approach prioritizes the rule of law principle that rights without remedies are meaningless. The counter-proposal would also expand scope to include informal economy protections and explicit judicial remedy provisions mirroring the full EU Pay Transparency Directive.
key_changes: Phase 1 (months 1-6): Recruit and train 20 additional labour inspectors specializing in wage transparency and gender pay auditing; establish digital reporting platform for gender pay gap data
Phase 2 (months 7-12): Implement wage transparency and paternity leave provisions for public sector and state-owned enterprises as a pilot; launch public awareness campaign
Phase 3 (months 13-18): Extend all provisions to private sector employers with 100+ employees; activate gender pay gap reporting requirements
Include explicit burden-of-proof reversal provisions for pay discrimination claims, mirroring Article 18 of EU Directive 2023/970
Establish a fast-track mediation and adjudication mechanism within the existing court system for wage transparency and pay discrimination disputes
Add provisions for informal economy workers, including portable benefit schemes and extending non-discrimination protections to non-standard work
expected_benefits: Higher compliance rates from day one due to adequate enforcement infrastructure being in place before obligations take effect

Stronger EU accession positioning by demonstrating not just legislative alignment but implementation capacity -- directly addressing the Chapter 19 closing benchmark requirement for adequate administrative structures and enforcement capacity
More complete transposition of EU Directive 2023/970, including judicial remedy provisions that the current amendments appear to omit
Reduced risk of the law becoming dead letter due to enforcement failures, which would undermine public trust in the legal system
Inclusion of informal economy workers addresses a structural gap that affects the most vulnerable segments of the workforce
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High feasibility. The phased approach actually reduces implementation risk by allowing institutional learning and adjustment. The additional inspectors can be funded through IPA III pre-accession assistance, which already earmarks funds for Chapter 19 capacity-building. The judicial training component leverages existing HELP (Human Rights Education for Legal Professionals) programs of the Council of Europe. The main political obstacle would be the delayed effective date for private sector obligations, but this is standard practice in EU directive transposition and can be presented as responsible governance.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Reforma radnih prava sa prioritetom izvršavanja
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Ministarstvo pravde bi sprovodilo iste suštinske reforme, ali u drugačijem redoslijedu: najprije bi izgradilo infrastrukturu za izvršavanje i sudski kapacitet, a zatim postepeno uvodilo nove obaveze s jasnim rokovima implementacije. Ovaj pristup daje prioritet načelu vladavine prava, prema kojem prava bez pravnih sredstava zaštite nemaju smisla. Kontra-prijedlog bi takođe proširio obuhvat na zaštitu radnika u neformalnoj ekonomiji i na izričite odredbe o sudskoj zaštiti, ogledajući se u potpunoj EU Direktivi o transparentnosti plata.
key_changes_mne: Faza 1 (mjeseci 1–6): Regrutovanje i obuka 20 dodatnih inspektora rada specijalizovanih za transparentnost plata i reviziju rodnog jaza u platama; uspostavljanje digitalne platforme za izvještavanje o podacima o rodnom jazu u platama

Faza 2 (mjeseci 7–12): Implementacija odredbi o transparentnosti plata i porodiljskom odsustvu za očeve u javnom sektoru i državnim preduzećima u okviru pilot-projekta; pokretanje javne kampanje podizanja svijesti
Faza 3 (mjeseci 13–18): Proširenje svih odredbi na poslodavce u privatnom sektoru s 100 ili više zaposlenih; aktiviranje zahtjeva za izvještavanje o rodnom jazu u platama
Uključivanje izričitih odredbi o prenosu tereta dokazivanja za tužbe zbog diskriminacije u platama, po uzoru na član 18 EU Direktive 2023/970
Uspostavljanje mehanizma brzog posredovanja i suđenja unutar postojećeg sudskog sistema za sporove u vezi s transparentnošću plata i diskriminacijom u platama
Dodavanje odredbi za radnike u neformalnoj ekonomiji, uključujući prenosive sisteme beneficija i proširenje zaštite od diskriminacije na nestandardne oblike rada
expected_benefits_mne: Viši stepeni usklađenosti od prvog dana, zahvaljujući adekvatnoj infrastrukturi za izvršavanje koja je uspostavljena prije nego što obaveze stupe na snagu

Jača pozicija u procesu pristupanja EU, demonstriranjem ne samo zakonodavne usklađenosti, već i kapaciteta za implementaciju — što direktno odgovara zahtjevu završnog mjerila iz Poglavlja 19 u pogledu odgovarajućih administrativnih struktura i kapaciteta za izvršavanje
Potpunije prenošenje EU Direktive 2023/970, uključujući odredbe o sudskoj zaštiti koje, čini se, nedostaju u trenutnim izmjenama i dopunama
Smanjen rizik od toga da zakon ostane mrtvo slovo zbog neuspjeha u izvršavanju, što bi narušilo povjerenje javnosti u pravni sistem
Uključivanje radnika iz neformalne ekonomije otklanja strukturni nedostatak koji pogađa najranjivije segmente radne snage
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka izvodljivost. Fazni pristup zapravo smanjuje rizik od implementacije, jer omogućava institucionalno učenje i prilagođavanje. Dodatni inspektori mogu se finansirati kroz IPA III pretpristupnu pomoć, koja već predviđa sredstva za izgradnju kapaciteta u okviru Poglavlja 19. Komponenta obuke sudija koristi prednosti postojećih programa HELP (Obrazovanje o ljudskim pravima za pravne stručnjake) Savjeta Evrope. Glavna politička prepreka bila bi odloženi datum stupanja na snagu obaveza privatnog sektora, ali to je standardna praksa u prenošenju direktiva EU i može se predstaviti kao odgovorno upravljanje.

assessments.summary_mne: Izmjene Zakona o radu predstavljaju ustavno utemeljeni i pravno napredni korak koji unapređuje usklađenost Crne Gore sa pravnom tekovinom EU, a posebno sa Direktivom EU 2023/970 o transparentnosti plata i Direktivom 2019/1158 o ravnoteži između poslovnog i privatnog života. Uvođenje očinskog odsustva, transparentnosti zarada, izvještavanja o rodnom jazu u platama i minimalne zarade vezane za kupovnu moć jača zaštitu osnovnih prava garantovanih Ustavom Crne Gore i odgovara na dugogodišnju strukturnu diskriminaciju dokumentovanu od strane MOR-a (rodni jaz u platama od 21,6%). Međutim, Ministarstvo pravde identifikuje značajne rizike u pogledu sprovođenja: Crna Gora ima svega 36 inspektora rada (1,28 na 10.000 radnika), neformalna ekonomija i dalje čini oko 20,6% BDP-a, a kazne za povrede radnog prava rijetko se primjenjuju. Bez robusnih mehanizama sprovođenja, sudskih pravnih lijekova i jačanja institucionalnih kapaciteta, postoji ozbiljan rizik da ove napredne zakonske odredbe ostanu na nivou aspiracije, a ne stvarne primjene.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Izmjene Zakona o radu predstavljaju pozitivnu, ustavno utemeljenu reformu koja suštinski unapređuje proces pristupanja EU u okviru Poglavlja 19 i jača osnovna prava radnika. Centralna zabrinutost jeste da bi hronično nedovoljno resursima opremljeni Inspektorat rada u Crnoj Gori i slaba kultura sprovođenja mogli učiniti ove odredbe neprimjenljivim u praksi. Ministarstvo pravde preporučuje uspostavljanje namjenskih sudskih i mehanizama sprovođenja, uključujući dostupne postupke podnošenja pritužbi sa preokretom tereta dokazivanja, i to prije nego što ove izmjene stupe na snagu.
key_concerns_mne: Kapaciteti za sprovođenje su kritično nedovoljni: Crna Gora ima svega 36 inspektora rada (1,28 na 10.000 radnika), a postojeće kazne predviđene radnim zakonodavstvom se rijetko primjenjuju, što ozbiljno dovodi u pitanje mogućnost efikasnog nadzora i sprovođenja novih obaveza u pogledu transparentnosti zarada i izvještavanja o rodnom jazu u platama.

Izmjenama možda nedostaju adekvatne odredbe o sudskim pravnim lijekovima: Direktiva EU o transparentnosti plata zahtijeva preokret tereta dokazivanja, pristup naknadi uključujući isplatu cjelokupnog duga po osnovu zarade i sudske postupke za tužbe zbog diskriminacije u pogledu plata — ukoliko ovi elementi nisu uključeni, transpozicija je nepotpuna i radnici nemaju djelotvoran pravni lijek.

Oko 20,6% BDP-a ostvaruje se u neformalnoj ekonomiji, što znači da će znatan dio radnika — nesrazmjerno žene i ranjive grupe — biti potpuno izvan domašaja ovih zaštitnih mjera, čime se stvara sistem radnih prava sa dva standarda.

Centar za ženska prava ukazao je da sprovođenje reformi mora biti praćeno izgradnjom institucionalne infrastrukture i kapaciteta — bez adekvatnog finansiranja tijela nadležnih za sprovođenje, obuke sudija i inspektora te kampanja podizanja javne svijesti, zakon riskirа da ostane na nivou deklarativnosti, a ne stvarne efikasnosti.

Zaštita od odmazde za radnike koji razgovaraju o platama ili podnose pritužbe zahtijeva robusne mehanizme sprovođenja, budući da je strah od odmazde od strane poslodavca dobro dokumentovana prepreka ostvarivanju prava na transparentnost, posebno na crnogorskom malom i međusobno povezanom tržištu rada.
recommendations_mne: Uspostaviti namjensku jedinicu za zaštitu prava na radu u okviru Inspektorata, sa najmanje 20 dodatnih inspektora posebno obučenih za provjeru transparentnosti zarada i reviziju rodnog jaza u platama, finansiranu kroz instrumente podrške pridruživanju EU (IPA III).

Zakonom izričito predvidjeti preokret tereta dokazivanja u slučajevima diskriminacije u pogledu plata, u skladu sa članom 18 Direktive EU 2023/970, tako da nakon što radnik utvrdi osnovanost tužbe zbog diskriminacije u platama, poslodavac mora dokazati da nije došlo do povrede.

Uspostaviti dostupan i jeftin mehanizam za podnošenje pritužbi radnika, potencijalno u okviru Institucije Zaštitnika/zaštitnice ljudskih prava i sloboda ili namjenskog tijela za ravnopravnost, sa jasnim rokovima za istragu i rješavanje — kako bi se osiguralo da sudski pravni lijekovi nisu jedini put do zaštite.

Uvesti obavezan period implementacije od 18 mjeseci sa faznim rokovima za sprovođenje, počevši od poslodavaca u javnom sektoru i državnih preduzeća, a zatim proširujući na subjekte privatnog sektora, uz sprovođenje kampanje podizanja javne svijesti.

Razviti posebne odredbe za radnike u neformalnoj ekonomiji, uključujući podsticaje za formalizaciju i proširenje osnovnih zaštitnih mjera u pogledu transparentnosti zarada na nestandardne oblike zapošljavanja.

Obavezati da se kriterijum kupovne moći za utvrđivanje minimalne zarade izračunava primjenom transparentne metodologije dostupne javnosti, razvijene u konsultaciji sa MONSTAT-om i verifikovane od strane nezavisnih ekonomskih stručnjaka.

Uvesti obavezne programe obuke za sudije iz oblasti prava o transparentnosti plata i odlučivanja u slučajevima rodne diskriminacije, kako bi sudovi bili pripremljeni za rješavanje nove kategorije tužbi koje će ove izmjene generisati.

assessments.summary: The Labour Law amendments represent one of the most consequential legislative steps Montenegro has taken toward meeting Chapter 19 (Social Policy and Employment) closing benchmarks. By transposing core provisions of EU Directive 2019/1158 on work-life balance and anticipating the EU Pay Transparency Directive 2023/970, Montenegro demonstrates serious legislative alignment with the acquis communautaire. The 10-day paternity leave matches the EU minimum exactly, the 5% gender pay gap threshold mirrors the EU directive's trigger for corrective action, and the wage transparency provisions address a documented 21.6% gender pay gap that the ILO and European Commission have flagged as problematic. This decision directly strengthens Montenegro's negotiating position on Chapter 19 and signals to the European Commission that the government is committed to the ambitious goal of closing all chapters by end of 2026.
assessments.executive_summary: This decision is strongly positive for EU integration, directly fulfilling Chapter 19 closing benchmarks through alignment with the Work-Life Balance Directive and Pay Transparency Directive. The main concern is whether enforcement capacity and institutional infrastructure will match the legislative ambition, as the European Commission evaluates implementation in practice, not just laws on paper.
