The Ministry recommends prioritizing the establishment of a dedicated enforcement body and extending pay gap reporting to companies with 50+ employees to fully align with the EU directive's trajectory.
assessments.reasoning:
DIRECT ACQUIS ALIGNMENT

These amendments constitute a targeted transposition of two major EU directives. First, EU Directive 2019/1158 on work-life balance for parents and carers, which requires a minimum of 10 working days of paternity leave (Article 4), at least two months of non-transferable parental leave (Article 5), the right to request flexible working arrangements for parents and carers (Article 9), and protection against adverse treatment for exercising these rights (Article 10). The Montenegrin amendments match these requirements precisely. The 10-day paternity leave corresponds exactly to the directive minimum. The two months of non-transferable parental leave replicates the directive's "use it or lose it" mechanism designed to encourage fathers' uptake. The flexible hours, reduced workdays, and remote work provisions implement Article 9. The protection against consequences for declining remote work goes beyond the directive's minimum.

Second, the amendments anticipate EU Directive 2023/970 on pay transparency, which EU member states must transpose by June 2026. This directive requires pre-employment salary disclosure, prohibits salary history inquiries, mandates gender pay gap reporting for companies with 100+ employees (initially, scaling down to 50+ by 2031), and triggers mandatory joint pay assessments when gaps exceed 5% without objective justification. Montenegro's amendments track this directive closely: salary disclosure before acceptance, prohibition on salary history questions, the 100+ employee reporting threshold, and the identical 5% corrective action trigger.

CHAPTER 19 CLOSING BENCHMARK IMPLICATIONS

Chapter 19 (Social Policy and Employment) remains among the 20 chapters still open in Montenegro's accession negotiations. The European Commission's annual progress reports and the Chapter 19 benchmarks specifically require amendment of the Labour Law to align with the acquis in labour law, health and safety, and anti-discrimination provisions. The government's own press release from the 114th session explicitly frames these amendments as fulfilling a closing benchmark under Chapter 19. This is consistent with the EU's methodology: closing benchmarks typically require demonstration of legislative alignment plus initial steps toward implementation capacity.

Given that Montenegro provisionally closed Chapter 32 (Financial Control) at the 25th Accession Conference on January 26, 2026, bringing the total to 13 of 33 chapters closed, meeting the Chapter 19 closing benchmarks is strategically important for maintaining momentum toward the government's ambitious goal of closing all chapters by end of 2026. Commissioner Kos has called Montenegro the EU's "frontrunner and best-performing candidate," and delivering on Chapter 19 benchmarks reinforces this narrative.

GENDER PAY GAP CONTEXT

The ILO's statistical update on Montenegro documented a 21.6% gender pay gap, meaning women earn approximately 0.78 euros for every euro men earn. This gap persists despite women having higher average educational qualifications and is driven largely by structural discrimination rather than differences in observable characteristics. Montenegro's Gender Equality Index score of 59.3 sits more than nine points below the EU average. The reporting and corrective action requirements in these amendments directly address a documented problem that the European Commission has flagged in successive progress reports.

IPA III FUNDING IMPLICATIONS

Montenegro has secured 80 million euros in IPA III grant funding through the Operational Programme for Employment and Social Inclusion (2024-2027). Legislative alignment with the social acquis is a prerequisite for continued IPA III disbursement under Window 4 (Competitiveness and Inclusive Growth). These amendments strengthen Montenegro's position for drawing down remaining IPA III allocations and for positioning itself favorably in the next programming cycle. They also signal readiness to absorb EU structural funds, which is relevant to Chapter 22 (Regional Policy and Coordination of Structural Instruments).

AREAS FOR CONCERN

Despite the strong legislative alignment, several concerns remain. First, the 100+ employee threshold for pay gap reporting is appropriate as a starting point, but Montenegro's economy is dominated by micro and small enterprises. According to MONSTAT data, the vast majority of Montenegrin companies have fewer than 100 employees, meaning the reporting requirement will capture only a fraction of the labour market. The EU directive itself envisions extending reporting to companies with 50+ employees by 2031, and Montenegro should plan for this trajectory now.

Second, legislative adoption without adequate enforcement infrastructure risks becoming a "paper transposition" -- a pattern the European Commission has repeatedly criticized in its progress reports on Western Balkan countries. The amendments introduce new obligations (pay gap reporting, corrective action mechanisms, salary transparency enforcement), but there is no indication of whether the Labour Inspectorate has been adequately staffed and trained to monitor compliance, whether a dedicated pay transparency monitoring body will be established, whether sanctions for non-compliance are sufficiently dissuasive, or whether data collection and reporting infrastructure exists for the pay gap reporting requirement.

Third, while the paternity leave provision is positive, 10 days represents the absolute EU minimum. Several EU member states offer significantly more (Spain offers 16 weeks, Finland 7 weeks). As Montenegro seeks to position itself as a progressive accession candidate, matching only the floor may limit the reform's practical impact on gender equality and fathers' participation in childcare.

REGIONAL COMPARISON

Among Western Balkan candidates, this puts Montenegro ahead of Serbia and Albania on pay transparency legislation, reinforcing its frontrunner status. Serbia's labour legislation lacks comparable wage transparency or gender pay gap reporting requirements. This differentiation strengthens Montenegro's narrative in the accession process and could serve as a model for regional cooperation through the Berlin Process.

IMPACT ON OTHER CHAPTERS

Beyond Chapter 19, these amendments have positive spillover effects on Chapter 23 (Judiciary and Fundamental Rights) through strengthened anti-discrimination protections, Chapter 2 (Freedom of Movement for Workers) through improved worker protection standards, Chapter 28 (Consumer and Health Protection) through worker safety provisions, and Chapter 8 (Competition Policy) through wage transparency reducing information asymmetries. The anti-retaliation provisions for workers discussing pay also touch on fundamental rights protections under Chapter 23, which together with Chapter 24 constitutes the critical "rule of law" gateway for overall accession progress.
key_concerns: Ch.19 Social Policy and Employment -- While legislative alignment is strong, there is no clear plan for building enforcement capacity at the Labour Inspectorate to monitor the new pay transparency and paternity leave obligations, risking 'paper transposition' that the European Commission regularly criticizes.

Ch.19 Social Policy and Employment -- The 100+ employee threshold for gender pay gap reporting will capture only a small fraction of Montenegro's workforce, given that the economy is dominated by micro and small enterprises. The EU directive envisions extending to 50+ employees by 2031.

Ch.19 Social Policy and Employment -- The 10-day paternity leave matches the EU Directive 2019/1158 minimum exactly, but this floor may be insufficient to drive meaningful behavioral change in fathers' childcare participation given Montenegro's traditional gender norms.

Ch.23 Judiciary and Fundamental Rights -- The anti-retaliation provisions for discussing pay are welcome but their effectiveness depends on robust whistleblower protection mechanisms, which remain underdeveloped in Montenegro per European Commission assessments.

Ch.22 Regional Policy and Coordination of Structural Instruments -- The absence of a clear data infrastructure plan for collecting and processing gender pay gap reports may create difficulties when Montenegro transitions to managing EU structural funds that require reliable labour market statistics.

Ch.19 Social Policy and Employment -- No explicit provisions for sanctions or penalties for employers who fail to comply with the new reporting obligations or who violate wage transparency requirements, which may undermine the deterrent effect of the legislation.
recommendations: Ch.19 -- Allocate dedicated budget and staffing for the Labour Inspectorate to enforce new obligations before the law takes effect, including specialized training on pay transparency auditing and gender pay gap analysis methodologies.

Ch.19 -- Adopt a phased implementation roadmap that extends gender pay gap reporting to companies with 50+ employees within 3-4 years, aligning with the EU Pay Transparency Directive 2023/970 trajectory for member states.

Ch.19 -- Increase paternity leave from 10 to at least 15 working days within 18 months, with a public awareness campaign targeting fathers, to ensure the reform drives real behavioral change rather than meeting only the EU minimum.

Ch.19 and Ch.18 (Statistics) -- Establish a centralized digital reporting platform for gender pay gap data, coordinated with MONSTAT, to build the statistical infrastructure required both for enforcement and for future EU structural fund management.

Ch.23 -- Strengthen the link between pay transparency protections and the existing whistleblower protection framework to ensure workers face no retaliation for reporting pay discrimination, as this connects to the Chapter 23 anti-corruption and fundamental rights benchmarks.

Ch.19 -- Define clear and dissuasive sanctions for non-compliance with reporting obligations and salary transparency requirements, calibrated to company size, to meet the EU directive's requirement for 'effective, proportionate, and dissuasive' penalties.

Ch.22 and IPA III -- Use the EUR 80 million IPA III Operational Programme for Employment and Social Inclusion to fund the institutional capacity building needed for enforcement, including IT systems for pay gap reporting and Labour Inspectorate modernization.

Regional cooperation -- Position these amendments as a Western Balkans best practice through the Berlin Process and offer technical assistance to other candidate countries through RESPA, reinforcing Montenegro's frontrunner narrative with the European Commission.

counter_proposal.title: Enhanced Labour Law Reform with Full Enforcement Architecture
counter_proposal.summary: The Ministry of EU Integration would retain all provisions of the current amendments but embed them within a comprehensive enforcement and institutional framework. Rather than passing the legislative text alone, the Ministry would bundle the law with a binding implementation action plan, dedicated budget allocation for the Labour Inspectorate, a digital pay gap reporting platform developed with IPA III funds, a phased timeline to extend reporting to 50+ employee companies, increased paternity leave to 15 days, and explicit dissuasive sanctions for non-compliance. This approach addresses the European Commission's consistent criticism that Montenegro passes EU-aligned laws but lags on implementation.
key_changes: Bundle the Labour Law amendments with a binding 18-month implementation action plan specifying institutional responsibilities, timelines, and measurable indicators.
Allocate EUR 2-3 million from the state budget for Labour Inspectorate capacity building, including hiring 15-20 specialized inspectors for pay transparency enforcement.
Develop a centralized digital pay gap reporting platform using IPA III Employment and Social Inclusion programme funds, operational within 12 months.
Include a phased schedule to lower the reporting threshold from 100+ to 50+ employees within 4 years, matching the EU Pay Transparency Directive trajectory.
Increase paternity leave from 10 to 15 working days, exceeding the EU minimum and signaling genuine commitment to gender equality.
Define explicit sanctions: fines of EUR 5,000-20,000 for non-reporting and EUR 10,000-50,000 for unjustified pay gaps above 5%, scaled by company size.
Mandate annual public disclosure of aggregate pay gap data by MONSTAT to enable public accountability and track national progress.
expected_benefits: Significantly strengthens Montenegro's Chapter 19 closing benchmark submission by demonstrating not just legislative alignment but implementation readiness, which is what the European Commission evaluates.

Positions Montenegro to absorb remaining IPA III Employment and Social Inclusion funds (from the EUR 80 million allocation) more effectively by building the institutional infrastructure those funds are designed to support.
Reduces the risk of the European Commission flagging enforcement gaps in the next annual progress report, which could delay Chapter 19 provisional closure.
Creates a replicable model for other open chapters where Montenegro faces the same legislative-adoption-without-enforcement pattern.

Exceeding the EU paternity leave minimum demonstrates genuine reform commitment rather than box-ticking, strengthening Montenegro's frontrunner narrative with Commissioner Kos and the enlargement directorate.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility: High feasibility. The legislative text already exists and requires only targeted additions (sanctions schedule, phased thresholds, action plan obligation). The Labour Inspectorate budget increase of EUR 2-3 million is modest relative to Montenegro's state budget. The digital reporting platform can be funded through the existing EUR 80 million IPA III allocation for Employment and Social Inclusion, which already includes capacity building components. The main political challenge is the 15-day paternity leave increase, which requires consensus from social partners, but the incremental nature of the change (5 additional days) makes it achievable within the social dialogue framework.
counter_proposal.title_mne: Unaprijeđena reforma Zakona o radu sa potpunom arhitekturom sprovođenja
counter_proposal.summary_mne: Ministarstvo evropskih integracija zadržalo bi sve odredbe trenutnih izmjena, ali bi ih ugradilo u sveobuhvatan okvir sprovođenja i institucionalnih mehanizama. Umjesto donošenja samo zakonodavnog teksta, Ministarstvo bi zakon upotpunilo obavezujućim akcionim planom implementacije, namjenskim budžetskim sredstvima za Inspektorat rada, digitalnom platformom za izvještavanje o razlici u zaradama razvijenom uz finansiranje iz IPA III fondova, faznim rasporedom za proširenje obaveze izvještavanja na kompanije sa 50 i više zaposlenih, produženim očinskim odsustvom na 15 dana, te jasno definisanim odvraćajućim sankcijama za neusklađenost. Ovim pristupom odgovara se na dosljednu kritiku Evropske komisije da Crna Gora usvaja zakone usklađene s pravnom tekovinom EU, ali zaostaje u njihovoj primjeni.
key_changes_mne: Upotpuniti izmjene Zakona o radu obavezujućim 18-mjesečnim akcionim planom implementacije koji precizira institucionalne nadležnosti, rokove i mjerljive pokazatelje.
Iz državnog budžeta izdvojiti 2–3 miliona EUR za jačanje kapaciteta Inspektorata rada, uključujući zapošljavanje 15–20 specijalizovanih inspektora za sprovođenje transparentnosti zarada.
Razviti centralizovanu digitalnu platformu za izvještavanje o razlici u zaradama koristeći sredstva IPA III programa za zapošljavanje i socijalnu uključenost, s rokom operativnosti od 12 mjeseci.
Uvesti fazni raspored za snižavanje praga izvještavanja sa 100 na 50 i više zaposlenih u roku od 4 godine, u skladu s dinamikom Direktive EU o transparentnosti zarada.
Produžiti očinsko odsustvo sa 10 na 15 radnih dana, čime se premašuje minimum EU i iskazuje stvarna posvećenost rodnoj ravnopravnosti.
Definisati jasne sankcije: novčane kazne od 5.000 do 20.000 EUR za neizvještavanje i od 10.000 do 50.000 EUR za neopravdane razlike u zaradama iznad 5%, srazmjerno veličini kompanije.
Obavezati MONSTAT na godišnje javno objavljivanje zbirnih podataka o razlici u zaradama radi obezbjeđivanja javne odgovornosti i praćenja nacionalnog napretka.
expected_benefits_mne: Značajno jača crnogorski zahtjev za zatvaranje Poglavlja 19 dokazujući ne samo zakonodavnu usklađenost, već i spremnost za implementaciju – što je upravo ono što Evropska komisija ocjenjuje.

Pozicionira Crnu Goru da efikasnije apsoruje preostala sredstva IPA III programa za zapošljavanje i socijalnu uključenost (iz iznosa od 80 miliona EUR), izgradnjom institucionalne infrastrukture koju ti fondovi i podržavaju.
Smanjuje rizik da Evropska komisija u narednom godišnjem izvještaju o napretku ukaže na nedostatke u sprovođenju, što bi moglo odložiti privremeno zatvaranje Poglavlja 19.
Stvara model koji se može primijeniti i na druga otvorena poglavlja u kojima Crna Gora ima isti obrazac – usvajanje zakona bez stvarnog sprovođenja.

Prevazilaženje minimalnog standarda EU u pogledu očinskog odsustva svjedoči o stvarnoj reformskoj posvećenosti, a ne pukom ispunjavanju formalnosti, čime se jača narativ Crne Gore kao predvodnice u procesu proširenja pred komesarkom Koš i Generalnom direkcijom za susjedsku politiku i pregovore o proširenju.
counter_proposal.estimated_feasibility_mne: Visoka izvodljivost. Zakonodavni tekst već postoji i zahtijeva samo ciljane dopune (raspored sankcija, fazni pragovi, obaveza akcionog plana). Povećanje budžeta Inspektorata rada za 2–3 miliona EUR skromno je u odnosu na državni budžet Crne Gore. Digitalna platforma za izvještavanje može se finansirati iz postojeće alokacije od 80 miliona EUR u okviru IPA III za zapošljavanje i socijalnu uključenost, koja već uključuje komponente izgradnje kapaciteta. Glavni politički izazov predstavlja povećanje očinskog odsustva na 15 dana, koje zahtijeva saglasnost socijalnih partnera, no postupna priroda ove promjene (5 dodatnih dana) čini je ostvarivom u okviru socijalnog dijaloga.

assessments.summary_mne: Izmjene i dopune Zakona o radu predstavljaju jedan od najznačajnijih zakonodavnih koraka koje je Crna Gora preduzela ka ispunjenju završnih mjerila za Poglavlje 19 (Socijalna politika i zapošljavanje). Transponovanjem ključnih odredaba Direktive EU 2019/1158 o ravnoteži između poslovnog i privatnog života te anticipiranjem Direktive EU o transparentnosti plata 2023/970, Crna Gora demonstrira ozbiljno zakonodavno usklađivanje s acquis communautaireom. Deset dana očinskog odsustva u potpunosti odgovara minimalnom standardu EU, prag od 5% razlike u platama između polova odražava okidač Direktive EU za korektivne mjere, a odredbe o transparentnosti zarada odgovaraju na dokumentovani jaz u platama između polova od 21,6% koji su MOR i Evropska komisija označili kao problematičan. Ova odluka neposredno jača pregovaračku poziciju Crne Gore u Poglavlju 19 i šalje signal Evropskoj komisiji da je Vlada posvećena ambicioznom cilju zatvaranja svih poglavlja do kraja 2026. godine.
assessments.executive_summary_mne: Ova odluka ima snažno pozitivan učinak na proces evropskih integracija, neposredno ispunjavajući završna mjerila Poglavlja 19 usklađivanjem s Direktivom o ravnoteži između poslovnog i privatnog života i Direktivom o transparentnosti plata. Glavna zabrinutost tiče se pitanja da li će kapaciteti za sprovođenje i institucionalna infrastruktura biti u skladu sa zakonodavnom ambicijom, s obzirom na to da Evropska komisija vrednuje implementaciju u praksi, a ne samo zakone na papiru. Ministarstvo preporučuje davanje prioriteta uspostavljanju namjenskog tijela za sprovođenje propisa i proširenju izvještavanja o platnom jazu na preduzeća s 50 i više zaposlenih, kako bi se u potpunosti uskladilo s putanjom Direktive EU.
key_concerns_mne: Pogl. 19 – Socijalna politika i zapošljavanje -- Iako je zakonodavno usklađivanje snažno, ne postoji jasan plan za izgradnju kapaciteta za sprovođenje propisa u Inspektoratu rada radi nadzora novih obaveza u pogledu transparentnosti plata i očinskog odsustva, što prijeti takozvanom 'papirnom transpozicijom' koju Evropska komisija redovno kritikuje.

Pogl. 19 – Socijalna politika i zapošljavanje -- Prag od 100 i više zaposlenih za izvještavanje o rodnom jazu u platama obuhvatiće samo mali dio crnogorske radne snage, s obzirom na to da privreda dominantno počiva na mikro i malim preduzećima. Direktiva EU predviđa proširenje na preduzeća s 50 i više zaposlenih do 2031. godine.

Pogl. 19 – Socijalna politika i zapošljavanje -- Deset dana očinskog odsustva u potpunosti ispunjava minimum Direktive EU 2019/1158, ali taj pod možda nije dovoljan da pokrene suštinsku promjenu u ponašanju očeva kada je u pitanju učešće u njezi djece, s obzirom na tradicionalne rodne norme u Crnoj Gori.

Pogl. 23 – Pravosuđe i osnovna prava -- Odredbe o zaštiti od odmazde zbog razgovora o platama su dobrodošle, ali njihova efikasnost zavisi od čvrstih mehanizama zaštite uzbunjivača, koji su, prema ocjenama Evropske komisije, i dalje nedovoljno razvijeni u Crnoj Gori.

Pogl. 22 – Regionalna politika i koordinacija strukturnih instrumenata -- Odsustvo jasnog plana za informatičku infrastrukturu za prikupljanje i obradu izvještaja o rodnom jazu u platama može stvoriti teškoće kada Crna Gora bude preuzimala upravljanje strukturnim fondovima EU koji zahtijevaju pouzdane statistike tržišta rada.

Pogl. 19 – Socijalna politika i zapošljavanje -- Ne postoje izričite odredbe o sankcijama ili kaznama za poslodavce koji ne ispunjavaju nove obaveze izvještavanja ili krše zahtjeve za transparentnost zarada, što može umanjiti odvraćajući učinak zakonodavstva.
recommendations_mne: Pogl. 19 -- Opredijeliti namjenski budžet i kadrove za Inspektorat rada radi sprovođenja novih obaveza prije stupanja zakona na snagu, uključujući specijalizovanu obuku o reviziji transparentnosti plata i metodologijama analize rodnog jaza u zaradama.

Pogl. 19 -- Usvojiti faznu implementacionu mapu puta kojom se izvještavanje o rodnom jazu u platama proširuje na preduzeća s 50 i više zaposlenih u roku od 3 do 4 godine, usklađujući se s putanjom Direktive EU o transparentnosti plata 2023/970 za države članice.

Pogl. 19 -- Povećati trajanje očinskog odsustva sa 10 na najmanje 15 radnih dana u roku od 18 mjeseci, uz javnu kampanju podizanja svijesti usmjerenu na očeve, kako bi se osiguralo da reforma donese stvarnu promjenu u ponašanju, a ne samo ispunjenje minimalnog standarda EU.

Pogl. 19 i Pogl. 18 (Statistika) -- Uspostaviti centralizovanu digitalnu platformu za izvještavanje o rodnom jazu u platama, u koordinaciji s MONSTAT-om, radi izgradnje statističke infrastrukture potrebne kako za sprovođenje propisa tako i za buduće upravljanje strukturnim fondovima EU.

Pogl. 23 -- Ojačati vezu između zaštite u okviru transparentnosti plata i postojećeg okvira za zaštitu uzbunjivača, kako bi se osiguralo da radnici ne trpe odmazdu zbog prijave diskriminacije u platama, što je povezano s mjerilima Poglavlja 23 o borbi protiv korupcije i osnovnim pravima.

Pogl. 19 -- Definisati jasne i odvraćajuće sankcije za nepoštovanje obaveza izvještavanja i zahtjeva za transparentnost zarada, prilagođene veličini preduzeća, kako bi se ispunio zahtjev direktive EU za 'efektivnim, proporcionalnim i odvraćajućim' kaznama.

Pogl. 22 i IPA III -- Iskoristiti 80 miliona evra iz Operativnog programa IPA III za zapošljavanje i socijalnu inkluziju za finansiranje izgradnje institucionalnih kapaciteta potrebnih za sprovođenje propisa, uključujući IT sisteme za izvještavanje o platnom jazu i modernizaciju Inspektorata rada.

Regionalna saradnja -- Pozicionirati ove izmjene i dopune kao primjer dobre prakse u Zapadnom Balkanu kroz Berlinski proces i ponuditi tehničku pomoć drugim zemljama kandidatkinjama putem RESPA, čime se jača narativ Crne Gore kao predvodnika u procesu evropske integracije u odnosima s Evropskom komisijom.

debate.consensus_summary: All nine ministries agree that the Labour Law amendments represent a legitimate and broadly positive step toward EU accession under Chapter 19 (Social Policy and Employment). There is universal acknowledgment that Montenegro's 21.6% gender pay gap requires legislative intervention, that statutory paternity leave fills a genuine legal gap, and that wage transparency strengthens worker protections. Every ministry that assessed enforcement capacity -- Finance, Economy, Justice, EU Integration, Interior, and Tourism -- agrees that Montenegro's labour inspectorate (36 inspectors, 1.28 per 10,000 workers) is critically inadequate to enforce the new obligations, and that without dedicated funding and additional staffing, the reforms risk becoming what the European Commission calls "paper transposition." Seven of nine ministries support phased implementation to allow institutional capacity to develop. All ministries acknowledge that the 100-employee threshold for gender pay gap reporting excludes the vast majority of Montenegro's 59,000 businesses, though they differ on the appropriate remedy. There is also broad consensus that the informal economy (estimated at 20-30% of GDP) will remain entirely unaffected by these formal legal mechanisms.
disagreements: Whether maternal/primary caregiver leave should be redistributed (12 months reduced to 10 plus 2 non-transferable months for fathers) or extended (maintaining 12 months and adding father-specific leave on top). The Ministries of Health and Education, along with civil society organizations including the Parents' Association and the Women's Rights Center, argue the redistribution model harms maternal and infant health outcomes. The Ministry of EU Integration views the current structure as meeting EU Directive 2019/1158 requirements.

The appropriate threshold for mandatory gender pay gap reporting: the current 100-employee threshold captures fewer than 1% of businesses. The Ministries of Economy and Tourism recommend lowering to 50 employees within three years, while Finance recommends phasing in from 250-plus employees first, then to 100-plus, mirroring the EU directive's own phased approach.

Whether the purchasing power criterion for minimum wage requires a defined methodology with fiscal sustainability caps (Finance position) or an automatic annual indexation mechanism (Economy position). Finance warns of unpredictable budgetary exposure through public sector wage bill cascading, while Economy argues the two-year review cycle is too slow to respond to inflation volatility.

The adequacy of 10 days of paternity leave: the Ministry of EU Integration recommends increasing to 15 working days within 18 months to drive behavioral change, while other ministries accept the EU minimum floor as a pragmatic starting point given Montenegro's traditional gender norms and low current father leave uptake (only 353-426 fathers per year).

Legislative priority allocation between Chapter 19 (Social Policy) and Chapter 27 (Environment and Climate Change): the Ministry of Environment argues that government legislative capacity devoted to Chapter 19 represents an opportunity cost while Chapter 27 -- the most complex accession chapter -- remains critically behind schedule with the National Waste Management Plan still unadopted.

Whether sector-specific implementation protocols are needed for security services (Interior), healthcare (Health), education (Education), and tourism (Tourism), or whether uniform implementation is sufficient. The Ministry of Interior argues that the 5% gender pay gap threshold may produce misleading results in the security sector where legitimate pay differentials exist for hazardous duty and night shifts.
debate.debate_transcript:
PARLIAMENTARY DEBATE ON LABOUR LAW AMENDMENTS -- SKUPSTINA CRNE GORE

Session opened by the Speaker of Parliament.

INTRODUCTION AND GOVERNMENT PRESENTATION

The ruling Europe Now! (Pokret Evropa sad) coalition presents the Labour Law amendments as a flagship achievement of the 114th Cabinet session. The coalition argues that these amendments demonstrate Montenegro's serious commitment to EU accession under Chapter 19, noting that the provisions directly transpose EU Directive 2019/1158 on work-life balance and anticipate the EU Pay Transparency Directive 2023/970. The coalition highlights that Montenegro's 21.6% gender pay gap, documented by the ILO, is among the highest in the Western Balkans and that legislative action is overdue. The coalition frames the purchasing power criterion for minimum wage as a direct response to the cost-of-living pressures facing Montenegrin workers, and the paternity leave provision as a civilizational advance that places Montenegro alongside EU member states.

The junior coalition partner URA (Gradanski pokret URA) offers support for the amendments while emphasizing the transparency and civic participation dimensions. URA notes that wage transparency in job postings and the prohibition on salary history inquiries represent a shift toward a more open labour market. URA stresses that the gender pay gap reporting obligation, while limited to companies with 100-plus employees, establishes the principle of corporate accountability that can be expanded over time. However, URA signals concern about the environmental legislative backlog, echoing the Ministry of Environment's assessment that Chapter 27 implementation remains critically behind schedule. URA argues that the government must demonstrate equal urgency on environmental legislation and calls for dedicated Cabinet sessions on the National Waste Management Plan and the National Energy and Climate Plan.

OPPOSITION RESPONSE

The DPS-led opposition (Demokratska partija socijalista and SDP coalition) adopts a measured but critical stance. The opposition acknowledges that the substance of the amendments is broadly positive and consistent with the EU integration path that DPS governments pursued for three decades. However, the opposition raises several pointed concerns.

First, the DPS-led opposition highlights the absence of a fiscal impact assessment, noting that the Ministry of Finance itself acknowledges it was not presented with cost projections. The opposition argues that this represents a failure of legislative due diligence and questions how the government can propose linking minimum wage to purchasing power without defining the calculation methodology, the price index to be used, or any fiscal sustainability cap. The opposition warns that without these safeguards, the provision creates open-ended budgetary exposure through public sector wage bill cascading and indexed social transfers.

Second, the DPS-led opposition raises the parental leave redistribution as the most politically sensitive element of the package. Citing the strong public backlash documented by the Parents' Association (Udruzenje Roditelji) -- which has called for the immediate withdrawal of the provision reducing mothers' leave from 12 to 10 months -- the opposition argues that the government has failed to consult adequately with parents and civil society. The opposition notes that the Parents' Association reported "an unprecedented level of public opposition" not seen in any previous family policy proposal. The DPS-led bloc argues that the government should maintain the full 365 days for the primary caregiver and add father-specific leave on top, as recommended by both the Women's Rights Center and the Ministries of Health and Education. The opposition frames this as a matter of protecting acquired rights rather than redistributing them.

Third, the SDP component of the opposition focuses on enforcement capacity. Citing the Ministry of Justice's finding that Montenegro has only 36 labour inspectors -- 1.28 per 10,000 workers -- the SDP argues that these progressive legal provisions risk becoming dead letters without a concrete enforcement plan and dedicated budget. The SDP notes that the Ministry of EU Integration itself warns against "paper transposition" and questions where the funding for additional inspectors, IT systems, and training will come from. The SDP calls for the amendments to include a mandatory 18-month implementation period with phased enforcement milestones and a ring-fenced enforcement budget.

The SDP also emphasizes the workers' rights dimension, noting that the informal economy -- estimated at 20 to 30 percent of GDP -- is entirely untouched by these formal legal mechanisms. The SDP argues that the most vulnerable workers, disproportionately women in seasonal tourism, agriculture, and domestic work, will see no practical benefit from wage transparency or pay gap reporting if they remain in the grey economy. The SDP calls for companion measures against informal employment, including increased penalties for undeclared work and simplified registration for micro-enterprises.

NATIONALIST BLOC RESPONSE

The DF/DNP bloc (Demokratski front and Demokratska narodna partija) adopts a sharply critical position, framing the amendments through the lens of sovereignty and traditional values. The DF/DNP bloc argues that the amendments represent an uncritical adoption of Brussels-dictated social engineering that disregards Montenegrin family traditions and the role of mothers in early childcare. The bloc specifically targets the non-transferable two-month parental leave provision, arguing that it imposes a particular model of family organization that may not reflect the preferences of Montenegrin families, particularly in rural and traditional communities.

The DF/DNP bloc challenges the government's framing of the amendments as necessary for EU accession, arguing that Montenegro is surrendering legislative autonomy to meet benchmarks set by external actors. The bloc notes that the 10-day paternity leave matches the EU directive's minimum floor exactly, suggesting that Montenegro is legislating to European specifications rather than to national needs. The bloc questions whether the gender pay gap reporting obligation is proportionate for Montenegro's small economy, where the 457 companies with 100-plus employees will bear compliance costs without any accompanying support or transition period.

On the minimum wage provisions, the DF/DNP bloc expresses qualified support, acknowledging that linking minimum wage to purchasing power addresses a real concern for ordinary Montenegrin workers facing inflation. However, the bloc argues that this provision should have been introduced as standalone legislation rather than bundled with what it characterizes as ideologically driven gender policy. The bloc calls for a separate, focused minimum wage law that reflects national economic conditions rather than EU directive requirements.

The DNP specifically raises the impact on the security sector, echoing the Ministry of Interior's concerns about chronically understaffed police forces. The DNP argues that new leave entitlements, applied without sector-specific protocols, could compromise operational readiness of border police units during a critical period of Schengen preparation. The DNP calls for explicit security sector exemptions or, at minimum, a 12-month transitional period for the Ministry of Interior.

MINORITY PARTY POSITIONS

The Bosnjacka stranka (BS) and Albanian minority parties express support for the amendments as a step toward greater equality and inclusion. These parties emphasize that gender pay discrimination disproportionately affects women from minority communities, who face intersectional barriers in the labour market. The minority parties welcome the wage transparency provisions as a tool for addressing not only gender-based but also ethnicity-based pay discrimination, and call for the reporting framework to be expanded to include ethnicity-disaggregated data in future revisions.

The minority parties also raise concerns about the geographical impact of the amendments, noting that the 100-employee threshold effectively excludes most businesses in minority-majority municipalities, where the economy is dominated by micro and small enterprises. They call for targeted support programs through the Employment Bureau to help SMEs in these municipalities voluntarily adopt wage transparency practices.

COMMITTEE-LEVEL ANALYSIS

The debate reflects the following cross-cutting themes emerging from the nine ministry assessments presented to Parliament:

On EU accession value, there is broad consensus across the ruling coalition, the DPS-led opposition, and minority parties that the amendments advance Chapter 19 alignment. The Ministry of EU Integration's assessment -- scoring the decision at 8 out of 10, the highest of any ministry -- carries significant weight, particularly its finding that the provisions directly transpose core elements of EU Directive 2019/1158 and anticipate Directive 2023/970. Even the DF/DNP bloc does not dispute the technical alignment, instead questioning whether such alignment should be a legislative priority.

On the parental leave redistribution, the debate reveals the sharpest division. The ruling coalition defends the model as consistent with EU norms and necessary to incentivize father participation. The opposition and civil society, backed by the Ministries of Health and Education, argue that given Montenegro's current reality -- where only 353 to 426 fathers per year take any parental leave -- the non-transferable provision will in practice result in lost leave for most families rather than shared caregiving. The Women's Rights Center has called for aligning reform with system capacities and the interests of children and parents. The Parents' Association has gone further, demanding full withdrawal and pledging to submit amendments through parliamentary committees if the government does not reverse course. This civil society mobilization represents a significant political challenge for the ruling coalition.

On enforcement, the consensus is striking. The Ministry of Justice documents that Montenegro has only 36 labour inspectors and that existing penalties are rarely applied. The Ministry of EU Integration warns that without enforcement infrastructure, the European Commission will characterize the reforms as paper transposition. The Ministry of Economy notes that the informal economy is entirely unaddressed. Multiple ministries recommend phased implementation, additional inspectorate funding through IPA III instruments, and a centralized digital reporting platform. No ministry argues that current enforcement capacity is adequate.

On the purchasing power minimum wage criterion, the Ministry of Finance raises the most substantive concern: the absence of a defined calculation methodology creates unpredictable budgetary exposure. The Ministry recommends specifying the price index, reference basket, and a fiscal sustainability clause capping annual increases at nominal GDP growth plus a defined margin. The Ministry of Economy, while supportive of the principle, argues for automatic annual indexation rather than biennial review. This technical disagreement will require resolution in the parliamentary committee stage.

On sector-specific concerns, the Ministries of Interior, Health, Education, and Tourism each identify implementation challenges unique to their workforces. The Ministry of Interior warns about understaffed police forces and Schengen preparation. The Ministry of Health raises maternal health risks from the leave redistribution. The Ministry of Education notes that the 100-employee threshold may exclude individual schools. The Ministry of Tourism identifies peak-season staffing disruptions and the absence of a permanent seasonal worker framework. These sector-specific concerns suggest that implementing regulations will need to address the diverse employment structures across the Montenegrin economy.

FINAL POSITIONS AND VOTE INDICATION

The ruling Europe Now!/URA coalition indicates it will support the amendments proceeding to parliamentary adoption, with openness to technical amendments on the minimum wage methodology and phased implementation timelines during committee stage.

The DPS-led opposition signals conditional support for the overall package but announces it will table amendments on three points: maintaining 365 days of primary caregiver leave, requiring a fiscal impact assessment before final adoption, and mandating a ring-fenced enforcement budget for the labour inspectorate.

The DF/DNP bloc indicates it will vote against the package as drafted, while expressing willingness to support a separated minimum wage provision if decoupled from the gender policy elements.

Minority parties indicate full support, with proposed amendments on ethnicity-disaggregated pay gap data and targeted SME support for minority-majority municipalities.

The parliamentary consensus, despite procedural and substantive disagreements, points toward likely adoption with amendments. The strongest consensus exists around the need for enforcement capacity building, fiscal impact assessment, and phased implementation -- recommendations shared across nearly all ministry assessments and most parliamentary blocs.
debate.consensus_summary_mne: Svih devet ministarstava saglasno je da izmjene Zakona o radu predstavljaju legitiman i u cjelini pozitivan korak ka pristupanju EU u okviru Poglavlja 19 (Socijalna politika i zapošljavanje). Postoji opšte priznanje da razlika u platama između polova od 21,6% u Crnoj Gori zahtijeva zakonodavnu intervenciju, da zakonsko porodiljsko odsustvo za očeve popunjava stvarnu pravnu prazninu i da transparentnost zarada jača zaštitu radnika. Svako ministarstvo koje je procijenilo kapacitete za sprovođenje – Finansija, Ekonomije, Pravde, Evropskih integracija, Unutrašnjih poslova i Turizma – slaže se da je inspekcija rada Crne Gore (36 inspektora, odnosno 1,28 na 10.000 radnika) krajnje nedovoljna za sprovođenje novih obaveza, te da bez namjenskog finansiranja i dodatnog kadra reforme prijete da postanu ono što Evropska komisija naziva "papirnom transpozicijom". Sedam od devet ministarstava podržava faznu implementaciju kako bi se institucionalnim kapacitetima dao prostor za razvoj. Sva ministarstva priznavaju da prag od 100 zaposlenih za obavezno izvještavanje o razlici u platama isključuje veliku većinu od 59.000 preduzeća u Crnoj Gori, mada se razilaze po pitanju odgovarajućeg rješenja. Postoji i široka saglasnost da neformalna ekonomija (procijenjena na 20–30% BDP-a) neće biti ni najmanje pogođena ovim formalnim pravnim mehanizmima.
disagreements_mne: Da li bi porodiljsko odsustvo i odsustvo primarnog staratelja trebalo preraspodijeliti (smanjiti sa 12 na 10 mjeseci i dodati 2 neprenosiva mjeseca za očeve) ili produžiti (zadržati 12 mjeseci i naknadno dodati odsustvo namijenjeno isključivo očevima). Ministarstva zdravlja i prosvjete, zajedno s organizacijama civilnog društva, uključujući Udruženje roditelja i Centar za ženska prava, tvrde da model preraspodjele negativno utiče na zdravlje majki i odojčadi. Ministarstvo evropskih integracija smatra da postojeća struktura ispunjava zahtjeve Direktive EU 2019/1158.

Odgovarajući prag za obavezno izvještavanje o razlici u platama između polova: trenutni prag od 100 zaposlenih obuhvata manje od 1% preduzeća. Ministarstva ekonomije i turizma preporučuju snižavanje na 50 zaposlenih u roku od tri godine, dok Ministarstvo finansija preporučuje fazno uvođenje – prvo od 250 i više zaposlenih, a potom od 100 i više – po uzoru na sam fazni pristup EU direktive.

Da li kriterijum kupovne moći za minimalnu zaradu zahtijeva definisanu metodologiju s ograničenjima fiskalne održivosti (stav Ministarstva finansija) ili automatski godišnji mehanizam indeksacije (stav Ministarstva ekonomije). Ministarstvo finansija upozorava na nepredvidivo buđetsko opterećenje usljed kaskadnog efekta na fondove zarada u javnom sektoru, dok Ministarstvo ekonomije smatra da je dvogodišnji ciklus preispitivanja previše spor za odgovor na inflatornu volatilnost.

Adekvatnost 10 dana očinskog odsustva: Ministarstvo evropskih integracija preporučuje povećanje na 15 radnih dana u roku od 18 mjeseci radi podsticanja promjene ponašanja, dok ostala ministarstva prihvataju minimalni prag EU kao pragmatičnu polaznu tačku s obzirom na tradicionalne rodne norme u Crnoj Gori i trenutno nisku stopu korišćenja očinskog odsustva (svega 353–426 očeva godišnje).

Zakonodavni prioritet između Poglavlja 19 (Socijalna politika) i Poglavlja 27 (Životna sredina i klimatske promjene): Ministarstvo životne sredine tvrdi da zakonodavni kapaciteti Vlade usmjereni na Poglavlje 19 predstavljaju oportunitetni trošak dok Poglavlje 27 – najsloženije poglavlje u procesu pristupanja – znatno kasni za rasporedom, a Nacionalni plan upravljanja otpadom još uvijek nije usvojen.

Da li su potrebni sektorski protokoli implementacije za službe bezbjednosti (Unutrašnji poslovi), zdravstvo (Zdravlje), prosvjetu (Prosvjeta) i turizam (Turizam), ili je uniformna implementacija dovoljna. Ministarstvo unutrašnjih poslova tvrdi da prag razlike u platama od 5% može dati pogrešne rezultate u sektoru bezbjednosti, gdje postoje opravdane razlike u naknadi za opasne dužnosti i noćni rad.
debate.debate_transcript_mne:
PARLAMENTARNA RASPRAVA O IZMJENAMA ZAKONA O RADU – SKUPŠTINA CRNE GORE

Sjednicu je otvorio predsjednik Skupštine.

UVOD I PREZENTACIJA VLADE

Koalicija na vlasti Pokret Evropa sad predstavlja izmjene Zakona o radu kao ključno dostignuće 114. sjednice Vlade. Koalicija ističe da ove izmjene svjedoče o ozbiljnoj opredijeljenosti Crne Gore ka pristupanju EU u okviru Poglavlja 19, napominjući da odredbe direktno transponuju Direktivu EU 2019/1158 o ravnoteži između poslovnog i privatnog života i anticipiraju Direktivu EU o transparentnosti plata 2023/970. Koalicija naglašava da razlika u platama između polova od 21,6% u Crnoj Gori, dokumentovana od strane MOR-a, spada među najviše u Zapadnom Balkanu te da su zakonodavne mjere odavno zakasnile. Koalicija predstavlja kriterijum kupovne moći za minimalnu zaradu kao direktan odgovor na pritisak troškova života koji osjećaju crnogorski radnici, a odredbu o očinskom odsustvu kao civilizacijski napredak koji Crnu Goru svrstava uz bok državama članicama EU.

Mlađi koalicioni partner Građanski pokret URA pruža podršku izmjenama uz isticanje dimenzija transparentnosti i građanskog učešća. URA napominje da transparentnost zarada u oglasima za posao i zabrana pitanja o platnoj istoriji predstavljaju pomak ka otvorenijem tržištu rada. URA naglašava da obaveza izvještavanja o razlici u platama, mada ograničena na kompanije s 100 i više zaposlenih, uspostavlja princip korporativne odgovornosti koji se može proširivati tokom vremena. Ipak, URA izražava zabrinutost zbog zaostajanja u ekološkom zakonodavstvu, ponavlja ocjenu Ministarstva životne sredine da implementacija Poglavlja 27 znatno kaspi i poziva Vladu da pokaže jednaku hitnost u donošenju ekološkog zakonodavstva, te traži posvećene sjednice Vlade o Nacionalnom planu upravljanja otpadom i Nacionalnom energetskom i klimatskom planu.

ODGOVOR OPOZICIJE

Opozicija predvođena DPS-om (Demokratska partija socijalista i koalicija SDP-a) zauzima odmjeren, ali kritičan stav. Opozicija priznaje da je sadržina izmjena uglavnom pozitivna i usklađena s putem evropskih integracija koji su tri decenije obilježavale Vlade DPS-a. Međutim, opozicija iznosi nekoliko oštrih primjedbi.

Prvo, opozicija predvođena DPS-om ukazuje na odsustvo procjene fiskalnog uticaja, napominjući da samo Ministarstvo finansija potvrđuje da mu nisu dostavljene projekcije troškova. Opozicija tvrdi da to predstavlja propust u zakonodavnoj dužnoj pažnji i dovodi u pitanje kako Vlada može predlagati vezivanje minimalne zarade za kupovnu moć bez definisanja metodologije obračuna, indeksa cijena koji će se koristiti ili bilo kakvog fiskalnog ograničenja. Opozicija upozorava da bez ovih garancija odredba stvara otvorenu buđetsku izloženost kroz kaskadni efekat na fond zarada u javnom sektoru i indeksovane socijalne transfere.

Drugo, opozicija predvođena DPS-om ističe preraspodjelu roditeljskog odsustva kao politički najosjetljiviji element paketa. Pozivajući se na snažno javno nezadovoljstvo koje je dokumentovalo Udruženje roditelja – koje je tražilo hitno povlačenje odredbe kojom se majčino odsustvo skraćuje sa 12 na 10 mjeseci – opozicija tvrdi da Vlada nije adekvatno konsultovala roditelje i civilno društvo. Opozicija napominje da je Udruženje roditelja prijavilo "neviđeni nivo javnog protivljenja" kakav nije zabilježen ni u jednom ranijem prijedlogu porodične politike. Blok predvođen DPS-om tvrdi da Vlada treba da zadrži punih 365 dana za primarnog staratelja i doda posebno odsustvo za očeve povrh toga, kako preporučuju Centar za ženska prava i ministarstva zdravlja i prosvjete. Opozicija to okvirno predstavlja kao pitanje zaštite stečenih prava, a ne njihove preraspodjele.

Treće, SDP komponenta opozicije fokusira se na kapacitete za sprovođenje. Pozivajući se na nalaz Ministarstva pravde da Crna Gora ima svega 36 inspektora rada – 1,28 na 10.000 radnika – SDP tvrdi da ove napredne zakonske odredbe prijete da postanu mrtvo slovo na papiru bez konkretnog plana sprovođenja i namjenskog budžeta. SDP napominje da i samo Ministarstvo evropskih integracija upozorava na "papirnu transpoziciju" i dovodi u pitanje odakle će se finansirati dodatni inspektori, IT sistemi i obuka. SDP poziva da izmjene uključe obavezan period implementacije od 18 mjeseci s faznim milokamenima sprovođenja i zaštićenim buđetom za nadzor.

SDP takođe naglašava dimenziju zaštite prava radnika, ističući da neformalna ekonomija – procijenjena na 20 do 30 posto BDP-a – ostaje u potpunosti nedotaknuta ovim formalnim pravnim mehanizmima. SDP tvrdi da najranjiviji radnici, nesrazmjerno zastupljeni u ženskoj populaciji zaposlenih u sezonskom turizmu, poljoprivredi i kućnom radu, neće imati nikakve praktične koristi od transparentnosti zarada ili izvještavanja o razlici u platama ukoliko ostanu u sivoj ekonomiji. SDP poziva na prateće mjere protiv neformalne zaposlenosti, uključujući povećane kazne za rad na crno i pojednostavljivanje registracije za mikropreduzetnike.

ODGOVOR NACIONALISTIČKOG BLOKA

Blok DF/DNP (Demokratski front i Demokratska narodna partija) zauzima oštro kritičan stav, sagledavajući izmjene kroz prizmu suvereniteta i tradicionalnih vrijednosti. Blok DF/DNP tvrdi da izmjene predstavljaju nekritičko usvajanje socijalnog inžinjeringa koji diktira Brisel, a koji zanemaruje crnogorske porodične tradicije i ulogu majke u ranom odgoju djece. Blok se posebno suprotstavlja odredbi o dva neprenosiva mjeseca roditeljskog odsustva, tvrdeći da ona nameće određeni model porodičnih odnosa koji možda ne odražava preferencije crnogorskih porodica, posebno u ruralnim i tradicionalnim zajednicama.

Blok DF/DNP osporava vladino okvirno predstavljanje izmjena kao neophodnih za pristupanje EU, tvrdeći da Crna Gora odriče zakonodavnu autonomiju kako bi ispunila kriterijume koje postavljaju vanjski akteri. Blok napominje da 10 dana očinskog odsustva tačno odgovara minimalnom pragu EU direktive, što sugeriše da Crna Gora donosi zakone prema evropskim specifikacijama, a ne prema nacionalnim potrebama. Blok dovodi u pitanje da li je obaveza izvještavanja o razlici u platama proporcionalna za crnogorsku malu ekonomiju, u kojoj će 457 kompanija s 100 i više zaposlenih snositi troškove usklađivanja bez ikakvog prateće podrške ili prelaznog perioda.

Kad je riječ o odredbama o minimalnoj zaradi, blok DF/DNP izražava ograničenu podršku, priznavajući da vezivanje minimalne zarade za kupovnu moć odgovara na stvarnu zabrinutost crnogorskih radnika suočenih s inflacijom. Međutim, blok tvrdi da je ovu odredbu trebalo uvesti kao zasebni zakon, a ne svrstati je s onim što karakteriše kao ideološki motivisanu politiku ravnopravnosti polova. Blok poziva na zaseban, fokusiran zakon o minimalnoj zaradi koji odražava nacionalne ekonomske prilike, a ne zahtjeve EU direktiva.

DNP posebno ukazuje na uticaj na sektor bezbjednosti, ponavljajući zabrinutost Ministarstva unutrašnjih poslova zbog hronički nedostatnih policijskih snaga. DNP tvrdi da nova prava na odsustvo, primijenjena bez sektorskih protokola, mogu ugroziti operativnu gotovost jedinica granične policije u kritičnom periodu šengenskih priprema. DNP traži izričita izuzeća za sektor bezbjednosti ili, barem, prelazni period od 12 mjeseci za Ministarstvo unutrašnjih poslova.

STAVOVI MANJINSKIH STRANAKA

Bošnjačka stranka (BS) i albanske manjinske stranke izražavaju podršku izmjenama kao koraku ka većoj ravnopravnosti i uključivosti. Ove stranke naglašavaju da diskriminacija po osnovu pola u oblasti zarada nesrazmjerno pogađa žene iz manjinskih zajednica, koje se suočavaju s intersekcionim preprekama na tržištu rada. Manjinske stranke pozdravljaju odredbe o transparentnosti zarada kao sredstvo za suzbijanje ne samo diskriminacije zasnovane na polu već i one zasnovane na etničkoj pripadnosti, te pozivaju da se okvir izvještavanja proširi na podatke razvrstane prema etničkoj pripadnosti u budućim revizijama.

Manjinske stranke takođe izražavaju zabrinutost zbog geografskog uticaja izmjena, napominjući da prag od 100 zaposlenih efektivno isključuje većinu preduzeća u opštinama u kojima manjine čine većinu, gdje ekonomijom dominiraju mikro i mala preduzeća. One pozivaju na ciljane programe podrške kroz Zavod za zapošljavanje kako bi se MSP-ovima u tim opštinama pomoglo da dobrovoljno usvoje prakse transparentnosti zarada.

ANALIZA NA NIVOU ODBORA

Rasprava odražava sljedeće horizontalne teme koje se javljaju u procjenama devet ministarstava podnesenim Skupštini:

Kad je riječ o vrijednosti za pristupanje EU, postoji široka saglasnost vladajuće koalicije, opozicije predvođene DPS-om i manjinskih stranaka da izmjene unapređuju usklađivanje s Poglavljem 19. Procjena Ministarstva evropskih integracija – koje je odluku ocijenilo s 8 od 10, što je najviša ocjena bilo kojeg ministarstva – ima značajnu težinu, posebno njen nalaz da odredbe direktno transponuju ključne elemente Direktive EU 2019/1158 i anticipiraju Direktivu 2023/970. Čak ni blok DF/DNP ne osporava tehničku usklađenost, već dovodi u pitanje da li takva usklađenost treba da bude zakonodavni prioritet.

Kad je riječ o preraspodjeli roditeljskog odsustva, rasprava otkriva najoštrije podjele. Vladajuća koalicija brani ovaj model kao usklađen s normama EU i neophodan za podsticanje učešća očeva. Opozicija i civilno društvo, uz podršku ministarstava zdravlja i prosvjete, tvrde da će, s obzirom na trenutnu crnogorsku realnost – gdje svega 353 do 426 očeva godišnje koristi bilo kakvo roditeljsko odsustvo – neprenosiva odredba u praksi dovesti do gubitka odsustva za većinu porodica, a ne do podijeljene brige o djeci. Centar za ženska prava pozvao je na usklađivanje reforme s kapacitetima sistema te interesima djece i roditelja. Udruženje roditelja otišlo je korak dalje, tražeći potpuno povlačenje i najavivši podnošenje amandmana putem parlamentarnih odbora ukoliko Vlada ne promijeni kurs. Ova mobilizacija civilnog društva predstavlja značajan politički izazov za vladajuću koaliciju.

Kad je riječ o sprovođenju, konsenzus je upečatljiv. Ministarstvo pravde dokumentuje da Crna Gora ima svega 36 inspektora rada i da se postojeće sankcije rijetko primjenjuju. Ministarstvo evropskih integracija upozorava da će Evropska komisija, bez infrastrukture za sprovođenje, reforme okarakterisati kao papirnu transpoziciju. Ministarstvo ekonomije napominje da neformalna ekonomija ostaje u potpunosti neadresirana. Više ministarstava preporučuje faznu implementaciju, dodatno finansiranje inspekcije kroz instrumente IPA III i centralnu digitalnu platformu za izvještavanje. Nijedno ministarstvo ne tvrdi da su trenutni kapaciteti za sprovođenje odgovarajući.

Kad je riječ o kriterijumu minimalne zarade zasnovanom na kupovnoj moći, Ministarstvo finansija iznosi najsuštinsku primjedbu: odsustvo definisane metodologije obračuna stvara nepredvidivo buđetsko opterećenje. Ministarstvo preporučuje preciziranje indeksa cijena, referentne korpe i klauzule fiskalne održivosti koja godišnja povećanja ograničava na nominalni rast BDP-a uz definisanu maržu. Ministarstvo ekonomije, mada podržava princip, zalaže se za automatsku godišnju indeksaciju umjesto dvogodišnjeg preispitivanja. Ovo tehničko neslaganje zahtijevaće razrješenje u fazi rada parlamentarnog odbora.

Kad je riječ o sektorskim zabrinutostima, ministarstva unutrašnjih poslova, zdravlja, prosvjete i turizma svako za sebe identifikuje izazove implementacije specifične za njihovu radnu snagu. Ministarstvo unutrašnjih poslova upozorava na nedostatne policijske snage i šengenske pripreme. Ministarstvo zdravlja ukazuje na rizike po zdravlje majki usljed preraspodjele odsustva. Ministarstvo prosvjete napominje da prag od 100 zaposlenih može isključiti pojedinačne škole. Ministarstvo turizma identifikuje poremećaje u zapošljavanju tokom sezone i odsustvo stalnog okvira za sezonske radnike. Ove sektorske zabrinutosti ukazuju da će provedbeni propisi morati da odgovore na raznolike strukture zapošljavanja u crnogorskoj ekonomiji.

KONAČNI STAVOVI I NAGOVJEŠTAJ GLASANJA

Vladajuća koalicija Pokret Evropa sad/URA naznačava da će podržati upućivanje izmjena na parlamentarno usvajanje, uz otvorenost za tehničke amandmane o metodologiji minimalne zarade i rokovima fazne implementacije u toku rada odbora.

Opozicija predvođena DPS-om nagovještava uslonvu podršku cjelokupnom paketu, ali najavljuje podnošenje amandmana u tri tačke: zadržavanje 365 dana odsustva za primarnog staratelja, obavezna procjena fiskalnog uticaja prije konačnog usvajanja i mandatni zaštićeni buđet za inspekciju rada.

Blok DF/DNP naznačava da će glasati protiv paketa u predloženom obliku, izražavajući pri tome spremnost da podrži izdvojenu odredbu o minimalnoj zaradi ukoliko se odvoji od elemenata politike ravnopravnosti polova.

Manjinske stranke naznačavaju punu podršku, s predloženim amandmanima o podacima o razlici u platama razvrstanih prema etničkoj pripadnosti i ciljanoj podršci MSP-ovima u opštinama u kojima manjine čine većinu.

Parlarmentarni konsenzus, uprkos proceduralnim i suštinskim neslaganjima, ukazuje na vjerovatno usvajanje uz amandmane. Najjači konsenzus postoji oko potrebe za izgradnjom kapaciteta za sprovođenje, procjenom fiskalnog uticaja i faznom implementacijom – preporuke koje dijele gotovo sva ministarska mišljenja i većina parlamentarnih blokova.

blind_spots: No ministry questioned whether the EU Directive's mandatory burden of proof reversal in pay discrimination cases was included in the amendments -- this is a core requirement of Directive 2023/970 and its absence would make the transposition legally incomplete

No fiscal impact assessment was prepared for these amendments, violating EU best practice for legislation with budgetary implications, yet no ministry formally objected to this procedural gap or demanded one be produced before adoption

The interaction between these Labour Law amendments and the still-stalled Law on Permanent Seasonal Workers was not addressed -- these amendments apply imperfectly to seasonal tourism and agriculture workers who cycle between formal and informal arrangements, and the government missed an opportunity to advance both simultaneously

No ministry analyzed what actually happens to families when fathers do not take the non-transferable two months -- given that only 353-426 fathers per year currently use any parental leave, the near-certain outcome is that most families will simply lose two months of infant care, effectively punishing mothers and children for fathers' non-participation

The constitutional amendment withdrawal at the same 114th session was not discussed by any ministry for its implications on Chapter 23 (Judiciary and Fundamental Rights) progress, which gates overall accession

No ministry addressed the digital infrastructure and data collection systems needed for gender pay gap reporting -- without standardized reporting templates, verification mechanisms, and a designated monitoring body, the 457 affected companies have no practical guidance on compliance

Social partner consultation was barely mentioned -- the Social Council's role in shaping these amendments is unclear, and meaningful tripartite dialogue is both an EU expectation and an ILO convention requirement that Montenegro has ratified

No comparative analysis was offered against regional competitors beyond cursory mentions -- Serbia has no comparable pay transparency law, giving Montenegro a genuine competitive advantage for EU-oriented FDI, but Albania's lower labour costs mean compliance burdens could shift investment in price-sensitive sectors

The amendments' interaction with Montenegro's tax incentive scheme for young workers (sliding scale income tax exemptions for ages 18-35) was not analyzed -- wage transparency and minimum wage increases could either complement or conflict with these retention measures designed to combat brain drain
critic_report.overall_analysis:
These Labour Law amendments are genuinely good policy direction. They address a real 21.6% gender pay gap documented by the ILO, introduce paternity leave where none existed, and create wage transparency mechanisms that align with EU Directive 2023/970 and Directive 2019/1158. The decision advances Chapter 19 closing benchmarks at a critical moment when Montenegro has closed only 13 of 33 accession chapters and faces an ambitious 2026-2027 deadline. That said, the decision deserves a 7 not higher for several reasons.

First, Montenegro is adopting only the absolute EU minimum on paternity leave: 10 working days. Spain already offers 16 weeks. Finland gives both parents 160 days each. Even Portugal offers 28 days. Adopting only the floor standard while claiming progressive reform is misleading. The practical impact on gender norms in a society where only 353-426 fathers per year take any parental leave will be negligible.

Second, and most critically, the parental leave restructuring appears to reduce mothers' total protected leave from 12 months to approximately 10 months by designating two months as non-transferable for fathers. Given the documented reality that fewer than 500 fathers per year use parental leave, this means most families will simply lose two months of care time for their infants. The Center for Women's Rights has flagged this concern. This is not a theoretical risk -- it is a near-certainty based on current uptake patterns. The government has essentially created a mechanism that punishes mothers for fathers' non-participation.

Third, the enforcement infrastructure is absent. Montenegro has only 36 labour inspectors for the entire country -- approximately 1.28 per 10,000 workers. The US State Department and European Commission have both documented that existing labour law penalties are rarely applied and inspectors rarely conduct unannounced visits. Adding new wage transparency and gender pay gap reporting obligations to this overstretched system without additional resources is performative legislating.

Fourth, the EU Pay Transparency Directive 2023/970 requires specific judicial remedies that appear absent from these amendments: reversal of the burden of proof in pay discrimination cases, access to full compensation including back pay, and protection for workers' representatives. If these elements are missing, the transposition is incomplete and Montenegro risks a "paper transposition" that the European Commission has repeatedly criticized in Western Balkan countries.

Fifth, the informal economy estimated at 30% of GDP is completely untouched. Workers receiving envelope wages -- common in tourism, construction, and services -- will see zero benefit from these provisions. The 100-employee threshold for pay gap reporting captures only about 457 companies out of approximately 59,000 active businesses, meaning less than 1% of employers face reporting obligations.

Sixth, the purchasing power criterion for minimum wage lacks any defined methodology. Will it use HICP, national CPI, or a consumption basket? The two-year review cycle may be too slow during volatile inflation periods. Without a formula, this becomes a political tool rather than an evidence-based mechanism.

The ministry assessments were generally thorough and substantive -- Finance provided useful fiscal modeling, Health raised important maternal health concerns, Justice identified the enforcement gap clearly, and EU Integration gave strong strategic framing. However, the ministries collectively missed several critical points: no one demanded an explanation for why no fiscal impact assessment was prepared (this is a basic EU legislative standard); no ministry questioned whether the burden of proof reversal from the EU Directive was included; and no one addressed the interaction between these amendments and the still-stalled seasonal worker framework that has been in legislative limbo for years despite broad political consensus. The Tourism ministry raised this gap but no one connected it to the Labour Law amendments as a missed opportunity.

Most telling is what is not on the 114th session agenda. While Chapter 19 gets attention with these amendments, Chapter 27 (Environment and Climate Change) -- widely recognized as the most complex and costly accession chapter -- received nothing. The National Waste Management Plan remains unadopted. The National Energy and Climate Plan submitted in draft in December 2024 still awaits formal adoption. The government's legislative pipeline does not match the scale of the Chapter 27 challenge, and every session without environmental legislation increases the risk of Chapter 27 becoming the bottleneck for Montenegro's entire accession timeline.

Overall: a solid reform that addresses real problems and advances EU accession, but one that adopts minimum standards, lacks enforcement teeth, may actually harm mothers through the parental leave restructuring, and leaves the hardest implementation work undefined. The government deserves credit for legislative action, but citizens should watch closely whether this becomes operational reality or remains law on paper.
critic_report.headline: Good Law on Paper, but Montenegro Adopts Only the EU Floor While Ducking the Hard Parts
eu_chapter_relevance: Ch.19 Social Policy and Employment -- Direct transposition target. The amendments address closing benchmarks by aligning with EU Directive 2019/1158 (work-life balance) and Directive 2023/970 (pay transparency). However, potentially incomplete transposition if burden of proof reversal and judicial remedy provisions are missing.

Ch.23 Judiciary and Fundamental Rights -- The anti-discrimination and anti-retaliation provisions strengthen fundamental rights protections. However, effectiveness depends on enforcement capacity (36 labour inspectors for the entire country) and judicial access, both chronic weaknesses flagged by the European Commission. The constitutional amendment withdrawal at the same session raises separate Chapter 23 concerns.

Ch.24 Justice, Freedom and Security -- Indirectly relevant through labour exploitation prevention. Wage transparency reduces vulnerability to exploitative employment practices, relevant to anti-trafficking benchmarks under this chapter.

Ch.28 Consumer and Health Protection -- The reduction of effective maternal leave from 12 to 10 months, given near-zero father uptake of non-transferable leave, risks negative maternal and infant health outcomes including reduced breastfeeding duration, contradicting WHO recommendations and EU public health standards.

Ch.2 Freedom of Movement for Workers -- Improved worker protection standards and wage transparency align Montenegro's labour market framework with EU standards, facilitating future labour mobility and mutual recognition of workers' rights.
Ch.8 Competition Policy -- Wage transparency reduces information asymmetry and monopsony power in labour markets, contributing to more competitive and efficient market functioning.

Ch.27 Environment and Climate Change -- Not directly relevant, but the absence of any environmental legislation at the 114th session highlights a legislative prioritization gap. Chapter 27 remains the most technically complex open chapter and risks becoming the bottleneck for accession if the government continues to deprioritize it.
